parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly make single-proposal trades in a 3-round, 3-turn-per-round schedule. All players value GREEN chips at $0.50 each (common-value), while RED chips have heterogeneous private values (dog $0.30, buffalo $0.10, cat $0.40). That creates a common pattern: proposers tend to want to convert their lower-value REDs into higher-value GREENs (or sometimes convert GREENs into multiple REDs if the exchange ratio is favorable). Recipients evaluate offers based only on their own private red value and current holdings, so they will accept only when the immediate chip-value change is nonnegative. Because acceptance decisions are simultaneous and a proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposers face uncertainty: making an attractive offer may yield multiple acceptances and a random partner, while making an offer that benefits only the proposer risks unanimous decline. Inventory constraints matter: some trades that are attractive in theory are infeasible if a counterparty lacks enough chips to give. Behavioral priors shape likely choices: dog (exploratory, medium bookkeeping) tends to experiment with exchange ratios; buffalo (high bookkeeping, cautious) prefers structured, predictably profitable exchanges; cat (conciliatory, moderately patient) tends to propose deals likely to be accepted or to extract modest gains. Over the three rounds we expect most successful proposals to be those that (a) improve the proposer’s chip value and (b) offer enough compensation (multiple REDs per GREEN) so at least one counterparty gains value by accepting. When an offer benefits multiple recipients, a random selection determines the partner, which adds strategic risk for proposers.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: players test simple, small-ratio swaps. Proposers attempt to convert RED → GREEN when profitable for themselves, but simple 1-for-1 conversions are unlikely to be accepted because recipients lose value. Successful deals require offering enough REDs per GREEN to make the recipient whole or better.

After initial conversions, players adapt to observed acceptance patterns and inventories. Buffalo and dog both continue to engineer multi-chip ratios that are profitable for the proposer while leaving at least one counterparty better off. Cat continues to propose ratios that balance its own gain with acceptability.

Late-stage bargaining: inventories matter strongly. Some players have few REDs left and must decide whether to propose offers that are likely to be accepted (but harmful) or to propose terms that will be declined. Risk of multiple acceptances remains relevant, so proposers sometimes prefer offers that will produce a single, predictable acceptor given holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player repeated bargaining with symmetric starting inventories (10 red, 10 green each). Greens are common-value (0.50) for everyone; reds are heterogeneous (badger 0.80, otter 0.30, goose 0.30). That creates consistent opposing incentives: badger strongly prefers accumulating reds, while otter and goose prefer greens. Proposers will tend to request greens and offer reds (since that improves their own value) while badger will often be willing to accept trades that convert greens into reds. Acceptance decisions are private and based on each recipient's own chip values and current holdings: badger will accept many 1-for-1 green-for-red proposals (gain ≈ +0.30), whereas otter and goose only accept when the exchange benefits them (typically accepting red->green, not green->red). Because multiple non-senders can simultaneously accept, some proposals that are attractive to both otter and goose risk random assignment of the recipient; proposers who value control less (or cannot target a partner) must tolerate that risk. Uncertainty arises from two sources: (1) each player only knows their own chip values and must infer others' preferences from public history, and (2) when multiple players accept, the proposer cannot choose which acceptor completes the trade. Given the personas (otter: patient and numerate, low aggressiveness; badger: conciliatory but red-preferring; goose: disciplined and anchor-sensitive) the likely path is repeated small 1-for-1 exchanges: otter/goose repeatedly propose to buy 1 GREEN for 1 RED (gaining for themselves), and badger frequently accepts those proposals because converting green into red is profitable for him. Badger also proposes symmetric 1-for-1 RED-for-GREEN trades to acquire even more reds; those proposals are often accepted by otter and goose because they gain green, but when both accept the recipient is randomly chosen.

rounds.round_explanation: Early signaling and testing: proposers try small 1-for-1 exchanges to probe who will accept. Otter (who values green more) offers red for green; badger (who values red more) accepts such favorable conversions. Badger later tests demand for reds by offering green for red; both otter and goose find that favorable and may accept, producing some random recipient outcomes.

Patterns repeat: otter and goose continue to propose 1-for-1 red->green trades (small positive gains for them) and badger continues to accept to accumulate reds. Badger also makes symmetric offers to buy reds, and when both non-senders accept those offers the recipient is chosen at random.

By the final round inventories show accumulated red concentration at badger and extra greens at otter/goose. Players continue low-risk, small-unit trades (1-for-1) consistent with earlier rounds. Badger maintains opportunistic acceptance of green-for-red offers; when badger offers red-for-green, both otter and goose often accept and the recipient is sometimes chosen randomly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start symmetric inventories (10 red, 10 green) but differ in private red-chip values (peacock high 0.80, otter medium 0.50, badger low 0.20). All value green at 0.50. The core dynamic: peacock aggressively accumulates red because it is privately more valuable to her; badger is the natural seller of red (prefers green) and therefore often accepts offers that give green in exchange for red. Otter is value-neutral between colors and acts as a cautious, cooperative trader who accepts near-neutral swaps but is moderately selective. Because proposals are public and responses are private and simultaneous, proposers structure offers to attract the player who profits most from the exchange (often badger when buying red, often peacock when buying green). Trades are small, typically one-chip swaps, and most proposals that create a clear private gain for at least one non-sender are accepted. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private red values: each player infers incentives from past trades, leading peacock to repeatedly buy red, badger to repeatedly sell red for green, and otter to make neutral, facilitative swaps that sometimes get accepted by the most-profit responder. Over three rounds this produces a steady flow of red toward peacock and green toward badger, with otter remaining near parity.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers test simple 1-for-1 swaps. Otter tends to offer fair, neutral swaps; peacock seeks red by offering green; badger seeks green by offering red. Because peacock privately values red highly, she accepts offers that give her red in exchange for green; badger accepts offers that give him green in exchange for red. These immediate private gains drive most first-round acceptances.

Patterns solidify: peacock repeatedly proposes to buy red (selling green) because badger is inclined to sell red. Badger likewise proposes to buy green (selling red), and peacock frequently accepts those offers because she benefits by receiving red. Otter continues to make neutral swaps that are attractive to whichever counterparty has the biggest private gain.

Endgame consolidation: peacock accumulates more red across final proposals; badger accumulates more green. Otter remains a neutral counterparty and occasional aggregator of parity swaps. By round end, repeated 1-for-1 exchanges that create clear private gains continue to be accepted, and the distribution of red and green shifts toward peacock and badger respectively.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-turn-per-round bargaining game centers on a common high-value GREEN chip (0.50 for all) and a lower-value RED chip that is asymmetrically valued (0.10 for cat and camel, 0.30 for buffalo). Proposers can offer one color in exchange for a different color; because greens are universally more valuable, beneficial proposer trades typically involve offering low-value REDs to acquire GREENs. Cat and camel both value RED cheaply and thus are natural sellers of RED to accumulate GREEN. Buffalo values RED more than the other two players but still less than GREEN, so buffalo will accept some trades that convert their GREEN into multiple REDs only when the arithmetic benefits them (e.g., receiving two REDs for one GREEN yields a small net gain for buffalo). Because players observe holdings but only know their own private valuations, proposers craft deals that look plausible from public holdings while anticipating which counterparties can profitably accept. Personality priors steer behavior: cat is moderately cautious and low-search (prefers safe, clear gains and will often wait), camel is conciliatory and willing to make acceptable offers, and buffalo is deal-ready with a relatively low acceptance threshold — making buffalo a likely recurring trade partner when deals are individually profitable. Uncertainty remains about who will accept when multiple players would benefit; such multi-accept situations are resolved randomly, which encourages proposers to frame trades that appeal to at least one counterparty rather than rely on picking a preferred recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: all players start with equal inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN). Cat and camel can profit by offering RED to acquire GREEN; initial proposers will test small, clearly profitable swaps (e.g., 1 GREEN for 1 or 2 REDs). Buffalo will accept only if the exchange increases her own value (e.g., receiving 2 REDs for 1 GREEN is mildly profitable for buffalo). Expect cat's first test offer to be declined, camel's subsequent offer to succeed with buffalo, and buffalo to then propose a deal that both low-RED-value players find attractive.

With updated holdings after round 0 (camel has fewer REDs and more GREEN; buffalo has more REDs and fewer GREEN), similar incentives persist. Cat remains likely to test a small RED-for-GREEN swap and decline if no counterparty benefits. Camel, having demonstrated a successful trade pattern, repeats a profitable RED->GREEN offer that buffalo finds beneficial. Buffalo again leverages its willingness to trade GREEN for multiple REDs, and when both non-senders would gain by exchanging REDs for a GREEN, multiple acceptances are possible and the recipient is randomly selected.

Final round: inventories are more polarized (buffalo accumulating RED, camel holding more GREEN). Cat remains cautious but continues to propose simple one-for-one RED->GREEN swaps that fail if they are unattractive to others. Camel again uses the reliable pattern of selling a couple REDs for a GREEN; buffalo keeps converting marginal GREEN into multiple REDs when profitable. When both non-senders can gain, multiple acceptances occur and the recipient is resolved randomly, producing some variability in who benefits from the last-turn offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game centers on a common-value green chip (GREEN = $0.50 to everyone) and a heterogeneous red chip whose private value is higher for dog (RED = $0.90) and peacock (RED = $0.80) and low for owl (RED = $0.50). Incentives: dog and peacock both prefer acquiring reds (they value RED > GREEN) while owl is indifferent between a red and a green (both $0.50) and therefore is the natural liquidity provider of red chips. Proposers who want RED will typically offer GREEN; such proposals are beneficial for dog/peacock and neutral or slightly beneficial for owl (so owl is the likely acceptor). Conversely, when owl proposes to sell RED for GREEN (i.e., offer RED, request GREEN), both dog and peacock can gain by accepting; that creates coordination risk (multiple accepts lead to a random realized recipient). Uncertainty arises from (1) private knowledge of only one’s own red value during play (each responder must infer others’ preferences from past trades), (2) simultaneous, private accept/decline responses (leading to random selection when multiple accept), and (3) modest heterogeneity in acceptance thresholds across personas (dog: numerate and moderately patient; peacock: conciliatory but less patient and somewhat guarded; owl: quick-deal oriented and likely to accept neutral/small gains). Given these incentives and personas, the most probable path is a sequence of 1-for-1 RED-for-GREEN trades: dog and peacock repeatedly offer GREEN to acquire RED from owl, and owl alternates proposing RED-for-GREEN, which both dog and peacock accept, with realized recipients determined by random selection when both accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Early game: proposers target the low-valuing owl as the seller of RED. Dog opens by offering 1 GREEN for 1 RED (a clear gain for dog, neutral for owl), so owl is the likely sole acceptor. Peacock follows with the same 1-for-1 request for RED; again owl is the likely acceptor. Owl, preferring quick deals and indifferent between colors, responds by offering 1 RED for 1 GREEN; both dog and peacock benefit from accepting, so both are likely to accept and a random selection determines who receives the red.

Mid game: roles repeat. Dog again requests 1 RED for 1 GREEN; peacock does likewise. Owl, holding accumulated GREEN and fewer RED, continues to offload RED for GREEN. Given private values, dog and peacock typically decline proposals that would cost them RED and give them GREEN, so they do not accept each other’s RED-requests; owl remains the marginal seller/buyer who accepts neutral or small-loss trades. When owl offers RED-for-GREEN, both dog and peacock again stand to gain and both accept, so the realized recipient is determined randomly.

Late game: patterns persist as all players continue to exploit the relative valuations. Dog and peacock keep proposing 1 GREEN for 1 RED because those deals increase their own value; owl continues to accept neutral or small-benefit swaps and occasionally initiates RED-for-GREEN offers that both others accept. The main uncertainty remains which accepting partner is randomly selected when both non-senders accept an owl-originated deal.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player exchange centers on an asymmetric private value for RED and a common value for GREEN. All players value GREEN at $0.50 each; RED is valued privately (ram $0.30, alligator $0.40, butterfly $1.00). That asymmetry makes bilateral offers that trade low-value RED from ram/alligator for GREEN attractive to butterfly (she gains much), while the reverse (butterfly offering GREEN to buy RED) is unattractive to ram/alligator. Given personas, ram (low acceptance threshold, exploratory) and alligator (disciplined, numerate) will repeatedly propose swaps that convert their lower-valued RED into higher-valued GREEN; butterfly (high RED value, patient) will tend to accept such offers when they give her RED in exchange for GREEN, but her own proposals to acquire RED by giving GREEN will usually be declined. Uncertainty remains about tie-breaking if multiple players would accept; here forecasts assume only butterfly finds the terms profitable and accepts, so trades execute cleanly on those turns.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test straightforward 1-for-1 swaps. Ram and Alligator can improve their own holdings by offering RED for GREEN (they give a lower-valued chip to get a higher-valued one), and Butterfly benefits by giving GREEN to obtain RED. Butterfly's own attempts to buy RED by selling GREEN are unlikely to be accepted because other players value GREEN more than RED.

By round 1 patterns reinforce: ram and alligator continue proposing the same advantageous 1-for-1 RED→GREEN swaps, and butterfly keeps accepting (she values RED strongly). Butterfly retains the initiative to try buying RED by selling GREEN, but other players decline because that would reduce their own value.

Final round: with the same incentives and limited rounds remaining, ram and alligator push the same exchange that improves their own payoff; butterfly continues to accept those offers but cannot find partners willing to sell much RED for GREEN. No new trade pattern emerges, and butterfly's attempts to buy RED remain unattractive to the others.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade two chip types with heterogeneous private values: GREEN is common-value at $0.50 each; RED is valued differently (dolphin $0.40, ram $0.20, iguana $0.80). Proposers can request one color and offer the other, and non-senders accept if the exchange increases their own total value. This environment creates repeated Pareto-improving bilateral gains when low-value holders of a color (ram or dolphin) offer their low-valued RED in exchange for GREEN (which all value at $0.50) and iguana, who values RED highly, accepts. Conversely, when iguana proposes to buy RED by offering GREEN, both dolphin and ram also typically benefit (they swap RED for GREEN), so they are likely to accept, creating competition among recipients. Strategic considerations: proposers who sell RED for GREEN tend to attract iguana as the sole acceptor; iguana proposals to buy RED by selling GREEN often attract both non-senders, producing random recipient selection. Inventory constraints and declining GREEN holdings for iguana introduce mild uncertainty about how many more mutually beneficial swaps remain. Player personas (dolphin patient/conciliatory, ram low-search/conciliatory, iguana bookkeeping-disciplined and exploitation-cautious) make small, single-chip offers likely, with repeated 1-for-1 and occasional multi-acceptance outcomes resolved by random selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage rebalancing: both dolphin and ram can profit by selling low-valued RED to acquire GREEN; iguana prefers RED and is the natural acceptor. Iguana also has incentive to propose obtaining additional RED by offering GREEN, which benefits both other players and therefore typically elicits multiple accepts.

Mid-game: pattern repeats. Proposers selling RED for GREEN continue to attract iguana until their GREEN inventory declines. Iguana's proposals to buy RED by selling GREEN remain attractive to both non-senders, producing competing accepts and random partner selection. Players remain conservative in quantities (single-chip exchanges) due to stock sensitivity and modest gains per chip.

Late-stage: iguana's GREEN stock is getting low but still sufficient for more beneficial swaps. Dolphin and ram continue to sell marginal REDs for GREENs when profitable; iguana continues to buy RED by selling GREEN and faces multiple acceptors. Random selection resolves competing accepts and determines final partner allocations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with symmetric inventories (10 red, 10 green) but asymmetric private values: all value green at $0.50 each, while red is low-value for iguana ( $0.10 ), medium for owl ( $0.30 ), and high for whale ( $0.80 ). This creates a clear pattern of mutually beneficial exchange opportunities: players who value red less (owl, iguana) can trade red to the high-valuing whale in return for green, and the whale can sometimes structure multi-unit offers (give a small number of greens to receive multiple reds) that are value-improving for both sides. Key incentives: each proposer wants to improve their own total value by swapping away lower-value chips for higher-value ones; potential recipients accept only if the trade increases their own value given their private prices. Uncertainty enters because acceptors only know their own values and the public history (not others' private valuations), and when multiple acceptances occur the actual recipient is chosen at random. Given the players' personas (generally low aggression, good bookkeeping, and moderate patience), we forecast a cooperative pattern: owl and iguana repeatedly offer 1 red for 1 green (an attractive deal for the whale), and the whale alternates by offering compact multi-unit trades (buy 2 red for 1 green) that are attractive to the low-red-valuing iguana. Over three rounds this yields several accepted 1-for-1 red->green trades (owl/iguana selling red to whale) and repeated 2-for-1 deals initiated by the whale that concentrate reds with whale while leaving owl and iguana better off than their starting positions.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers test simple, low-friction trades. Owl and Iguana likely propose straightforward 1 red -> 1 green swaps because each trader (owl/iguana) increases value by +$0.20 or +$0.40 respectively while the whale gains +$0.30 receiving a red instead of giving a green. Seeing those accepted, whale uses a compact multi-unit proposal (buy 2 red, sell 1 green) that is profitable for whale and -- crucially -- also profitable for iguana (who values red very little) but unattractive to owl. This yields two 1:1 trades accepted by whale and a 2-for-1 accepted by iguana.

Mid-game dynamics: holdings have shifted toward whale owning more reds and fewer greens. Owl and Iguana continue with low-friction 1-for-1 offers (selling reds for greens) since these remain value-improving for both the sellers and whale. Whale repeats the effective multi-unit buy (2 red for 1 green) targeting iguana whose marginal loss from extra red is smallest. The pattern sustains the concentration of reds in whale's hands while owl and iguana steadily replace low-value reds with higher-value greens.

Endgame: inventories become more lopsided (whale accumulating reds). The same exchange logic persists: owl and iguana make small red->green offers that the whale accepts; whale again uses a targeted multi-unit buy (2 red for 1 green) that iguana accepts because it remains value-improving for them. By the end of the three rounds whale holds many of the red chips it values highly, while owl and iguana have shifted inventory toward greens they value more.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start symmetric inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but differ in private RED values (duck 0.20, gorilla 0.20, kangaroo 0.60) while GREEN is common-value (0.50). This creates a clear trade incentive: players with low RED value (duck and gorilla) prefer to sell RED for GREEN (gain +0.30 per 1-for-1 trade), while the high-RED-valuing kangaroo prefers to acquire RED even at a small premium (she is willing to give GREEN to get RED because RED is worth more to her). Because proposals ask for one color and offer another, the natural equilibrium pattern is repeated small 1-for-1 exchanges where duck and gorilla offer RED and kangaroo frequently accepts (or where kangaroo proposes to buy RED offering GREEN and duck/gorilla accept). Uncertainty arises from (1) private knowledge of values (each player only knows their own RED valuation), (2) the risk of multiple acceptances (recipient is chosen at random), and (3) inventory constraints as GREEN stocks for kangaroo fall over rounds. Personas bias behavior modestly: duck is more trusting/low-threshold (likely to accept profitable offers readily), gorilla is conciliatory and patient (likely to propose modest, fair terms), and kangaroo is moderately patient and anchor-light but strategically converts GREEN into RED when profitable. Given these incentives, the forecasted game is dominated by repeated 1 RED <-> 1 GREEN trades that move RED toward kangaroo and GREEN toward duck/gorilla, with most proposals accepted by at least one counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: inventories symmetric but private values make RED attractive to kangaroo and GREEN attractive to duck/gorilla. Proposers will use small, easy-to-evaluate 1-for-1 terms. Duck and gorilla will offer RED for GREEN aiming to trade with kangaroo; kangaroo will opportunistically propose buying RED by offering GREEN and may receive competing accepts.

Mid-game: inventories begin to shift (kangaroo accumulates RED, others accumulate GREEN). Proposers repeat the successful 1-for-1 pattern. Gorilla continues to make modest, fair offers; duck remains willing to transact. Kangaroo still buys RED using GREEN until her GREEN supply becomes scarcer.

Endgame: kangaroo's GREEN stock is now low but each marginal GREEN can still buy RED because donors with low RED value benefit. Duck and gorilla continue to sell RED for GREEN when proposing; kangaroo continues to buy RED while GREEN remains, leading to substantial RED concentration with kangaroo and GREEN concentration with duck/gorilla.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED at $0.80 and GREEN at $0.50, so RED is strictly more valuable for every player. Any two-player exchange that swaps one color for the other necessarily benefits one party and hurts the other (given identical per-chip valuations). Therefore, proposals that ask for RED in exchange for GREEN benefit the proposer but will be rejected by self-interested recipients unless the proposer overpays (offers extra GREEN). Conversely, proposals that offer RED to get GREEN can be accepted because recipients gain value, but such proposals cost the proposer. Given player personas: Dolphin is the most conciliatory and closure-seeking (more likely to propose generous offers), Duck and Swan are more state-disciplined and less exploratory (duck low search, swan low search). Expect many declined, simple one-for-one proposals early, with occasional accepted trades when a proposer overpays slightly (e.g., offers 2 GREEN for 1 RED) to entice recipients. Acceptance decisions are purely self-interested: recipients accept only when the trade raises their own total chip value. Uncertainty arises from (a) whether multiple non-senders will accept (in which case a random accepting recipient is chosen) and (b) how willing proposers are to make mildly loss-making offers to secure a trade or shape future positions.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: proposers test simple swaps. Duck (low-search) opens with a straightforward 1-for-1 request that harms recipients and is declined. Dolphin (conciliatory) then offers a slight overpayment (gives 1 RED to receive 2 GREEN) that benefits the recipient; one non-sender accepts and the trade executes. Swan responds conservatively with a simple 1-for-1 that is declined.

Players adjust after the first accepted trade. Duck responds by making a slightly generous offer (2 GREEN for 1 RED) that is attractive to holders of RED; both non-senders find it beneficial and accept, with the realized recipient chosen at random. Dolphin then makes a modest, self-beneficial single-chip swap that recipients reject. Swan follows with a mildly generous request (3 GREEN for 2 RED) that benefits the recipient and is accepted by one counterparty.

Endgame: duck, now heavier in RED, again posts a mildly generous offer (2 GREEN for 1 RED) that both potential recipients could accept; multiple acceptors appear and a random accepting recipient is realized. Dolphin tries a self-favoring 1-for-1 swap but is rejected (recipients won't accept a deal that loses them value). Swan closes with another small overpayment (2 GREEN for 1 RED) that benefits a recipient and is accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game pits a common-value GREEN chip (0.50 for everyone) against a player-specific RED chip (pig=0.30, bear=0.60, hamster=1.00). Proposers trade one color for another; recipients accept if the deal raises their private payoff. Because RED value varies across players, offers that exchange one RED for one GREEN will be attractive to players who value RED more than GREEN (bear, hamster) and unattractive to those who value RED less (pig). Proposers who value GREEN more than RED (pig) will repeatedly try to swap RED for GREEN; proposers who value RED more (bear, hamster) will try to swap GREEN for RED. Uncertainty arises from (a) private RED valuations (proposers do not know exact recipient valuations), and (b) random partner assignment when multiple recipients accept. Player personas bias behavior: pig is search-oriented and numerate but moderately reference-sensitive (willing to propose modest 1-for-1 swaps); bear is conciliatory and low-aggressive (prefers small, likely-accepted trades); hamster is guarded but aggressive about securing high-value REDs. As an analyst using the given private values, the most likely pattern is repeated 1-for-1 swaps: pig proposes RED→GREEN, which both bear and hamster find profitable and often accept (random recipient); bear proposes GREEN→RED, which pig finds profitable and accepts while hamster typically declines; hamster proposes GREEN→RED, which pig accepts while bear typically declines. This leads to several sequential 1-for-1 exchanges, with pig steadily converting RED into GREEN and bear/hamster increasing RED holdings when they are the recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are balanced (10R/10G each). Pig (prefers GREEN) will offer RED for GREEN; both bear and hamster value RED more than GREEN and so accept, producing a randomized recipient (here realized as hamster). Bear then requests RED in exchange for GREEN; pig benefits and accepts uniquely. Hamster then requests RED in exchange for GREEN; pig again uniquely accepts. Expect three executed 1-for-1 swaps that shift RED toward bear/hamster and GREEN toward pig.

Holdings shifted: pig has more GREEN and fewer RED; bear and hamster accumulated some RED. Pig continues converting RED→GREEN with modest 1-for-1 offers. Bear continues requesting RED in exchange for GREEN; pig (who benefits from receiving GREEN) accepts those requests, while hamster (who values RED highly) declines. Hamster continues to try to acquire RED by offering GREEN; pig again is the accepting partner. The likely pattern is three more 1-for-1 executed swaps.

By the final round pig holds substantially more GREEN and fewer RED. Pig still prefers marginal GREEN and will propose 1-for-1 swaps; both bear and hamster often find RED attractive and will accept such offers, risking random selection. Bear and hamster continue to try to acquire RED by offering GREEN; pig remains the marginal acceptor when doing so. Expect three final 1-for-1 swaps that further move RED to bear/hamster and GREEN to pig.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on exchanges between two chip types: GREEN (common value $0.50 to all) and RED (private values differ). Whale values RED highly (0.80) and prefers acquiring RED by offering GREEN; Swan (RED=0.10) and Turtle (RED=0.20) prefer GREEN and are willing to trade RED for GREEN. That creates repeated, bilateral gains from simple 1-for-1 trades: proposers who ask for RED and offer GREEN have offers attractive to both Swan and Turtle, and proposers who ask for GREEN offering RED are attractive to Whale. Strategic uncertainty arises because acceptance is simultaneous and if multiple players accept, the recipient is randomly selected; proposers cannot target a specific partner. Given player personas (Whale: low search, moderate guardedness; Swan: mixed patience; Turtle: methodical and moderately exploratory) we forecast straightforward, inventory-constrained 1-for-1 trades each turn. Whale repeatedly proposes to buy RED for GREEN; Swan and Turtle alternate proposing to buy GREEN for RED, and most proposals are accepted by the player who benefits most (often Whale accepts buys of GREEN, and Swan/Turtle accept buys of RED). The main uncertainty is which of the two potential sellers will be randomly selected when both accept a Whale offer; acceptance thresholds and limited green inventory for Whale slightly reduce the number of profitable trades he can run before green becomes scarce.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are symmetric (10/10 each). Early trades are high-gain and simple: Whale will open by buying RED offering GREEN (mutually beneficial); Swan and Turtle will respond in later turns by requesting GREEN offering RED. Because both Swan and Turtle benefit from giving up RED for GREEN, Whale's first offer is likely to draw multiple acceptances and be randomly assigned to one recipient. Subsequent proposer turns exploit the same cross-player complementarities until inventories shift.

Holdings shift in favor of Whale accumulating RED and depleting GREEN. Players continue to propose the same mutually beneficial 1-for-1 trades. Whale's buys of RED for GREEN still attract both sellers, but randomness may pick the recipient; Swan's and Turtle's buy-GREEN offers typically find Whale as the sole acceptor because Whale values RED highly.

By the final round Whale's GREEN stock is low but still sufficient for additional 1-for-1 trades. Proposals follow the established pattern. Whale's buy-RED-for-GREEN offers continue to be attractive to both Swan and Turtle, producing another random assignment when both accept; Swan and Turtle's buy-GREEN-for-RED offers typically find Whale as the accepting partner. Limited GREEN inventory for Whale is the main constraint on additional trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game is driven by private valuations: all players value GREEN at $0.50, while RED is worth $0.60 to frog, $0.20 to hedgehog, and $0.50 to badger. That creates clear bilateral trading incentives: frog prefers to acquire RED (it is more valuable than GREEN), hedgehog prefers to acquire GREEN (RED is cheap to them), and badger is broadly indifferent between colors but will accept trades that yield positive gain. Because each proposer must both request one color and offer a different color, mutually beneficial exchanges are typically those where a player who values a color less sells it to a player who values it more. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private values and from inventory constraints that evolve after each accepted trade. Based on the provided personas, we forecast that frog will repeatedly seek RED by offering GREEN (small, patient, low-search proposals), hedgehog will often be willing to sell RED to obtain GREEN and sometimes sweeten offers to attract badger, and badger will accept only when the exchange yields them a clear gain (or occasionally decline neutral swaps). We expect most successful trades to be 1-for-1 exchanges initially (hedgehog selling RED for GREEN to frog) and occasional 2-for-1 offers from hedgehog to entice badger (since badger's indifference means they accept when they receive more RED for a GREEN). The game therefore unfolds as a sequence of small, inventory-constrained conversions of RED into GREEN by hedgehog and repeated RED accumulation by frog, with badger opportunistically taking profitable terms. Strategy is myopic (each player maximizes their own chip-value given current holdings and private values) and acceptance decisions are based only on private valuations and the immediate payoff to the responder.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial positions are symmetric (10 RED, 10 GREEN each). Frog, who values RED most, is likely to open by offering GREEN to acquire RED. Hedgehog values GREEN more than RED and is inclined to accept simple 1-for-1 swaps from frog. Hedghog's own proposal is likely cautious and may be declined; badger—indifferent—will accept only clearly beneficial offers, so a badger-initiated offer that asks for GREEN while offering RED may be accepted by hedgehog.

After round 0 hedgehog and badger have adjusted inventories; frog continues to prefer acquiring RED and will make another small 1-for-1 offer. Hedgehog, seeing hedgehog/badger holdings, is likely to try a slightly more attractive offer (selling 2 RED for 1 GREEN) to entice badger, since badger accepts profitable gains but declines neutral swaps. Badger may then turn around and target hedgehog for a simple 1-for-1 that hedgehog finds beneficial.

With inventories further shifted (hedgehog richer in GREEN, frog richer in RED), frog makes one more conservative 1-for-1 request to acquire RED. Hedgehog again can profitably convert some remaining RED into GREEN by offering 2-for-1 to badger, who takes the positive payoff. Finally, badger finds a small 1-for-1 swap attractive if frog stands to gain (frog values RED more), so badger purchases GREEN from frog by offering RED, which frog accepts for a small net gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but different private valuations: GREEN is universally valuable (0.50) while RED is worth 0.10 (tiger), 0.30 (mouse), or 0.20 (unicorn). Every proposer benefits from trading away the chip color they value less (RED) to obtain the color they value more (GREEN). Recipients decide only with their private values and current holdings, so a recipient accepts when the quantity of RED offered times their private RED value exceeds the GREEN they must surrender (0.50 per GREEN). That implies simple quantity thresholds: mouse (RED=0.30) typically requires 2 RED >= 0.60 to accept 1 GREEN; unicorn (RED=0.20) typically requires 3 RED >= 0.60; tiger (RED=0.10) would need 5 RED to justify giving up 1 GREEN. Given these thresholds and the proposer ordering, we forecast that proposers who value RED least (tiger, unicorn) will repeatedly offer modest multi-RED packages (commonly 2 RED → 1 GREEN) that are attractive to mouse but not to unicorn or tiger as recipients. Mouse, being bookkeeping-disciplined and sensitive to gains, will accept deals that give them net positive value; mouse also tends to avoid making proposals that would reduce their own value. There is uncertainty about exact quantities offered each turn and risk of multiple acceptances (which would trigger a random recipient selection) — proposers often choose terms that target mouse alone to avoid random allocation. Over three rounds the dynamics lead to mouse accumulating RED while tiger and unicorn trade away low-value RED to obtain extra GREENS, producing repeated accepted 2-for-1 trades targeted at mouse and several declined one-for-one attempts.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round signaling and testing. Tiger, who most prefers GREEN over RED, opens with a small targeted premium (2 RED for 1 GREEN) that is sufficient to tempt mouse but not unicorn. Mouse, now richer in RED, tests a symmetric proposal that is unattractive to recipients and is declined. Unicorn then mirrors tiger's successful structure (2 RED → 1 GREEN), again targeting mouse and gaining GREEN at small personal net gain.

With public history showing that 2 RED → 1 GREEN is attractive to mouse, tiger repeats that profitable structure. Mouse continues to prefer deals that improve their own value and declines proposals that would not benefit them (they avoid selling too many RED for GREEN since that would hurt them). Unicorn again opts for the same two-for-one pattern, securing another GREEN while passing on riskier or loss-making ratios.

By the final round mouse has accumulated a large RED inventory and is willing to accept repeated 2-for-1 offers (they remain net beneficiaries). Tiger and unicorn continue exploiting their low RED valuations to convert RED into additional GREENs at terms that are profitable for them and attractive to mouse. Mouse again refrains from making costly proposals and instead accepts favorable offers from others.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with 10 red and 10 green chips each. Green chips have a common value of $0.50 for everyone; red chips have heterogeneous private values (parrot $0.10, fox $0.70, bear $0.50). Because values differ, mutually beneficial bilateral trades are available: players who value red highly (fox) will be willing to trade away greens to obtain reds; players who value red low (parrot) will be willing to trade reds for greens. Bear is intermediate/indifferent and serves as a flexible counterparty. Proposers can exploit other players' comparative valuations: parrot repeatedly offers low-value red to obtain green (gains to parrot are large because red is cheap to them and green is relatively valuable), while fox repeatedly asks for red in exchange for green (gains to fox because red > green for them). Bear structures trades to convert small amounts when it can capture surplus (often by asking for reds and offering fewer greens), but because bear's private values are equal for the two colors, many of bear's own proposals are close to neutral and primarily succeed when they appeal to a player with much lower red value (parrot). Uncertainty arises from simultaneous acceptance by non-senders (possible random recipient if multiple accept) and from players not knowing others' private valuations; nevertheless public range information and repeated play let players infer likely partners and target straightforward 1-for-1 exchanges or small skewed bundles that are attractive to at least one counterparty. Overall, we forecast a sequence of small, repeated 1-for-1 exchanges (and occasional 2-for-1 offers targeted at parrot) that transfer reds to fox and bear and greens to parrot, increasing each player's private payoff.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple 1-for-1 swaps and one slightly skewed bundle. Parrot, who values red very little, will start by offering red for green; fox likes red and will accept 1-for-1 offers quickly; bear will propose a 2-for-1 (asking for 2 reds and giving 1 green) targeted to parrot who has many low-value reds early in the game.

After initial exchanges, parrot still holds many greens and relatively fewer reds, while fox accumulates reds. Players continue with simple 1-for-1 proposals: parrot keeps buying greens with single reds (appealing to fox), fox keeps buying reds with single greens (appealing to parrot), and bear again targets parrot with a 2-for-1 red purchase that parrot finds attractive.

Endgame: parrot's red inventory is low but they still benefit from single-red-for-green swaps, so parrot continues accepting or proposing 1-for-1 trades until running low on reds. Fox continues to accumulate reds via 1-for-1 trades. Bear proposes a simple 1-for-1 that is neutral for them but attractive to fox, allowing bear to adjust holdings slightly before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. All three value green at $0.50; red is worth more to everyone (monkey $0.80, buffalo $0.70, goose $0.70). Proposers can only request one color while offering a different color, so the natural direction for self-interested gains is for a proposer to offer greens to acquire reds. However, because every responder also values red more than green, simple 1-for-1 exchanges (1 green for 1 red) benefit the proposer but hurt any responder who would give up a red. That creates a structural impediment to mutually beneficial trades: rates that improve the proposer’s payoff typically reduce every recipient’s payoff, and rates that make a recipient better off would often be costly enough that the proposer would not offer them. Given the players’ personas (all are state-disciplined and numerate; monkey and buffalo are relatively conciliatory/search-oriented, while goose is more guarded), we expect proposers to test fair-looking, simple offers (e.g., 1 RED for 1 GREEN) but also to avoid making large-value concessions. Responders, driven mainly by their private chip values, will decline offers that reduce their own expected total value. Uncertainty enters through private valuations (each player only knows their own values) and the random tie-breaking rule if multiple responders accept. Overall, the prior implies few if any executed trades: proposers will frequently make small, self-favoring proposals and responders will usually decline, leaving holdings unchanged across the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players probe the market. Monkey (high bookkeeping, low anchor sensitivity) is likely to open with a simple, self-favoring ask (request red, offer green) that is attractive to the proposer but unattractive to others. Buffalo and goose will similarly test straightforward proposals. Because all players value red more than green, most proposals that benefit the proposer will be declined by responders.

With no trades executed in round 0, players retain full inventories. Proposers may try the same simple rates again or slightly adjust quantities. Buffalo's search orientation suggests she might vary terms, but given private values that still favor red, responders are likely to decline unless a proposer makes a concession large enough to make a responder strictly better off—something proposers are unlikely to do repeatedly.

By the final round players have observed repeated rejections. Strategic patience and bookkeeping discipline (all players) suggest they will avoid large concessions; instead they may repeat simple, modest proposals hoping for a low-threshold responder. However, because private valuations still make giving up red costly for responders, the most likely outcome remains no trades executed.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players trade two chip types over three rounds. Green chips have a common value (0.50) for everyone; red chips have heterogeneous private values (dog 0.30, cat 0.70, rabbit 0.50). Proposers tend to offer chips they personally value less and request chips they value more; recipients accept only when the trade raises their own private total. Because responses are simultaneous and private, proposers favor simple 1-for-1 or near-1-for-1 terms that create a clear individual gain for at least one other player. Dog (who prefers green) will frequently propose selling red for green; cat (who prefers red) will do the opposite; rabbit (value-neutral) makes small, low-risk proposals. Personas shape behavior: cat is conciliatory and accepts modest gains quickly, dog is exploratory but accepts individually beneficial offers, and rabbit is patient and often proposes neutral or small beneficial swaps. Uncertainty about others' private red values makes some mutually beneficial trades small in size (1-for-1) rather than aggressive multi-chip deals.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments (10R/10G each). Dog dislikes red and will attempt to trade red for green but must offer terms attractive enough to induce acceptance; cat prefers red and will try to buy red with green; rabbit, value-neutral, tests small swaps. Early offers that are one-sided (too favorable to proposer) are likely declined until a modest mutually beneficial ratio appears.

After round 0, dog and cat hold somewhat skewed inventories (dog accumulated some green, cat more red). Proposers shift to modest 1-for-1 swaps that are individually profitable for at least one other player. Cat's conciliatory style and dog’s moderate acceptance of small gains make 1-for-1 trades likely to clear frequently; rabbit continues making low-friction proposals that primarily benefit cat or dog.

Final round: inventories are more asymmetric (dog holding many greens, cat many reds). Proposers again favor 1-for-1 swaps that produce clear immediate gains for some counterpart. Cat continues to accept modest beneficial offers quickly; dog often accepts small gains to consolidate value. Rabbit's final offer aims to pick off a counterparty who benefits from swapping a red for a green.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but DIFFERENT private valuations: all value GREEN at $0.50; RAM values RED at $0.50 (indifferent between colors), while SQUID and OTTER value RED at $0.40 (they strictly prefer GREEN). This creates a clear demand for GREEN from SQUID and OTTER and a weak or neutral demand for RED from RAM. Strategic dynamics: (1) Trades that exchange RED for GREEN (proposer gives RED and receives GREEN) are profitable for SQUID/OTTER but costly for whoever must give GREEN; conversely, trades that exchange GREEN for RED benefit the recipient who receives GREEN and give RED — that is, recipients who take GREEN gain, while proposers who receive GREEN gain when offering RED. (2) Because private values are known only to each player, proposers must anticipate accept/decline decisions based on likely gains for recipients; many mutually profitable trades require at least one player who is indifferent (RAM) to be the counterparty. (3) Inventory constraints matter: early successful trades rely on RAM having GREEN to give; as RAM sells GREEN to get RED, later rounds see fewer feasible, mutually beneficial trades and more declined proposals. (4) The three-player acceptance rule (simultaneous responses; random resolution if multiple accept) creates two risks for proposers: attracting multiple acceptors (random partner) or repelling everyone (decline). Given the players' personas (generally conciliatory, patient, and low-aggressiveness), proposals tend to be simple 1:1 or small multi-unit swaps; acceptances follow straightforward payoff comparisons (accept if the received chips' value exceeds given chips' value to the responder). The forecast below uses these priors and the exact private valuations as analyst-side information to predict proposals, likely accept/decline responses, and inventory updates over three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial symmetry and inventories make 1:1 exchanges easy to propose. SQUID and OTTER prefer GREEN, while RAM is indifferent. Early proposers can often secure a partner because RAM is willing to exchange GREEN for RED (neutral) and SQUID/OTTER will accept exchanges that increase their value. Expect multiple accepted 1:1 swaps early, with RAM acting as the frequent counterparty because his indifference makes him the least costly giver of GREEN.

Holdings have shifted: RAM has accumulated more RED and given away GREEN, while SQUID and OTTER increased their GREEN. RAM remains the relatively inexpensive source of GREEN (neutral for him), so mid-game proposals continue to route through him. SQUID and OTTER keep proposing small profitable RED->GREEN swaps, and RAM often accepts neutrally or cooperatively. Proposers avoid deals that would lower their own value; when only loss-making deals are available (because potential counterparties would lose), proposals are less likely to succeed.

RAM has largely depleted his GREEN stock by now, while SQUID and OTTER hold substantially more GREEN. That creates a bottleneck: SQUID and OTTER still want GREEN but the cheapest counterparty (RAM) has few GREEN left to give. Proposals that ask for GREEN increasingly face declines because the only players who can give GREEN without losing value are scarce. Expect some declined offers late in the game as the feasible, mutually beneficial matches dry up.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on trade between two chip colors: GREEN (common value $0.50) and RED (player-specific: camel $1.00, butterfly $0.60, alligator $0.30). Incentives: players who value RED highly (camel > butterfly) want to acquire RED and are willing to give up GREEN; the alligator values RED least and is the natural frequent seller of RED to obtain GREEN. Proposals are one-color-for-another exchanges; acceptance depends on the private change in the responder's own point value (value received minus value given). Uncertainty enters because responders see only public holdings and trades but not others' private values, and in cases where multiple players accept, the actual recipient is chosen at random. Given player personas, camel tends to make modest, quick, prosocial offers to accumulate RED; butterfly makes anchor-sensitive, modest proposals seeking slight gains; alligator frequently accepts favorable exchanges that trade RED for GREEN. Expect many 1-for-1 or slightly lopsided exchanges, with alligator supplying RED to both partners throughout the game, and occasional random tie-resolution when both non-senders accept the same proposal.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple 1-for-1 and small multi-chip offers. Camel, who most values RED, will try to buy RED by offering GREEN; butterfly tests a slightly more generous request (asking for two GREENs in exchange for one RED) to improve her GREEN holdings; alligator, who dislikes RED, is inclined to sell RED for GREEN and may accept several offers. Early trades will shift holdings so the alligator becomes a recurrent RED seller.

Middle round: holdings have shifted (camel and butterfly gained some RED; alligator holds fewer RED and more GREEN). Camel continues to make small, reliable requests for RED by offering GREEN—other players with low RED value (alligator) keep accepting. Butterfly favors modest, fair-seeming 1-for-1 swaps to incrementally improve her preferred chip mix. Alligator prefers to exchange RED for GREEN and will accept several such proposals; when both non-senders would benefit, tie-breaking randomness determines the recipient.

Final round: players make small, low-friction trades to fine-tune personal holdings. Camel still pursues RED via 1-for-1 offers; butterfly continues modest 1-for-1 swaps to edge toward a slightly RED-weighted portfolio. Alligator, low on RED and high on GREEN, remains willing to sell RED for GREEN; when both non-senders gain, randomness resolves who receives the deal. Expect final holdings with camel holding the most RED, alligator holding many GREEN, and butterfly in the middle.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with equal inventories (10 red, 10 green) but different valuations: owl values green (0.50) more than red (0.30), while dolphin and iguana value red (0.60) more than green (0.50). This creates frequent, bilateral gains from simple 1-for-1 exchanges of red for green: a player who is relatively red-poor and green-rich (owl) benefits by selling red to get green, and players who value red more (dolphin, iguana) benefit by selling green to get red. Because proposals must state a request (buy) and an offer (sell) of different colors, 1 red for 1 green proposals are mutually beneficial for both sides and are the most likely trades to appear. Behavioral priors (owl: quick-deal, low search; dolphin: patient, conciliatory; iguana: search-oriented, prosocial) make repeated, straightforward 1:1 trades likely across rounds. Uncertainty arises because when both non-senders accept a proposal, the actual partner is chosen at random; proposers cannot pick which accepter executes the trade. That creates a small risk for proposers who might prefer trading with one partner over another, but the symmetric valuation structure keeps that risk limited. Over the three rounds, we expect many simple 1-for-1 accepted trades, shifting reds toward the high-red-valuers (dolphin and iguana) and greens toward owl, while holdings and marginal willingness-to-trade gradually constrain later proposals.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in play all players still hold many chips. Owl (prefers green) is likely to propose selling red for green; dolphin and iguana (prefer red) are likely to propose the mirror. 1-for-1 exchanges are mutually beneficial, so most offers are accepted. Because two players commonly accept the same offer, random selection determines the actual partner on those turns.

Mid-game inventories diverge: owl accumulates more greens, dolphin and iguana accumulate more reds. Proposers continue to favor simple 1-for-1 red↔green swaps because they reliably yield private gains. Given their priors, players continue to accept straightforward mutually beneficial exchanges; multiple acceptances and random partner selection remain common.

Late-game: holdings are more polarized (owl richer in green, others richer in red). Straight 1-for-1 trades are still individually profitable and therefore commonly accepted. Proposers may be somewhat cautious if inventories run low, but given quick-deal tendencies (owl) and conciliatory tendencies (dolphin, iguana), simple exchanges continue and finish the three-round sequence.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories (10 red, 10 green) but different private valuations: whale and swan value red at 0.80 and green at 0.50, while zebra values red at 0.10 and green at 0.50. That creates strong bilateral gains from exchanging red for green: zebra benefits from trading away red to obtain green (gain per 1-for-1 ≈ +0.40), while whale and swan benefit from receiving red in exchange for green (gain per 1-for-1 ≈ +0.30). Strategic incentives therefore push zebra to propose selling red for green and push whale and swan to propose buying red offering green. Acceptance decisions are private and based only on each player's valuation and current inventory; acceptors will take any offer that increases their own total value. Uncertainty arises because when multiple players accept a proposal, the proposer cannot choose which accepter is realized (random selection), and each player sees only public history (not others' valuations). Given the personas, proposals are likely to be modest (small, incremental trades), zebra will repeatedly try to convert red into green, and whale and swan will take opportunities to purchase cheap red. Over three rounds (each with three ordered proposers), we expect several small mutually beneficial swaps, mostly 1-for-1 or occasional 2-for-2 swaps, with decline only when a proposed recipient would suffer (e.g., a high-red-valuing player asked to give red in exchange for green).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple, safe trades. Whale (conciliatory, patient) initiates by offering a small green-for-red swap to increase red holdings; zebra (low red value) finds selling red for green attractive and accepts. Zebra then aggressively (search-oriented) offers red-for-green at a slightly larger size; swan (high red value) accepts. Swan closes the round with a small buy-red offer that zebra accepts. Trades are small and mutually beneficial, shifting red toward whale/swan and green toward zebra.

Mid game: patterns from round 0 repeat and intensify. Whale continues to propose small green-for-red swaps that zebra finds attractive. Zebra, with many greens, again offers small bundles of red-for-green; both whale and swan can accept such offers, so random assignment determines who transacts when both accept. Swan continues opportunistic buying of red. Inventories move further toward the configuration where zebra accumulates green and whale/swan accumulate red.

Final round: with inventories shifted, zebra has many greens and few reds and pushes final conversions; whale still finds small red purchases attractive and zebra trades down its last reds. By the last turn, options to buy red are limited (few red holders willing to give red for green), so an attempted final buy may be declined if no recipient can both afford the requested chip and expect a value gain from the swap.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for RED chips (turtle: $1.00, pig: $0.90, elephant: $0.60) while GREEN chips are common-value ($0.50). All players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Because every player values RED more than GREEN, proposers will tend to request RED and offer GREEN. Proposers want to turn GREEN into higher-value REDs; potential recipients evaluate offers by comparing the value they lose (REDs given up) to value they gain (GREENs received). Small exchange ratios (e.g., 1 RED for 2 GREENs) are attractive to lower-valuing-RED players (elephant and pig) but are marginal for turtle (who values RED most). Player personas shape behavior: turtle is patient, numerate, and selective (so he makes reasonably efficient offers and can reject marginal deals); elephant is conciliatory and has a low acceptance threshold (likely to accept modest gains); pig is moderately exploratory and bookkeeping-disciplined, so will accept small positive gains but be cautious on marginal or zero-net offers. There is uncertainty for each player about others' private RED values and about whether multiple players will accept (if multiple accept, a random chooser determines the recipient), so proposers sometimes slightly oversupply GREEN to attract at least one acceptor. Over three rounds we expect repeated offers that convert GREEN into RED at roughly 2 GREEN per 1 RED, producing several accepted trades primarily with elephant and pig as recipients and turtle accumulating REDs across the game.

rounds.round_explanation: Starting holdings: all players 10 RED / 10 GREEN. Turtle (first mover) will propose a modestly generous rate to attract at least one acceptor (1 RED for 2 GREEN). Elephant (low acceptance threshold) and pig (small net gain) both find that exchange acceptable, so a recipient is realized. Subsequent proposers (elephant and pig) face slightly altered holdings; they will repeat offers close to 1 RED for 2 GREEN to secure a sure positive payoff to at least one counterpart. Turtle's high RED valuation makes him less likely to accept similar offers later in the round.

With updated holdings (after round 0 turtle has more RED and fewer GREEN; pig and elephant adjusted), proposers again prefer to trade 2 GREEN for 1 RED to produce clear positive gains for lower-RED-value players. Turtle continues to push to accumulate RED but remains selective; elephant continues to make conciliatory offers and accept modest gains; pig accepts small positive exchanges but declines zero-net deals. Repeated 1-for-2 offers attract at least one acceptor each turn.

By round 2 holdings reflect several conversions of GREEN into RED (turtle especially has accumulated RED). Proposers see that 2-for-1 GREEN-to-RED exchanges continue to be the simplest way to secure acceptances from lower-RED-value players. Turtle still demands favorable trades and typically obtains REDs; elephant remains a frequent acceptor; pig accepts when the net gain is positive. The randomness when multiple players accept continues to introduce partner uncertainty, so proposers typically keep offers at a level that will attract at least one acceptor rather than trying for extreme terms.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player game is shaped by a clear payoff asymmetry: green chips are a common high-value good (all players value GREEN at $0.50), while RED chips have heterogeneous private values (kangaroo low $0.10, gorilla medium $0.40, fox high $0.70). As a result, kangaroo and gorilla prefer to trade away RED for GREEN, while fox prefers to trade GREEN for RED. Proposers cannot pick which accepting partner is realized if multiple players accept, so they tend to make simple, inventory-feasible proposals that are attractive to at least one counterparty. Given the players' personas (kangaroo and gorilla are conciliatory, lower acceptance thresholds; fox is moderately guarded but responds to profitable deals), the most likely path is repeated 1-for-1 exchanges: kangaroo and gorilla propose to buy 1 GREEN selling 1 RED, and fox proposes to buy 1 RED selling 1 GREEN. Non-senders accept whenever the trade increases their own private value. Uncertainty comes from (a) private valuations known only to each player, (b) the random selection when multiple accept, and (c) how proposers balance offering terms that are acceptable to at least one counterparty without overpaying.

rounds.round_explanation: Start-of-game incentives: all players begin with abundant inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN). Kangaroo (prefers GREEN) will offer RED for GREEN; fox (prefers RED) will offer GREEN for RED. Early offers are small (1-for-1) because these are clearly beneficial to at least one counterparty and are easy to track. Kangaroo and gorilla are likely to accept favorable, simple swaps; fox accepts profitable trades too but may be slightly more selective.

By round 1 holdings have shifted modestly (proposers have slightly fewer of the colors they sell). Players continue to favor low-friction 1-for-1 trades because they reliably increase one side's value and are easy to accept. Fox's buy-1-RED-for-1-GREEN offers remain attractive to both others, so multiple acceptances are possible and the realized partner alternates probabilistically; kangaroo and gorilla remain willing to sell RED for GREEN when it's personally profitable.

Near the final round inventories are more asymmetric (fox accumulates RED, others accumulate GREEN). Players continue small, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 trades. Fox still finds buying RED attractive and sells off low-value GREEN; kangaroo and gorilla keep selling the RED they value less for GREEN. Acceptance patterns remain stable: fox accepts others' buy-GREEN-for-RED offers; both non-senders accept fox's buy-RED-for-GREEN offer, creating occasional multiple-accept outcomes resolved by random selection.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game pits one player (unicorn-pink-1559) who strongly prefers RED chips, one player (tiger-yellow-3019) who strongly prefers GREEN chips, and one relatively indifferent player (bat-purple-4553) who values red and green equally. Publicly, GREEN chips are common-value ($0.50) and RED has private values (tiger: $0.10, unicorn: $0.80, bat: $0.50). Each proposer must request one color and offer a different color; acceptors privately decide based on their own values and current inventories. Key dynamics: proposers will try to design exchanges that raise their own expected chip-value (often offering the color they value less to acquire what they value more). Recipients will accept only if the incoming exchange increases (or at least does not reduce) their own total value, given inventory constraints. Uncertainty enters because each player does not know the others' private RED valuations and there is a random tie-break if multiple accept; this encourages modest, inventory-aware offers early on and selective acceptance by cautious players. Personas push tiger toward cooperative but anchor-sensitive offers favoring greens, unicorn toward disciplined, numerically calibrated offers to accumulate reds, and bat toward patient, low-aggression responses—accepting only when personally profitable or neutral. Over three rounds we expect mutually beneficial swaps to happen where a high-red-value player trades greens for reds (unicorn buys red by selling green) and a high-green-value player trades reds for greens (tiger buys green by selling red), with bat acting as a bridge when exchanges give them positive value or are neutral.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: everyone starts with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Proposers will test modest, inventory-safe rates. Tiger (prefers GREEN) will attempt to swap some RED for GREEN; unicorn (prefers RED) will try to buy RED by offering GREEN in favorable ratios; bat will propose only trades that increase its own value or are nearly neutral. Early trades are diagnostic: they reveal who holds which chips and which offers are acceptable.

Mid game: inventories begin to diverge (tiger accumulates GREEN, unicorn accumulates RED). Proposers who hold their less-valued color will exploit it to obtain their preferred color. Offers become more targeted and numerically calibrated: unicorn and tiger craft swaps that are small but clearly value-increasing for themselves, while bat looks for marginal gains. Acceptance patterns reflect individual private values: unicorn accepts deals that increase RED holdings; tiger accepts deals that increase GREEN holdings.

Final round: inventories are polarized (one player holds many REDs, another many GREENs). With no future rounds to exploit, proposers make small, last-chance trades. Some proposals that would hurt the proposer are offered but often declined; acceptances occur when the recipient strictly gains. Random tie-breaking is relevant only if multiple players accept an offer, but most final offers are accepted by a single, clearly benefiting party.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player negotiation centers on asymmetric private valuations for RED chips (GREEN = $0.50 for everyone; RED = dog $0.50, buffalo $0.90, cat $0.40). That creates a clear pattern: buffalo has strong demand for RED (willing to pay in GREEN), cat has an incentive to supply RED (prefers GREEN), and dog is roughly indifferent between the two colors and can act as a facilitator or tester of trades. Proposers must pick one color to request and another to offer; non-senders accept privately if the immediate implied exchange increases their own total chip value given their private valuations. Uncertainty enters via (a) each player knowing only their own values, (b) others' acceptance thresholds and behavioral priors (search, patience, guardedness), and (c) the random selection rule when multiple players accept a single proposal. Inventory constraints (no selling more than you hold) and the immediate update of holdings after accepted trades make each accepted trade shape subsequent proposals: buffalo repeatedly structures offers to obtain RED from players who value RED less than GREEN (mainly cat), cat often sells RED for GREEN because that raises his payoff, and dog tends to make small, even exchanges (1-for-1) that are neutral for himself but attractive to at least one partner. Across rounds we therefore expect multiple small 1-for-1 RED<->GREEN exchanges, with many accepted trades between proposers seeking RED (buffalo) and cat as a frequent counterparty, and occasional dog-initiated 1-for-1 proposals that buffalo will accept when profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers will test simple 1-for-1 exchanges. Dog (neutral values) likely starts with a balanced 1 RED for 1 GREEN offer that is attractive to buffalo (who values RED highly) but not to cat. Buffalo will then propose to acquire REDs (offering GREEN) and target cat as the supplier. Cat will respond by offering a deal that trades his cheaper REDs for GREENs; he may offer slightly larger quantities to ensure acceptance or to extract certainty.

With initial patterns established, proposers refine target partners. Dog again makes a small 1-for-1 request to obtain RED from a player who values RED less (cat). Buffalo maintains a steady strategy of offering GREEN to buy RED and continues to recruit cat as a supplier. Cat uses modest 1-for-1 trades to accumulate GREEN and reduce his RED inventory; when he proposes, he asks for GREEN in exchange for RED and buffalo (who highly values RED) often accepts.

Final round: players consolidate gains. Dog continues to use small 1-for-1 trades to obtain RED from the lower-valuing supplier (cat). Buffalo persistently proposes 1-for-1 GREEN->RED trades to acquire additional REDs; cat—now holding many GREENs—responds favorably to selling RED. Cat's final proposal follows the same pattern, exchanging a RED for a GREEN to further rebalance toward GREEN.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players share the same chip valuations (GREEN = $0.50, RED = $0.40), so everyone prefers GREEN to RED. That symmetry means any bilateral trade that moves GREEN from one player to another necessarily transfers value from the recipient to the proposer (no trade creates joint surplus). Proposers therefore have an incentive to request GREEN while offering RED (they gain +$0.10 per 1 RED->1 GREEN exchange), but those offers make recipients worse off by $0.10 each, so recipients rationally tend to decline. Behavioral priors (badger and otter somewhat conciliatory; goose more disciplined and anchor-sensitive) create modest heterogeneity in acceptance thresholds, but with common valuations the dominant equilibrium tendency is few accepted trades. Because chip values are public/common-value for GREEN and identical privately for RED here, uncertainty about counterpart valuations is low — the main uncertainty is about other players' acceptance thresholds and about random selection when multiple accept. Given inventory constraints and three rounds, proposers are likely to test simple, small exchanges (e.g., 1 RED for 1 GREEN). Those offers are attractive to proposers but unattractive to recipients, so most offers are expected to be declined; occasional acceptance is possible only if a recipient is unusually conciliatory or mis-evaluates payoffs.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test straightforward, profit-seeking swaps (buy 1 GREEN offering 1 RED). Because that improves the proposer's value by $0.10 and worsens the recipient by $0.10, recipients are expected to decline. Badger (first mover) may be slightly exploratory and make a simple proposal; others respond conservatively.

Proposers repeat the pattern. Given the first-round rejections, proposers face the choice to either increase offers (which would be costly to themselves) or persist with the same small, self-beneficial terms. Expect continued 1 RED -> 1 GREEN proposals and continued declines; conciliatory players might be more willing to accept bad deals, but payoffs remain clearly unfavorable for recipients.

Final round: with two prior rejections, proposers have reduced time to experiment. They still prefer terms that increase their own value, so expect another sequence of buy-GREEN/sell-RED 1:1 proposals. Recipients' incentives remain to decline; with no structural reason for acceptance and identical private values, the round is also likely to produce no executed trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player chip bargaining with private asymmetric values for RED and a common value for GREEN creates predictable trade pressure: peacock highly values RED (1.00) and will seek REDs, badger values RED very little (0.20) and prefers GREEN (0.50), and otter is intermediate (RED=0.40, GREEN=0.50). Proposers can exploit these differences: players who undervalue RED (badger) can cheaply supply RED to those who overvalue it (peacock) in exchange for GREEN, and players who slightly prefer GREEN (badger, otter) will pay with RED if the exchange is net-positive for them. Because proposals are public but each player only knows their own values, there is uncertainty about whether any given counterparty will accept. Personas suggest peacock is conciliatory and willing to take deals that increase her value, badger prefers closure and is willing to make straightforward exchanges that benefit him, and otter is moderately cautious and will accept only modestly beneficial trades. Early rounds therefore favor mutually profitable swaps between peacock and badger (peacock pays GREEN for RED; badger pays RED for GREEN) with occasional involvement by otter when terms slightly tip in her favor. As holdings shift (peacock running low on GREEN, badger accumulating GREEN, otter holding moderate stocks), proposals become more constrained and some exploratory offers are declined because no counterparty can accept without losing value.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: everyone starts balanced (10R/10G). Badger wants GREEN more than RED and will offer cheap RED to obtain GREEN. Peacock highly values RED and will accept favorable deals that give her RED in exchange for GREEN. Otter is cautious but will accept small positive gains. Expect an early badger->peacock exchange, then peacock to press for more RED, and otter to try a small trade.

Holdings after round 0 create asymmetries: badger has accumulated GREEN, peacock has many RED but few GREEN, otter is intermediate. Badger will continue to trade RED for GREEN (still profitable for him), peacock can extract additional favorable exchanges but has little GREEN to offer, and otter will make cautious proposals that are acceptably beneficial for potential recipients.

Final round: badger is low on RED but high on GREEN, peacock is rich in RED but has almost no GREEN, and otter holds a mix. Badger can use remaining RED to extract more GREEN if he offers enough to make the recipient better off; peacock has bargaining power but little GREEN to give. Otter will accept only narrowly positive trades. Expect a targeted late trade that uses remaining RED to secure GREEN from otter, then several exploratory offers that are declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with ten red and ten green chips. Red chips are privately heterogeneous in value (buffalo 0.90, camel 0.80, cat 0.70); green chips are common-value at 0.50. Every proposed exchange moves chips between two players and must respect integer chip bundles. Because red is relatively more valuable than green for all players, single-chip swaps (1 red for 1 or 2 green) rarely produce mutually profitable integer trades. The most viable mutually beneficial trades are multi-unit bundles that exploit valuation differences (e.g., moving red from a lower-valued holder to a higher-valued holder using a bundle such as 2 red <-> 3 green or 3 red <-> 5 green). Players act to maximize their own total chip value and know only their own private red valuations; they use local, inventory-constrained logic and modest exploration. Given personas: buffalo is numerate and deal-ready (willing to propose profitable bundles for herself), camel is conciliatory but low-search (likely to propose straightforward profitable bundles), and cat is moderately guarded but will accept small positive gains when they appear. Early rounds therefore favor structured bundle trades that move reds from cat (lowest red value) to buffalo/camel using 2-for-3 style bundles. Later, after cat runs low on red, remaining proposals become harder to make mutually beneficial and are likely declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players begin with 10 red and 10 green. Proposers will look for small integer bundles that create positive payoff for themselves and at least one counterparty. The natural feasible bundle is buying 2 red for 3 green (2R <-> 3G): buyer gains more red-value than greens spent and seller (cat) can gain slightly because their red value is lowest. Expect buffalo and camel to target cat as seller; cat will both sell and occasionally propose a reciprocal sale that multiple players accept.

After the first round, cat has sold red inventory and accumulated greens; buffalo and camel hold extra red but have fewer greens. There remain still-feasible 2R <-> 3G bundles where cat (as the marginal low-red-value holder) can sell additional reds for a small positive gain while buyers (buffalo or camel) gain more total value. Expect another pair of 2R-for-3G trades with cat as seller. Once cat's red stock approaches zero, cat's proposals to buy red will have difficulty finding sellers.

By the final round cat's red stock is depleted (or very low) and buffalo/camel hold large red balances but limited greens. Integer divisibility constraints make new mutually beneficial bundles rare: small single-red trades cannot satisfy both sides. Proposers are therefore likely to try simple 1-for-1 or 2-for-3 offers but these are typically rejected by the counterparty whose private value would fall. Expect final-round proposals to be exploratory or attempt small gains but be declined.
