parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical inventories but different private red-chip values (peacock 0.40, owl 0.30, dog 0.20) while green chips are common-value (0.50). This creates repeated opportunities for mutually beneficial bilateral trades when a player with relatively low red value offers red in exchange for green: the low-red-value proposer sacrifices relatively cheap red to acquire high-value green, while a high-red-value recipient accepts because the additional reds they receive are worth more to them than the green they give up. Expect the lowest-red player (dog) to propose green-for-red deals that can attract acceptance from both higher-red players; the highest-red player (peacock) will also propose deals favorable to herself (selling green to buy red), and the middle player (owl) will attempt proposals but often face rejections because many terms that benefit the proposer hurt potential recipients. Behavioral priors matter: owl is quick-deal–oriented and may accept modestly favorable offers; peacock is conciliatory but moderately cautious; dog is patient and numerate and will search for profitable exchange rates. Uncertainty about others' exact red values (each player only knows their own) leads proposers to pick integer exchange rates that are plausibly acceptable (e.g., 2 red ↔ 1 green) and to test acceptance. When more than one non-sender would accept a proposal, the recipient is resolved randomly; that randomness creates some risk for proposers who might hope a particular partner is selected. Overall we forecast a few clean, small integer exchanges (typically 2 RED ↔ 1 GREEN style ratios) where the low-red proposer or high-red proposer captures gains when counterparties accept, and many intermediate offers are declined when they would make recipients worse off.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: peacock opens by proposing favorable terms to herself (selling a green to get two reds). Owl (middle red value) is likely to accept some deals that increase his value slightly; dog (lowest red) looks for opportunities to buy green cheaply and will propose such trades later in the round. Owl tends to propose simple 1-for-1 style swaps but these are often declined because recipients lose value.

After initial trades, peacock has accumulated extra red and continues to propose deals that further convert her green into red. Owl again attempts straightforward green-for-red swaps that would benefit him modestly, but recipients frequently decline. Dog, with the lowest red value, can again offer a small red bundle to buy one green; such proposals are attractive to higher-red partners and typically get accepted (sometimes by multiple players, triggering random selection).

Late-round adjustments: peacock continues to convert remaining greens into reds when favorable partner responses are expected. Owl again makes small test offers but gets declined as others won't give up high-value greens cheaply. Dog opportunistically repeats the low-red-driven green purchases; multiple acceptances remain possible and recipients are determined randomly when that happens.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip exchange is driven by asymmetric private values: GREEN is commonly worth $0.50 to all players; RED is worth $0.40 to ram, $0.20 to alligator, and $0.50 to butterfly. That creates predictable trading incentives: players who value GREEN more than RED (ram and alligator) want to acquire GREEN in exchange for RED, while butterfly values RED as much as GREEN and is flexible as a counterparty. Proposers will generally craft exchanges that increase their own total value (X * value_received - Y * value_given > 0) subject to inventory constraints; recipients privately accept only when the incoming terms increase their own value. Uncertainty enters because proposers do not know others' private valuations, so they sometimes make offers attractive enough that multiple recipients accept, and the partner is then chosen at random. Given the provided personas, ram tends to make straightforward, bookkeeping-aware small beneficial trades and often accepts workable offers; alligator is state-disciplined and structures offers to be attractive but can miscalculate partner selection risk; butterfly is moderately patient and often accepts or generates deals that exploit others' stronger preferences. Expect trades that move RED toward butterfly and GREEN toward ram/alligator when mutually beneficial, occasional multi-accept situations, and some declined exploratory offers early on.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple, inventory-feasible swaps. Ram (prefers GREEN over RED) will try modest RED->GREEN exchanges. Alligator (strongly prefers GREEN) makes an aggressive RED-for-GREEN offer to attract counterparties. Butterfly, who values RED as much as GREEN, is a likely accepting partner for deals that transfer RED toward her.

Mid-game: holdings have diverged (butterfly now richer in RED, alligator richer in GREEN). Ram continues to make compact beneficial swaps to pick up GREEN. Alligator posts offers sized to be attractive, sometimes generating multiple acceptances; randomness in partner selection can redirect intended gains. Butterfly offers remain flexible and occasionally trade away scarce GREEN to obtain RED.

Endgame: inventories are lopsided (alligator has many GREEN but few RED; butterfly has many RED). Players propose smaller, low-friction swaps that are clearly value-increasing for at least one counterparty. Because proposers cannot pick recipients, some precise offers lead to multiple acceptances and randomized partner selection; otherwise single-accept trades execute and finalize holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical chip counts but asymmetric private values: all players value GREEN at $0.50, while iguana values RED only $0.10 and the other two value RED $0.50. That asymmetry creates repeated opportunities for iguana to trade low-value RED for high-value GREEN (a clear gain for iguana) while counterparties often face neutral or positive swaps. The bargaining dynamics will therefore be driven by (1) iguana repeatedly proposing to sell RED for GREEN to extract value, (2) ram and dolphin responding pragmatically to immediate gains or neutral swaps, and (3) modest competition over who accepts when multiple players would benefit. Uncertainty arises from (a) simultaneous private accept/decline decisions when more than one non-sender stands to gain, (b) acceptance thresholds that are mixed (some proposals are neutral for recipients), and (c) inventory constraints after each executed trade that change future leverage. Given the player personas, we forecast mostly straightforward, low-aggression proposals where iguana exploits his low RED valuation and ram/dolphin accept neutral-or-positive exchanges rather than hold out for complex multilateral deals.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: iguana uses his initial stock of RED to target GREEN. Early proposals emphasize converting iguana's low-value RED into GREEN. Ram and dolphin are likely to accept clearly beneficial trades; when trades are neutral for a recipient, responses depend on modest acceptance thresholds and low search tendencies, making acceptance likely but not certain.

Middle round: holdings have shifted (iguana holding more GREEN, ram/dolphin accumulating RED). Iguana continues to propose favorable RED→GREEN swaps; ram tends to propose straightforward 1-for-1 exchanges, targeting the counterparty most likely to accept. Dolphin alternates between consolidating RED and occasionally purchasing GREEN when a favorable seller is available.

Final round: inventories create predictable motives — iguana still prefers GREEN, ram exploits opportunities to accumulate RED, and dolphin consolidates holdings. Proposals that hand clear immediate gains to a recipient (e.g., offering multiple RED for a GREEN when the recipient values RED highly) are accepted readily. Neutral 1-for-1 swaps are accepted selectively depending on the counterparty's private valuation and patience.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players share identical beginning inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but have different private valuations: iguana values RED highly (0.80) and GREEN at 0.50; owl values RED moderately (0.60) and GREEN 0.50; whale values RED very low (0.10) and GREEN 0.50. This creates a persistent asymmetric gains-from-trade: players who value RED (iguana and owl) can profit by buying RED from whale in exchange for GREEN, while whale can profit by selling RED for GREEN. Because proposals let the proposer offer some of one color to request some of the other, efficient, repeatedly feasible trades are ones where a proposer gives GREEN to get RED (small, often 1-for-1 swaps). Accept/reject choices are private-value comparisons for each responder: a responder will accept only if the chips they would give up are worth less to them than what they'd receive under their private valuation. Given the personas, iguana and owl are careful about bookkeeping and anchor-sensitive (likely to propose modest, repeatable 1-for-1 trades), while whale is conciliatory and repeatedly willing to sell RED cheaply for GREEN. Uncertainty arises because responders do not reveal valuations and multiple responders can accept; when more than one accepts the recipient is chosen at random. Over three rounds and three proposal turns per round, the most likely pattern is repeated small exchanges where iguana and owl each obtain additional RED by offering GREEN, and whale sells RED for GREEN several times. Whale's privately low RED value makes them the recurring acceptor of (GREEN for RED) proposals; whale will occasionally attempt richer proposals to reverse the flow (selling RED for more GREEN or buying GREEN with multiple RED), but such proposals are unlikely to be accepted by iguana or owl because those players value RED more than GREEN.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Proposers (iguana then owl) are likely to offer modest 1-for-1 GREEN->RED swaps that profit them slightly and are attractive to whale (who values RED much less than GREEN). Whale may try an opportunistic offer selling 2 RED for 1 GREEN, but such offers hurt the other two players and are likely to be declined.

By round 1, iguana and owl have each gained one RED and lost one GREEN; whale has lost RED and gained GREEN. Proposers again use low-friction 1-for-1 GREEN->RED requests. Whale continues to be the marginal seller of RED and accepts those modest offers. Whale-initiated proposals asking others to give up RED are unattractive to iguana and owl and are typically declined.

Late-game dynamics repeat earlier patterns: iguana and owl still gain from acquiring RED at small cost in GREEN, and whale, holding relatively many GREENs, continues to accept those one-for-one swaps. Whale proposals that would require iguana or owl to give up RED remain unattractive and are declined, so trade flow stays directional (GREEN -> RED from whale to the other two).

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but very different private values: kangaroo strongly prefers RED (RED=0.80, GREEN=0.50), gorilla moderately prefers GREEN (RED=0.20, GREEN=0.50), and duck prefers GREEN but values RED very little (RED=0.10, GREEN=0.50). Because every proposer must request one color and offer the other, most mutually attractive trades convert a chip the recipient values relatively less into a chip they value relatively more. Proposers will therefore tend to offer the color they value less and request the color they value more; recipients accept only when the offered quantity of the favored color compensates for the chips they give up. Proposers cannot choose who gets the offer if multiple players accept, so they typically craft terms that create at least one willing counterparty (often kangaroo, who profits heavily from exchanging GREEN for RED). Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own private values; they infer others' preferences from observed trades and from generally plausible incentives. Behavioral priors (duck: low acceptance threshold and low search; gorilla: conciliatory/patient; kangaroo: value-driven, moderately patient) make acceptance when profitable likely, and make proposers target the counterpart whose private values are most consistent with acceptance. Random selection between multiple acceptors creates additional strategic noise.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test exchange rates. Duck (low red value) tries to convert cheap RED into GREEN but must offer enough RED per GREEN to entice someone. Gorilla favors GREEN but will only give up RED if compensated. Kangaroo, who strongly prefers RED, is the most likely recipient of GREEN-for-RED offers; early trades therefore tend to route GREEN from kangaroo to others in exchange for RED, enlarging kangaroo's RED stock.

Midgame: holdings diverge (kangaroo accumulates RED), making repeated GREEN-for-RED proposals attractive to kangaroo and enticing to proposers who want GREEN. Proposers leverage the fact that kangaroo values RED much more than GREEN; gorilla remains conciliatory and will propose conservative, likely-to-be-accepted terms. Duck continues to convert remaining RED into GREEN when profitable.

Endgame: inventories are more asymmetric (kangaroo very heavy in RED, duck lower on RED). Proposals are smaller and targeted: duck still trades a bit of remaining RED for GREEN; gorilla continues proposing modest RED-for-GREEN swaps that kangaroo accepts; kangaroo pushes final GREEN-to-RED conversions while supply of GREEN dwindles. Random selection when multiple acceptors adds allocation noise to final distributions.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposal-per-round game is driven by asymmetric private values for RED (player-specific) and a common value for GREEN (0.50 for all). Duck values RED highest (1.00), Swan values RED moderately (0.70), and Dolphin values RED low (0.20). All players value GREEN at 0.50. Given these preferences, the predictable pattern is that Swan and Duck will try to acquire RED by offering GREEN, and Dolphin will most often act as the willing red-seller (liquidity provider) because he values RED least. Proposers prefer simple, quick trades (Swan is low-search and likes closure; Duck is disciplined and protective of resources but will buy profitable RED), so many proposals will be small, straightforward swaps (e.g., 1 GREEN for 1 RED). Recipients decide privately using their own values: a recipient will accept a proposal that increases their personal chip value. When a proposer requests RED and offers GREEN, Dolphin typically accepts (gain = +0.30 per 1-for-1), while Duck typically rejects 1-for-1 because he prefers RED (would be losing value). When a proposer requests GREEN and offers RED, both Swan and Duck typically accept because they gain value by trading GREEN away for higher-valued RED. Uncertainty arises from (a) whether a higher-value holder (Duck) can be enticed with larger green payments, and (b) the random selection rule when more than one non-sender accepts. Over the three rounds, the likely flow is repeated small swaps where Dolphin progressively sells RED for GREEN to Swan and Duck, increasing his GREEN and decreasing his RED holdings while Swan and Duck accumulate more RED. Strategic aggression is limited; proposals stay near value-improving, low-friction 1:1 swaps given the players' priors and preference asymmetries.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers are likely to make simple 1-for-1 offers. Swan and Duck both prefer to acquire RED and will offer GREEN; Dolphin (low RED value) is the natural acceptor for RED-for-GREEN swaps. When Dolphin is the proposer he will offer RED for GREEN, and both Swan and Duck will accept, producing some random matching risk for Dolphin.

By round 1 the pattern is reinforced: Swan and Duck continue attempting to buy RED with GREEN. Dolphin, having shown willingness to trade RED away for GREEN, remains the primary seller of RED. Duck remains cautious about giving up RED but will buy additional RED when terms are clearly favorable; Swan continues to prefer simple offers. When Dolphin proposes GREEN-for-RED, both others typically accept, producing a random recipient.

Final round: Dolphin's RED inventory has fallen but he still values GREEN relatively more, so he may continue to transact by offering RED for GREEN. Swan and Duck will continue requesting RED with GREEN. Expect continued 1-for-1 swaps; Dolphin remains the primary seller of RED and Swan/Duck the primary accumulators. Random selection when both non-senders accept will determine some final partner matches.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but different private valuations: bear and pig value RED at $0.60 and GREEN at $0.50, while hamster values RED at $0.10 and GREEN at $0.50. This creates a clear trade opportunity: bear/pig want extra REDs (higher-valued to them) and are willing to give GREENS; hamster is happy to sell RED cheaply to acquire GREENS. Proposers will therefore typically request the color they value more and offer the other color. Early rounds are likely to feature simple 1-for-1 proposals from bear and pig that hamster accepts because those moves increase hamster's payoff substantially. When hamster becomes proposer, he tends to offer multiple REDs for a single GREEN (e.g., 2 RED for 1 GREEN) to make the deal attractive to bear/pig while still improving his own position. Because acceptance is simultaneous, when an offer benefits both non-senders more than their current valuation there can be multiple accepts and a random recipient. Over time hamster's RED stock dwindles, reducing future supply of RED and making later buy proposals less likely to be accepted. Players act to maximize their own total chip value (not group surplus), so bear/pig will decline offers that exchange away their high-valued REDs unless the terms are generous.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test straightforward profitable swaps. Bear and pig will try simple 1 RED for 1 GREEN trades (gain +$0.10 each) that hamster finds attractive (+$0.40), so those offers are accepted by hamster. Hamster, having accumulated some GREEN and retaining RED, can then propose to trade away RED (cheap to him) for GREEN at favorable multi-for-one terms to attract bear/pig.

Middle round: hamster's earlier sales reduce his RED stock but he still can make profitable multi-for-one offers that appeal to bear/pig. Bear and pig continue to propose small RED-for-GREEN swaps which hamster accepts while he still has RED. Hamster continues converting RED into GREEN via generous offers (2 RED -> 1 GREEN), sometimes attracting both other players and therefore triggering random selection.

Final round: hamster's RED inventory has fallen to very low levels. Bear can still execute one last small RED-for-GREEN swap that hamster accepts if he has RED left. After hamster runs out of RED, pig's attempts to buy RED with GREEN are unlikely to be accepted (bear won't sell high-valued RED and hamster has none), so some late proposals are declined. Hamster, lacking RED to offer, cannot make further profitable sell-R actions, so his final proposals (if he tries to buy RED by offering GREEN) are typically rejected by the others.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with equal inventories but different private red-chip values: turtle values red very low (0.10) and green 0.50; whale and swan value red high (0.90) and green 0.50. This creates a persistent asymmetry: trades that move red from turtle to either whale or swan in exchange for green increase the acceptor's value (they receive cheap green for valuable red) and also increase the proposer's value when the proposer is the low-valuing red holder (turtle) trading away red. Proposers who are high-red types (whale, swan) tend to offer green to buy red; those low-red types (turtle) tend to offer red to buy green. Because private values are not observed, early turns are exploratory: proposers test exchange rates to find a counterparty willing to take the other side. Acceptance decisions are driven purely by private value comparisons and inventory constraints: recipients accept only if the trade raises their own total chip value and if they have enough of the requested color. Strategic differences matter: whale is quick-deal oriented and proposes offers likely to be accepted; turtle is moderately disciplined and accepts clear gains; swan is mixed and sometimes negotiates aggressively but may also make exploratory proposals that risk being taken by a beneficial counterparty. Over three rounds, we expect repeated, inventory-constrained transfers of red from turtle to the two high-valuing players in exchange for greens, with some declined exploratory offers early on and multiple small accepted trades thereafter.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves test opponents' red/green prices. Turtle, who dislikes red, opens by trying to trade a small amount of his red for extra green at a very favorable rate; both high-red players decline. Whale then posts a green-for-red deal attractive to turtle (who values the red little), and turtle accepts. Swan follows with a larger green-for-red offer that is also attractive to turtle; turtle accepts again. Early dynamics: whale probes for a quick, profitable acquisition; turtle accepts clear gains; swan finds a willing partner in turtle.

After initial transfers, holdings shift (turtle has fewer red and more green; whale and swan hold more red). Turtle continues to monetize remaining red for green in small, safe steps. Whale, favoring quick deals, posts small favorable exchanges that turtle accepts. Swan again targets turtle's remaining red with a profitable green offer and secures another transfer. The pattern is repeated small trades that benefit the accepting high-red players and the selling low-red turtle.

Final-round dynamics are constrained by inventories: turtle has nearly exhausted his red stock and sells his last red in a small, favorable exchange. Whale and swan keep making one-for-one or small-rate offers. Because turtle lacks red late in the game, some offers that would be acceptable to a high-red recipient become impossible for turtle to accept; whale takes a small green from swan in one turn, and in the final move swan — despite mixed incentives and private-value uncertainty — posts an exchange that whale accepts, transferring one more red to whale. Overall, red flows from the low-valuing holder (turtle) to the high-valuing holders (whale, swan), with whale ending up with the largest red holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start symmetric inventories (10 red, 10 green) but have differing private red valuations (hedgehog 0.20, frog 0.70, badger 0.60) while green is common-value (0.50). Hedghog prefers greens to reds and thus is inclined to sell reds for greens; frog and badger prefer reds and will generally offer greens to obtain reds. Because players only observe their own values, proposals and accept/decline decisions reflect each player's private marginal tradeoffs and their personas: hedgehog is relatively conciliatory and low-search (likely to make straightforward, slightly generous offers and to accept small gains), frog is patient and moderately guarded (prefers sure gains and will accept modest profitable trades), and badger is moderately cautious (selective but willing to take small positive trades). Early offers that are one-sided in the proposer’s favor (e.g., proposer asking for many greens while offering few reds) will tend to be declined because potential recipients would lose value. Fair or mildly favorable exchanges (one-for-one or small quantity swaps) will typically be accepted by at least one counterparty who gains, and when both outsiders would benefit, a randomized selection of recipient determines execution. Over the three rounds we expect a pattern of hedgehog proposing to buy greens by selling reds (often declined when terms are too generous to the proposer), while frog and badger alternate selling greens to acquire reds in modest, inventory-constrained swaps. Strategic considerations (acceptance thresholds and patience) make players prefer small guaranteed gains over speculative large gains, producing a sequence of mostly single-acceptor trades that redistribute red chips toward frog and badger and green chips toward hedgehog.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round with symmetric inventories. Hedghog (low value for red) opens with an aggressive green-seeking proposal that others decline. Frog then offers a simple 1-for-1 swap that benefits a red-seeker (badger) and is accepted. Badger follows by proposing a multi-red-for-one-green swap that is attractive to hedgehog (who values green more), so hedgehog accepts. Early trades establish asymmetries in holdings that shape subsequent offers.

After initial reallocations, hedgehog again seeks greens via a fair 1-for-1 swap; both outsiders would gain under their private values, so both accept and a single recipient is randomly chosen (frog). Frog then uses its turn to buy additional reds by offering a green for two reds; hedgehog, who values greens more than reds, accepts. Badger finishes the round with a modest 1-for-1 swap that is attractive to frog and executed.

Endgame round: hedgehog makes another slightly generous green-seeking proposal that both outsiders decline. Frog executes a simple 1-for-1 swap with badger (badger accepts to obtain a red). Badger finishes by proposing a two-red-for-one-green swap that hedgehog accepts, which further shifts reds toward badger and greens toward hedgehog.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with identical inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but distinct private values: GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone; RED is heterogeneous (mouse $0.30, tiger $1.00, unicorn $0.80). This creates a clear trade direction: mouse prefers GREEN over RED and will be motivated to sell RED for GREEN; tiger and unicorn prefer RED and are motivated to sell GREEN for RED. Because proposers cannot choose the accepting partner if multiple players accept, proposers tend to make safe, small, clearly beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges rather than aggressive multi-unit proposals that risk rejection. Uncertainty about others' private valuations means responders evaluate offers based only on their own payoff change if chosen; they may accept any trade that increases their own chip value (accounting for the small chance of being randomly selected when both responders accept). Behavioral priors (mouse = conciliatory, patient, bookkeeping-disciplined; tiger = moderately aggressive, pragmatic but numeracy-limited; unicorn = anchor-sensitive, mixed toughness) predict repeated, modest 1-for-1 red↔green swaps. Inventory constraints and mouse's lower RED value make mouse the frequent seller of RED, while tiger and unicorn compete to acquire RED. Over three rounds we therefore expect a sequence of mostly accepted 1:1 trades, sometimes with both responders accepting and a random recipient being realized.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round establishes the pattern: mouse as a buyer of GREEN (selling RED) and tiger/unicorn as buyers of RED (selling GREEN). Proposers choose modest 1-for-1 terms that are clearly value-increasing for at least one responder. Several offers will attract both responders (both gain if they give up GREEN to get RED or vice versa), producing random recipient selection when that happens.

Players observe the early trades and continue the low-friction 1-for-1 pattern. Mouse remains a frequent seller of RED to accumulate GREEN. Tiger, seeking RED, offers 1-for-1 terms likely to be accepted by mouse but not by unicorn (who already has many RED). Unicorn repeats attempts to buy RED at 1-for-1 when mouse still has RED to sell.

By the final round mouse's RED inventory has declined but the incentive structure is unchanged: mouse continues to trade RED for GREEN when profitable, and tiger/unicorn continue to request RED by offering GREEN. Proposers keep 1-for-1 offers to ensure acceptance; as mouse's RED stock thins, mouse accepts beneficial proposals until RED scarcity constrains further trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories (10 red, 10 green). Greens are common-value (0.50 each) while red is heterogeneous: fox highly values red (0.90) while parrot and bear value red low (0.20). This creates a clear direction of mutually profitable exchange: fox is motivated to acquire additional reds and is willing to part with greens, while parrot and bear can profitably exchange their low-value reds for greens. Because proposals request one color and offer the other, proposers who want greens (parrot, bear) will tend to offer reds and request greens; fox will tend to offer greens and request reds. Private valuations imply that when fox offers greens for reds, both parrot and bear usually gain, producing situations with multiple acceptances and a random realized recipient; proposers face the risk of an undesired partner. The player personas (low search, modest patience) favor early, simple proposals and relatively quick acceptances when offers are clearly value-improving for responders. Uncertainty mainly comes from (a) not knowing others' private red values during play and (b) the random selection if more than one responder accepts. Given those incentives, the forecasted path features repeated small exchanges where fox accumulates reds by offering greens and parrot/bear steadily trade away reds for greens when individually profitable; near the end a constrained player with few greens (fox) may be unable to participate further, and some late proposals by the lower-red-value players can be declined when they are not lucrative to others.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers will make straightforward, low-complexity swaps (1-for-1 or small multiples). Parrot and bear will propose selling low-value red for higher-value green; fox will counter-propose buying red with greens. Responders accept when the trade increases their own value. Multiple acceptances are possible when fox offers greens for reds.

Mid game: inventories have shifted (fox holding more reds, fewer greens). Fox continues to exploit its willingness to trade greens for reds; parrot and bear continue simple 1-for-1 green-seeking proposals. Because fox still values reds highly and usually benefits from giving greens, it frequently accepts 1-for-1 proposals to acquire reds or accepts proposals from others selling red for green. When fox proposes multi-unit purchases (limited by its remaining greens), both other players often benefit and may both accept, producing random recipient outcomes.

Late round: fox has accumulated many reds and is running low on greens; parrot and bear hold more greens. Fox's ability to offer greens is inventory-constrained, so its proposals request multiple reds in exchange for its remaining greens. These proposals still look attractive to suppliers (who trade low-value red for green), so multiple acceptances remain possible. Conversely, when bear tries late green-seeking trades, the pool of potential suppliers can be thin (fox may have no greens left), so such proposals can be declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. GREEN is a common-value chip (0.50 for everyone). RED is private-value: goose values RED high (0.90), buffalo is neutral (0.50), monkey values RED low (0.10). Given these valuations, efficient and individually profitable trades tend to move RED from players who value it less (monkey, sometimes buffalo) to players who value it more (goose). Proposers will try to design offers that exploit those differences while respecting inventory constraints. Because acceptance decisions are private and proposers cannot choose the accepting partner when more than one accept, proposers typically craft offers that are strongly attractive to at least one other player (often the one with the lowest RED value) and not overly generous to avoid self-loss. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own private RED value; proposers infer others' likely responses from prior behavior/personas and from public trade history. Over three rounds, we expect repeated small, inventory-conserving exchanges where: (1) goose repeatedly attempts to buy RED by offering GREEN (since RED is much more valuable to goose), (2) monkey is a frequent seller of RED early on (monkey values RED low) and hence accepts favorable GREEN-for-RED offers, and (3) buffalo is more conciliatory and may accept neutral or mildly favorable exchanges but will avoid trades that reduce their own value. When two players would accept the same proposal, a random selection determines the actual recipient; proposers implicitly trade off acceptance probability and risk of losing the deal to a rival acceptor.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players begin with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Goose (high RED value) will try to acquire RED by offering GREEN; monkey (very low RED value) is the natural seller and likely to accept such offers. Buffalo is neutral and will accept only if the deal is at least non-worsening for them. Early offers test willingness to trade small quantities and reveal private incentives.

After round 0 trades, goose and buffalo have accumulated more RED while monkey has reduced RED and increased GREEN. In round 1 goose continues to buy RED by offering small amounts of GREEN; monkey, still holding some RED, accepts favorable GREEN-for-RED swaps. Buffalo tests a small neutral exchange as a conciliatory move. If a proposer cannot find a profitable partner, they may submit a harmless/no-deal proposal that is declined.

By the final round monkey is largely out of RED and holds more GREEN; goose still values RED highly and continues targeted attempts to buy RED, while buffalo can execute neutral swaps to rebalance holdings. Monkey lacks RED to sell and so often cannot supply RED-based proposals; when monkey must propose an exchange for RED it may be unattractive and is frequently declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade two chip types (RED, GREEN) with private, asymmetric valuations: cat and rabbit value RED above GREEN, dog values GREEN above RED. Each proposer must request one color and offer the other, and non-senders decide simultaneously whether to accept based only on their own valuations and risk/strategy priors. Expect cat and rabbit to repeatedly try to acquire RED by offering GREEN; dog is the natural seller of RED early on (since dog values RED least) and the natural buyer of GREEN when it can offer RED. Because acceptance is determined by recipients' private payoffs, many offers that look favorable to the proposer will be accepted only by players for whom the trade creates nonnegative surplus. Strategic uncertainty arises when multiple recipients would accept (random partner selected) and when proposers run low on the chip they would like to offer. Personality priors (cat: low search, conciliatory; rabbit: patient, disciplined; dog: exploratory and patient) suggest moderate-sized, repeatable 1–2 chip exchanges rather than aggressive large-volume swaps. Over three rounds, expect repeated 1-for-1 or 2-for-2 exchanges where rabbit and cat steadily accumulate REDs by offering GREENs, with dog commonly on the other side, and occasional rounds where multiple acceptances yield random partner selection. Proposers avoid offers they cannot fill; late in the game limited RED inventory for dog can cause declined proposals or role reversal offers (dog requesting RED in exchange for GREEN) that recipients reject.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players start balanced. Cat and rabbit prefer RED and will ask for RED in exchange for GREEN; dog prefers GREEN and will accept reasonable GREEN-for-RED swaps. Early trades are small (1–2 chips) to test acceptance. When both non-senders would benefit, multiple accepts create random partner selection.

Mid game: inventories have shifted (cat and rabbit holding more REDs; dog more GREENs). Cat and rabbit continue proposing modest GREEN-for-RED trades; dog still willing to sell RED for GREEN while it has REDs. When both recipients would gain, trades are randomly assigned; otherwise the single beneficial recipient accepts.

Endgame: dog is running low on RED inventory and may increasingly decline to offer RED or be unable to offer it. Cat and rabbit persist with 1-for-1 exchanges to accumulate REDs. If dog lacks RED to offer, it may attempt reversed proposals (request RED in exchange for GREEN) but those are unattractive to cat and rabbit and are likely declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on repeated 1-for-1 exchanges between red and green chips. Green chips are common-value (all players value them at $0.50), while red chips are privately valued: Otter values red highly ($0.90), Squid moderately highly ($0.80), and Ram values red very low ($0.10). Consequently Otter and Squid have persistent incentives to acquire more red (sell green to get red), while Ram prefers green (would sell red to get green). Proposers will therefore most often request the color they privately value more and offer the other color. Acceptance decisions are driven by the change in the recipient’s own portfolio value: a recipient accepts if the immediate exchange (receive X, give Y) increases their personal value. That leads to a stable pattern: offers that give green in exchange for red are attractive to Ram (he gains value) and unattractive to Otter and Squid, while offers that give red in exchange for green appeal to Otter and Squid but not Ram. Because acceptance is simultaneous and, when multiple players accept, the recipient is chosen at random, proposers face partner uncertainty: targeting the high-value color often yields at least one acceptor but may result in a random match among multiple acceptors. Over rounds, holdings shift (net flows of red toward Otter and Squid and green toward Ram), but the direction of incentives remains stable because private valuations do not change. Given player personas (Otter and Squid patient and disciplined; Ram conciliatory and green-preferring), we forecast repeated small, 1-for-1 trades where proposers systematically ask for their preferred color, and recipients accept whenever the swap raises their own valuation.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are symmetric (10R,10G each). Otter and Squid prefer red, Ram prefers green. Expect proposers who value red to offer green-for-red, and Ram to accept offers that convert his low-value red into higher-value green. Early trades will move red toward Otter and Squid and green toward Ram.

After round 0 red has flowed toward Otter and Squid and green toward Ram. Proposers repeat the same incentive pattern: Otter and Squid request red (offering green) and Ram requests green (offering red). When Ram is the recipient of a green-for-red offer he consistently gains and accepts; when Ram proposes to buy green with red, both Otter and Squid find exchanging a green for a red attractive and may both accept, so the realized recipient can be random among acceptors.

By the final round holdings are skewed (more red with Otter/Squid, more green with Ram), but marginal incentives remain the same because valuations are private and fixed. Expect similar 1-for-1 proposals: Otter and Squid buy red by selling green and Ram buys green by selling red. Ram continues to accept green-for-red offers (he benefits), while Otter rejects offers that would trade away red for green.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with symmetric inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN). GREEN is a common-value chip worth $0.50 to everyone; RED is heterogeneous: camel-pink-767 values RED at $0.80, the other two value RED at $0.20. That creates repeated mutually beneficial trade opportunities where players who value GREEN more (butterfly, alligator) will happily exchange low-valued RED for GREEN, and the high-RED-valuing camel will happily trade GREEN for RED. Given the personas (camel and butterfly relatively conciliatory / quick to close deals; alligator protective but low-search), most proposals will be small, simple 1-for-1 exchanges. Proposers will typically ask for the color they value more and offer the color they value less; non-senders accept if the trade increases their own private payoff. When both non-senders would gain, multiple acceptances create random allocation risk for proposers. Over three rounds we forecast a sequence of mostly 1 RED <-> 1 GREEN trades, with several turns attracting multiple acceptances and others attracting a single accept from the player who gains, and immediate updates to holdings after each accepted trade. Uncertainty arises from (a) symmetric bilateral gains that can lead both non-senders to accept, triggering random selection of the recipient, and (b) declining availability of a particular color in a player’s hand over repeated accepted trades (especially camel’s GREEN stock), which slightly increases cautiousness late in the game.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: mutual gains are obvious. Camel (high RED value) proposes small GREEN-for-RED swaps to acquire more RED. Butterfly and Alligator (both prefer GREEN) will usually accept such offers. Early on both non-senders hold abundant RED and GREEN, so some proposals attract multiple accepts and resolve by random recipient selection; other proposals attract a single accept from the player who benefits most.

Middle round: inventories have shifted (camel has accumulated RED and given away GREEN). Proposals remain small. Camel continues to buy RED with GREEN; butterfly continues to seek GREEN by offering RED. Alligator also continues the same pattern. Because camel's GREEN stock is declining, she remains willing to accept beneficial exchanges (getting extra RED), but may become slightly more selective later.

Final round: inventories are more asymmetric (camel has many RED and few GREEN). Players make last small trades to improve individual payoff. Camel still values RED most and continues to propose small GREEN→RED swaps; butterfly and alligator continue to target GREEN by offering RED. Camel's dwindling GREEN makes her slightly more conscious about further selling, but the positive bilateral gains still justify one-unit swaps, and non-senders accept when the trade raises their private payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game centers on exchanging two chip types: GREEN (common value 0.50 to all players) and RED (player-specific: owl 0.20, iguana 0.70, dolphin 0.60). Each player starts with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Proposers request some chips (buy) and offer other chips (sell); non-senders privately accept or decline, and if multiple accept a recipient is chosen at random. Key incentives: players trade toward what they value more (iguana and dolphin prefer RED > GREEN; owl prefers GREEN > RED). Because trades are one-for-one or small multiples and holdings are limited, proposers balance attractive terms for prospective recipients against extracting surplus. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private RED valuations and from random selection when multiple accept. Given the provided personas, iguana tends to make disciplined, patient, low-aggression offers and accepts low-threshold profitable deals; owl tends toward quick, low-search proposals with medium acceptance thresholds; dolphin is moderately patient and conciliatory, so will accept modest positives. Using these priors and the private values, the forecast predicts many small exchanges where players with low RED value (owl) sell RED for GREEN, and players with high RED value (iguana, dolphin) buy RED by offering GREEN, with acceptance determined by whether the swap increases each responder's own total value.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining. Proposers try simple 1-for-1 or slightly leveraged swaps to test willingness to trade. Owl (who values GREEN more) offers RED to obtain GREEN; iguana leverages its appetite for RED to ask for 2 RED in exchange for 1 GREEN when it believes owl will accept; dolphin targets owl for small profitable RED purchases. Acceptances hinge on individual gains given private RED values.

Middle round: patterns reinforce. Owl continues to acquire GREEN by offering RED (still profitable for low-RED owl to sell). Iguana, with many REDs, pushes for favorable exchange rates (buying RED in multi-unit offers) when confident a low-RED seller will accept. Dolphin continues targeting owl for one-for-one RED buys; acceptances remain determined by each responder's private gain.

Final round: inventories matter. Owl has depleted RED and holds many GREEN; iguana has accumulated RED. Proposers make conservative 1-for-1 offers that still generate positive net payoff for at least one responder. With time running out, players accept modest, clear gains rather than hold out for risky improvements.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but have different private values: whale values RED low (0.30) so prefers GREEN (0.50); zebra values RED modestly higher (0.60) than GREEN (0.50); swan strongly prefers RED (1.00) to GREEN (0.50). Proposers can offer chips they value less to buy chips they value more; recipients accept only if the swap increases their own private value. Because GREEN is common-value at 0.50 and RED values vary across players, most mutually beneficial trades are simple 1-for-1 swaps: players who value RED more than GREEN are willing to trade a GREEN for a RED, and players who value GREEN more than RED are willing to trade a RED for a GREEN. The bargaining dynamics therefore devolve into repeated small, inventory-constrained trades where whale (who favors GREEN) repeatedly offers low-value REDs to acquire GREENs, and zebra and swan accept when the swap benefits them (zebra: small benefit; swan: large benefit). When multiple recipients would gain from the same offer, both may accept and the actual partner is chosen at random, which introduces outcome uncertainty for the proposer. Personas make whale reasonably conciliatory and patient (likely to propose modest, widely acceptable 1-for-1 offers); zebra is search-oriented and disciplined (likely to accept small but clear gains and continue to prefer accumulating REDs); swan is strongly motivated to accumulate REDs and will readily accept favorable swaps. Over three rounds (nine turns) the forecast predicts a sequence of simple 1-for-1 RED-for-GREEN swaps that shift REDs toward zebra and swan and GREENs toward whale, with most zebra proposals accepted by whale and most swan/whale offers attracting both zebra and swan but resolving randomly among them.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test straightforward 1-for-1 swaps. Whale, who values GREEN more than RED, offers single REDs for GREENs to convert low-value REDs into higher-value GREENs. Both zebra and swan would gain from exchanging a GREEN for a RED (zebra modestly, swan substantially), so whale's initial 1-for-1 attract multiple acceptances; random selection determines the recipient. Zebra then proposes 1 GREEN for 1 RED to expand her RED holdings; whale benefits and accepts. Swan ends the round with another 1-for-1 proposal, which whale accepts because it improves whale's portfolio.

Mid game: inventories have begun to polarize (whale accumulating GREEN, zebra and swan accumulating RED). Proposers repeat efficient 1-for-1 swaps. Whale again offers a RED for a GREEN; both zebra and swan still strictly prefer to swap GREEN for RED (they gain), so the offer attracts multiple acceptances and the recipient is selected at random. Zebra continues converting GREEN into RED by offering 1-for-1; whale, who benefits from receiving RED in exchange for GREEN, accepts. Swan again proposes to buy RED with GREEN; whale, who values GREEN more, accepts, further concentrating GREEN with whale and RED with zebra/swan.

Endgame: players continue the same locally optimal pattern. Whale persistently trades low-value REDs to obtain GREENs; zebra keeps selling GREENs to accumulate REDs; swan likewise keeps buying REDs. Most zebra proposals are accepted by whale (who gains by shedding RED for GREEN). Whale's repeated, modest offers often attract both zebra and swan when both would benefit, leaving final partner selection to chance. By the end of the third round inventories are concentrated: whale holds many GREENs, zebra and swan hold more REDs, matching their private valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories (10 RED, 10 GREEN). GREEN has a common value of $0.50 to everyone; RED has heterogeneous values: pig and elephant value RED at $0.40 each, turtle values RED at $0.10. Each player privately knows only their own values and tries to maximize their own total value. That structure creates repeated incentives for players who devalue RED (turtle) to offer RED in exchange for GREEN, and for players who value RED relatively more (pig and elephant) to accept such offers when the exchange is favorable to them. Because proposals require offering one color to buy another, proposers will only make trades that increase their own expected value given their private valuations. Accept/decline decisions are purely selfish: recipients accept only when the proposed exchange increases their own total value. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private valuations and from random resolution when multiple recipients accept. Over three rounds we expect turtle (low RED value) to be the primary seller of RED to acquire extra GREEN, and pig/elephant to accept those offers when the exchange is advantageous. Elephant's lower acceptance threshold and conciliatory tendencies make them more likely to accept moderately profitable offers, while turtle's high bookkeeping and patience make repeated, inventory-aware proposals likely. Pig is moderately exploratory and numerate, willing to accept small gains but sometimes hold out for slightly better terms.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers test favorable swaps. Pig will try to trade a little RED for GREEN (small profitable margins for pig) but both others decline because it would hurt their private payoffs. Turtle, who dislikes RED, is likely to offer RED for GREEN at a favorable ratio and both pig and elephant find the deal profitable; multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient. Elephant may then try a small marginal swap but is unlikely to find takers.

After an initial successful trade that shifts RED toward those who value it and GREEN toward turtle, pig remains interested in gaining GREEN but faces resistance. Turtle repeats a previously profitable pattern (selling RED to buy GREEN) and again finds both others willing to accept; random matching yields a recipient. Elephant continues to propose small, marginally beneficial swaps but faces no takers because most others would lose value by accepting.

Endgame: inventories have shifted (turtle holding more GREEN, pig and elephant holding more RED). Pig continues to seek GREEN but cannot offer terms that improve others' payoffs. Turtle, with depleted RED inventory, offers smaller profitable swaps (selling a bit of RED for GREEN); pig and elephant again find those swaps attractive and accept, with pig becoming the realized recipient. Elephant's last-minute, small-margin proposals fail to attract partners.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on exchanges between red and green chips where green has a common value (0.50) and red has player-specific private values (fox 0.60, kangaroo 1.00, gorilla 0.90). Each proposer can request (buy) some chips and offer (sell) others; acceptance depends on whether the trade raises a responder's private value. Incentives: players will propose trades that raise (or at least do not lower) their own expected total value given their private valuations and inventory constraints. Because red values differ, trades that move red toward the highest-valuing player (kangaroo) tend to be costly for red-holders but can be profitable if paid with enough greens. Uncertainty and strategic considerations: proposers know only their own private values but can infer likely acceptance from public holdings and common green value; responders decide privately based on their own valuations and acceptance thresholds (kangaroo and gorilla are priors to be relatively accepting, fox is more mixed). Early rounds involve testing rates (e.g., 1 green for 1 red is attractive to fox but unattractive to kangaroo/gorilla), while middle rounds show more mutually beneficial, inventory-constrained swaps (e.g., selling red for two greens). Because multiple acceptances result in a random recipient, proposers sometimes structure offers likely to attract at least one acceptor rather than targeting a specific partner. Over three rounds we forecast a sequence of conservative, inventory-feasible proposals where kangaroo steadily accumulates red (highest private value), fox opportunistically swaps 1-for-1 when profitable for fox, and gorilla alternates selling red for greens when advantageous. The result is repeated small-volume trades (mostly 1 red <-> 2 greens or 1-for-1 proposals) with some trades declined when they would hurt all responders.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple, low-friction trades. Fox, who gains from converting green into slightly more-valued red (0.60 vs 0.50), makes an initial 1-for-1 attempt that is too cheap for red-holders and is declined. Kangaroo then posts a competitively priced offer (pay 2 greens for 1 red) that both non-senders find acceptable; multiple accepts are plausible and a random recipient is realized (gorilla in this forecast). After receiving greens, gorilla opportunistically offers to sell a red for two greens, which fox accepts, moving a red toward fox while increasing gorilla's greens.

Middle round: players adjust to updated inventories. Fox, having gained a red earlier, finds selling red for two greens profitable and proposes that to kangaroo, who accepts (kangaroo's high red value means 2 greens for 1 red is marginal but acceptable given low acceptance threshold). Kangaroo then again posts a 2G-for-1R offer to acquire more red; gorilla accepts. Finally, gorilla repeats a sell-red-for-two-greens pattern and fox accepts, reflecting straightforward value-improving swaps given current holdings.

Closing round: proposers squeeze remaining marginal gains and inventories constrain possibilities. Fox repeats a 1G-for-1R attempt that remains too cheap for red-holders and is declined. Kangaroo, still pursuing red accumulation, posts another 2G-for-1R offer; multiple responders find it acceptable and a random recipient is realized (fox in this forecast). Finally, gorilla once more offers to sell a red for two greens; kangaroo — now lower on greens but still willing at this rate — accepts, sending another red to kangaroo and finishing the sequence with kangaroo as the principal red accumulator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with equal inventories (10 red, 10 green) but different private red-chip values (bat 0.90, tiger 0.80, unicorn 0.60). Green chips are common-value (0.50). Because every player values red more than green, simple 1-for-1 swaps favor the red-holder and typically reduce the acceptor's payoff; jointly beneficial exchanges require asymmetric quantities (e.g., the lower-red-value player selling multiple reds in exchange for more greens). This creates a predictable pattern: the lowest-red-value player (unicorn) can credibly offer packages that transfer reds to higher-valuing partners in exchange for enough greens to make both sides better off. Mid-value (tiger) sometimes acts as a conciliatory proposer and will propose modest mutually beneficial packages when the counterparty can afford the required greens. The highest-red-value player (bat) is the least willing to sell red but can profitably buy red if they can pay enough greens. Inventory constraints (who actually has enough greens to pay) matter and can determine which mutually beneficial opportunities are feasible. Because acceptance is simultaneous and proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposals that look attractive to more than one non-sender risk a random recipient selection. Overall, we forecast a mix of declined exploratory proposals and several executed multi-unit exchanges where lower-red-valued players sell bundles of red for larger bundles of green to higher-red-valued partners; the largest flow of red is from unicorn (lowest red value) toward tiger and bat when feasible given green balances. Each player acts to maximize their own chip-value given these constraints and the private valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-move testing: proposers explore mutually beneficial integer bundles. Tiger (conciliatory, medium patience) opens with a positive, slightly generous package that is mutually beneficial with bat (who values red most); bat then offers a package that targets tiger's holdings; unicorn follows by selling red to a buyer who can pay enough greens. Inventory feasibility (who has enough greens to pay) and private red valuations drive who accepts.

After the initial exchanges, holdings shift (some players have fewer greens). Proposers who would need counterparties to pay more greens face feasibility limits. Tiger and bat make exploratory/simple proposals that are unattractive or infeasible given private values and counterparty inventories, so they are declined. Unicorn again posts a packaged sale of red for greens that is mutually beneficial and feasible given current green balances, and it is accepted by the player who can afford the payment.

Endgame: with inventories shifted (some players low on greens), tiger finds a feasible bundled purchase from unicorn that benefits both and executes it. Bat remains restrained (low green inventory), making one more low-stakes proposal that is declined. Unicorn completes one final mutually beneficial sale of red for greens to the player who can pay, moving red toward higher-valuing hands.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles under private valuations. Greens have a common value (0.50) for everyone; blue and red values differ and therefore drive most strategic demand. Bear highly values blue (1.00) and moderately values red (0.60); buffalo also likes blue (0.70) but values red very little (0.10); goose dislikes blue and red (both 0.10) relative to green. Given these priors, expect proposers who want blue (bear and buffalo) to offer low-value red or middle-value green to attract sellers; proposers who want green (goose and sometimes others) will offer small bundles of the chips they value least (red or blue). Responders evaluate each proposal only with their private values and current holdings: they accept trades that raise their own total value; if multiple responders accept, the partner is determined at random, so proposers face trade-off between attractive terms (more likely to be accepted) and the risk of being matched to an unintended recipient. Inventory constraints and immediate updates after each executed trade create path dependence: early transfers of blue to bear concentrate blue with the highest-value player, while many small exchanges of red out of buffalo/goose reduce their ability to use red as a bargaining chip later. Uncertainty is primarily about which counterpart will accept and how holdings will evolve; behavioral priors (patience, acceptance thresholds) modulate but choices are driven by private payoff changes.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple, intuitively profitable swaps. Buffalo and bear compete for blue; goose is a likely willing seller of blue in exchange for red or green because goose values blue very little. Proposers tend to offer more of the chip they value least to obtain a chip they value more. Early trades therefore move blue toward bear and buffalo while goose accumulates red and green.

With holdings updated, proposers repeat small, high-margin swaps. Bear continues to target blue by offering green; buffalo repeats a compact red-for-blue offer that is attractive to low-blue-value goose; goose looks for green and offers red bundles. Acceptance depends on private gains for each responder, and when both non-senders gain the choice of recipient is random.

Late-round dynamics show similar micro-patterns: proposers adjust quantities to be just attractive enough to one of the other players. Bear continues to accumulate blue; buffalo uses red to buy incremental blue; goose trades small red bundles for green. As remaining inventories shift, acceptances track private payoff gains and the randomness when both accept can alter final allocations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with identical red valuations (RED = $1.00) and identical green valuations (GREEN = $0.50) but heterogeneous blue valuations (BLUE = $0.40, $0.50, $0.70) generate repeated, inventory-constrained trades where red is the most desirable common focal good and blue is differentially valuable across players. Proposers will tend to offer chips that are relatively cheap to them (e.g., BLUE for the low-blue-value player) in exchange for chips that are relatively valuable (RED). Recipients accept when the incoming trade raises their own private total value; asymmetric blue values produce predictable accept/decline patterns (the high-blue-value player is reluctant to give blue away, the low-blue-value player is willing to give blue). Because acceptance is simultaneous and multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers sometimes present terms that appeal strongly to one particular counterparty (e.g., 2 BLUE for 1 RED to attract the high-blue-valuing partner). Strategic considerations: early rounds create opportunities to pick off cheap blue from the low-blue player or cheap red from players who accumulate many reds; uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations, so proposers rely on inferred thresholds (from personas) to choose quantities that are likely to be accepted by at least one partner. Overall we forecast a sequence where otter (low-blue) repeatedly offers small quantities of BLUE to acquire RED; peacock (middle-blue) tries moderate, balanced swaps; cat (high-blue) exploits green-for-blue swaps to accumulate more BLUE while selectively parting with RED only when highly compensated.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers test simple, low-complexity exchange rates. Otter (low-blue) will try to convert surplus BLUE into RED with modest offers; peacock will try to obtain RED as well by offering BLUE or GREEN; cat (high-blue) will prefer to buy BLUE by selling GREEN. Responses reflect clear value gains for the responder (cat often accepts BLUE-for-RED offers; otter sometimes accepts GREEN-for-BLUE swaps). Holdings update immediately and shape subsequent offers.

With updated inventories, proposers repeat successful patterns. Otter again offers BLUE for RED (still profitable to otter and attractive to the high-blue recipient). Peacock repeats a similar BLUE-for-RED attempt targeting the high-blue partner. Cat continues to trade GREEN for BLUE to accumulate the color she values most. Recipients accept trades that raise their private total value (or are weakly nonnegative and align with personas' moderate acceptance tendencies).

Final round: proposers try modest, low-friction swaps to secure small marginal gains before the game ends. Peacock attempts a conservative BLUE-for-GREEN swap to adjust holdings; otter again leverages remaining BLUE to buy RED; cat finishes by converting one GREEN into one BLUE (a favorable marginal gain for cat). Randomization in multi-accept situations is rare here because offers are typically tailored to appeal to one specific counterparty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values: players disagree about red and blue but share the same green value (0.50). That creates clear trading motives: players will try to shed chips they value relatively less and acquire chips they value relatively more. Badger (BLUE=0.80, RED=0.80) favors high-value red/blue; camel (RED=0.60, GREEN=0.50, BLUE=0.40) prefers red and is relatively tolerant of small gains; squid (BLUE=0.60, GREEN=0.50, RED=0.20) disfavors red and is willing to sell red cheaply. Because proposals must exchange one color for another, proposers will typically offer colors that are low-value to potential partners (to entice acceptance) and request colors that are high-value to themselves. Uncertainty arises from private valuations (each responder only knows their own values) and the random tie-break if multiple responders accept. Personas bias likely behavior: camel is deal-ready and will accept small net gains, squid is conciliatory and reliably sells low-value red, and badger is numerate and opportunistic but somewhat cautious. Over three rounds we expect trades that move red away from squid (low value) toward badger/camel, and blue to move toward badger (high value), with green used as a medium to make mutually beneficial swaps. Some later proposals will be declined when mutually profitable counterparties are unavailable.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage exploration and opportunistic extraction of red from squid. Badger and camel target squid's cheap red by offering green. Squid sells red willingly. Squid then uses accumulated greens to acquire blue from others; multiple acceptances are possible when trades look profitable for more than one responder, producing random allocation risk.

With red concentrated more with badger/camel and squid holding more green/blue, trade opportunities shift. Badger pursues blue by offering red (profitable for him and acceptable to camel). Camel continues to buy green cheaply from squid by offering red. Squid then leverages its green stock to purchase additional blue from camel, who has some remaining blue and will accept a favorable swap.

Endgame adjustments: badger makes small, low-friction swaps to increase blue holdings; camel attempts a marginal swap to acquire red using green but lacks a willing counterparty, so declines occur when no partner gains. Squid explores an additional green-for-blue swap but fails to find a counterparty with matching incentives, so the final round contains declined proposals as inventories limit mutually beneficial trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-chip-for-single-chip offers across three rounds. Each player knows only their own chip values: dog values RED highest (1.00) and BLUE lowest (0.20); butterfly values BLUE highest (0.90) and RED intermediate (0.60); ram values GREEN highest (0.50) and BLUE lowest (0.10). These private valuations create clear directed incentives: dog repeatedly tries to acquire RED by offering low-value BLUE; butterfly trades RED for BLUE to accumulate her high-value BLUE; ram repeatedly offers low-value BLUE to acquire GREEN. Because accept/reject decisions are private and only non-senders can accept, most profitable one-for-one exchanges are attractive to at least one counterparty. Uncertainty arises when both non-senders would gain and both accept — the actual recipient is then chosen at random, which shapes proposers' risk about who benefits. Person-level priors (dog: conciliatory, accepts modest gains; butterfly: quick-deal, disciplined; ram: search-oriented and patient) make these repeated, small-value exchanges likely to execute, favoring trades that move chips toward the players who value them most while proposers exploit asymmetries by offering their lowest-value chips.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers test simple 1-for-1 exchanges that convert their low-value holdings into higher-value chips. Dog opens by offering BLUE for RED (cheap for dog, attractive to butterfly). Butterfly then leverages her valuation of BLUE to buy more BLUE by offering RED; because both dog and ram would gain from a RED-for-BLUE swap, both are likely to accept and the recipient is randomized. Ram uses his low-value BLUE to purchase GREEN from butterfly. These small, direct trades establish trade patterns and update inventories for later rounds.

Middle-round dynamics: inventories have shifted so dog and butterfly continue the proven pattern of swapping their low-value BLUE for higher-value RED (dog) or accumulating BLUE (butterfly). Butterfly's disciplined bookkeeping and low patience make her repeatedly offer RED to acquire BLUE; sometimes both non-senders benefit and accept, producing random recipients. Ram continues using BLUE to pick up GREEN, typically accepted by butterfly who values BLUE highly.

Final-round dynamics: with inventories more polarized (butterfly accumulating BLUE, dog accumulating RED, ram accumulating GREEN), players repeat the same low-friction 1-for-1 swaps to tilt holdings further toward their private values. Proposers keep offers small and predictable; when both non-senders benefit, acceptance is likely from both and the recipient is randomized, which introduces final-round allocation noise but not large strategic deviation given short horizon and modest per-chip values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but differ in private valuations: lion values BLUE most (1.00), iguana values RED most (0.80), and alligator prefers BLUE (0.60) over GREEN (0.50) over RED (0.30). Green is a common-value medium (0.50). Given these preferences and the rule that proposers request one color and offer another, predictable directional flows emerge: trades that move BLUE from low-value holders (iguana) to high-value holders (alligator, lion) and RED from low-value holders (alligator, lion) to the high-value holder (iguana). Proposers will therefore tend to offer their least-preferred chip in exchange for their most-preferred chip. Responders accept when the immediate, certain valuation gain (or expected gain if multiple acceptances create random selection) is positive. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers tend to structure offers so only the desired counterparty benefits enough to accept (e.g., offering RED for BLUE attracts iguana but not lion). Over three rounds with one proposal per player per round, we expect a sequence of small 1-for-1 exchanges that concentrate RED with iguana and BLUE with alligator and lion. Uncertainty arises because acceptance is simultaneous and proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, which leads proposers to target partners whose private valuations make acceptance likely; participants' personas (search orientation, bookkeeping discipline, and moderate acceptance thresholds) also bias toward repeated small, clearly beneficial trades rather than risky, large one-sided bundles.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers will make simple, clearly beneficial 1-for-1 offers. Alligator and lion will target BLUE (their top good); iguana will target RED. Proposers structure offers to attract a single acceptor (often iguana when BLUE is requested and RED offered, or iguana when GREEN is offered for BLUE). Early trades shift a small number of BLUE to the high-value players and RED to iguana, creating inventory asymmetries that shape later offers.

Second round: inventories are now asymmetric (ig­uana has more RED and fewer BLUE). Players repeat the profitable patterns: alligator and lion continue to buy BLUE by offering low-value chips (RED or GREEN) knowing iguana has both incentive and capacity to sell BLUE, while iguana continues to buy RED by offering BLUE. The personas (bookkeeping discipline and patience) make proposals conservative and targeted, favoring 1-for-1 exchanges that are easy to evaluate and likely to be accepted.

Final round: patterns crystallize. Alligator and lion continue to accumulate BLUE while iguana accumulates RED. Quantities remain small (1-for-1) because players are cautious about inventory limits and prefer clear, positive-payoff trades rather than risking multiple acceptances. By the end, BLUE is concentrated with alligator and lion and RED with iguana; greens have been used as a convenient medium in some lion proposals.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip exchange is driven by private valuations for red and blue chips (green is common-value). Each player seeks to increase their own total value. Whale prefers red strongly over blue, dolphin prefers blue over red, and duck values red highest but less extremely than whale; all value green at 0.50. The most natural mutually beneficial trades are pairwise swaps of red for blue: a player who values blue more will sell red to someone who values red more, and vice versa. Because acceptance is simultaneous and multiple acceptances lead to random matching, proposers tend to ask for small, one-for-one swaps that create positive surplus for at least one counterparty. Uncertainty comes from (1) not observing others' private values, so proposers infer likely acceptors from public patterns and personas, and (2) random selection if multiple players accept. Given the personas (whale and duck are relatively conciliatory; dolphin is pragmatic but low-search), we forecast a sequence of small 1-for-1 red↔blue trades, with green remaining untouched because its common value offers no private-arbitrage opportunity.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves test simple one-for-one red↔blue swaps. Whale, who values red far more than blue, proposes to convert one blue into one red. Duck benefits from giving red for blue and accepts. Dolphin then offers the reverse (sell red for blue); both whale and duck would gain from exchanging, so both accept and one is randomly chosen (whale). Duck then tries the same buy-blue/sell-red move and whale (again a good counterparty) accepts.

Players continue proposing small red↔blue swaps. Whale again tries to convert blue to red; duck (who benefits) accepts. Dolphin then proposes to swap red for blue; both other players would gain from this ratio, and duck is randomly selected as the recipient. Duck then proposes buying red for blue and whale, who benefits, accepts. These moves further concentrate red with the most red-preferring player(s) and blue with the blue-preferring player.

Final round continues the established pattern: whale again proposes blue→red and duck accepts; dolphin proposes red→blue, both others would accept and whale is selected as recipient; duck ends by trying another blue→red swap which whale accepts. No greens are exchanged because they offer no private-arbitrage gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red and blue chips (green is low and common-value for everyone). Turtle-yellow values blue the most, gorilla-purple values red the most, and zebra-blue values red modestly above blue. Because each proposer can request one color and offer another, most profitable and credible trades are 1-for-1 red↔blue exchanges: proposers who want blue will offer red, and proposers who want red will offer blue. Recipients accept only when the chip they receive is worth more to them than the chip they give up. Green plays little role because its value (0.50) is lower than most players' preferred chips, so offers involving green tend to be declined. Uncertainty comes from not knowing which non-sender will accept (if multiple accept, a random accepting partner is selected), and from private valuations: proposers must anticipate which counterpart(s) will find the terms profitable. Given player personas, we forecast straightforward 1-for-1 offers (minimal search/exploration by turtle, modest conciliation by gorilla and zebra) and acceptances when the receiving player gains value. Proposers generally aim to convert less-preferred chips into higher-valued chips for themselves; recipients accept when the swap raises their own private payoff.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: turtle (who most prefers blue) opens with a simple red→blue swap that benefits both gorilla and zebra; gorilla then tries to trade blue→red to capture more red for itself and targets the player for whom the swap is beneficial; zebra follows a similar pattern, again using 1-for-1 red/blue swaps. Green remains unused because receiving green in exchange for red or blue would reduce recipients' value.

Players repeat the profitable red↔blue pattern. Turtle again offers red for blue (beneficial to both opponents), gorilla again offers blue for red (targeting whoever gains), and zebra repeats a targeted request for red in exchange for blue. Because proposers cannot choose the partner, acceptances are driven purely by private value gains, producing mostly single-acceptor outcomes when only one other player benefits.

Final round: the same incentive structure yields more 1-for-1 red/blue exchanges. Turtle continues to convert red into blue (it values blue most), and gorilla repeatedly trades blue for red (it values red most). Zebra continues to request red against blue when it is marginally beneficial to a single counterpart. Greens remain off the table because they would make recipients worse off.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles over three rounds. Each player privately values chips differently: zebra strongly prefers BLUE, cat prefers RED, and iguana prefers RED. GREEN is a common-value middle option. Proposers will typically offer a chip color they value relatively less in exchange for a color they value more (e.g., zebra selling RED to buy BLUE; cat selling BLUE or GREEN to buy RED; iguana selling BLUE or GREEN to buy RED). Responders accept when the trade increases their own private value (or is near indifference given persona-driven acceptance thresholds). Because multiple responders can accept and the partner is chosen randomly, proposers sometimes extract favorable terms from the player with the weakest marginal value for the requested chip. Uncertainty enters through private values (each player knows only their own valuations) and random selection when multiple accept; personas (patience, search, acceptance-threshold) bias whether a player accepts quickly or waits for a better offer.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial round: players test simple one-for-one swaps to acquire their top-valued color. Cat and iguana try to acquire RED by offering BLUE or GREEN; zebra tries to acquire BLUE by offering RED. Expect trades where zebra gives up RED (low value to zebra) in exchange for BLUE or GREEN, and cat or iguana give up their lower-valued color to gain RED.

After initial testing, proposers continue trading along revealed preference lines: cat and iguana keep targeting RED (selling GREEN or BLUE), zebra keeps targeting BLUE (selling RED). Given previous exchanges, zebra still holds relatively more BLUE and fewer RED, so zebra remains the likely seller of RED for modest offers.
