When both non-senders would gain, multiple acceptances can occur, producing random partner selection.

Final round: players consolidate holdings. Cat continues to exchange lower-valued BLUE for RED. Iguana again targets RED by offering GREEN (or occasionally BLUE). Zebra persistently offers RED to obtain more BLUE. Acceptance remains driven by private value gains; when both potential sellers would benefit, random selection determines who transacts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging higher-value red/blue chips for lower-value green chips (green = $0.50 for all). Players privately value red and blue differently: bear (R=0.80, B=0.80), peacock (R=0.80, B=0.70), swan (R=0.50, B=0.80). Proposers will try to capture positive personal surplus by asking for chips they value more and offering chips they value less. Because recipients decide privately, proposers often choose terms that some counterparty would strictly gain from (or be indifferent to) to increase acceptance probability. Uncertainty comes from (1) not knowing others' private valuations, (2) random selection if multiple accept, and (3) inventory constraints after early accepted trades altering later feasibility. Across rounds we expect repeated patterns: peacock frequently accepts swaps that increase their red holdings (they value red highest), swan trades away red (low value for swan) to acquire blue, and bear sometimes trades symmetrically to capture blue while balancing acceptance likelihood. Many proposals that offer green for valuable chips will be declined unless a recipient is indifferent or receives a strict gain.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test one-for-one exchanges. Bear looks to convert lower-value green into higher-value red/blue but faces likely resistance from peacock (who values red highly). Peacock prefers accumulating red and is willing to trade red when they get green in neutral cases. Swan prefers blue and is willing to give red (which they value less) for blue. Early offers thus gravitate toward one-for-one swaps where one party gains and another is indifferent.

Holdings have shifted: peacock increased red, swan increased green/blue. Proposers now favor terms likely to be accepted given observed inventories. Bear uses symmetric red-for-blue swaps to attract peacock (who benefits slightly), peacock repeats low-cost red-for-green gains to swap with swan, and swan again targets blue via red-for-blue swaps that peacock is willing to accept.

Final round: inventories reflect accumulated red for peacock and blue for swan. Bear again targets blue from peacock with a symmetric swap that peacock tolerates. Peacock continues trading green for marginal red gains with swan (swan is often indifferent and accepts). Swan secures a final blue via swapping relatively low-value red with peacock, who gains slightly from the exchange.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining sequence is driven by heterogenous private chip values and inventory constraints. All players start with equal physical holdings (10 of each color) but differ in how much they privately value red, blue, and green: monkey and camel place very high value on blue (monkey highest), gorilla places highest value on green, and green is a focal common-value chip for all (0.50). Strategic implications: (1) Players who highly value blue (monkey, camel) are reluctant to sell it; proposals that request blue must pay a premium (offer chips the seller values more than their blue). (2) Gorilla prefers green and will often offer lower-valued chips (red/blue) to acquire green; because gorilla values green more than others, some trades exchanging blue or red for green are mutually beneficial. (3) Monkey, who most values blue (0.90), will aggressively seek additional blue while being willing to part with low-value red or occasionally green when the exchange benefits them. (4) Camel values blue strongly too (0.80) and is numerically disciplined; camel will accept exchanges that clearly increase their private value and will sometimes convert red into blue if the price is acceptable. Uncertainty and bargaining frictions matter: proposers do not know the exact private values of others, only priors; acceptance thresholds vary by persona (monkey more conciliatory/searching, gorilla patient and selective, camel disciplined and instrumentally focused). In three-player proposals, multiple players can accept; proposers risk a randomly selected acceptor if more than one accepts. Across rounds we expect: (a) gorilla repeatedly targets green, offering red/blue; (b) camel targets blue and will offer red or green to get it if a counterparty values the incoming chips sufficiently; (c) monkey targets blue too and will sell low-value red to obtain blue. Inventory constraints lead to diminishing willingness to sell prized chips as holdings of those chips fall, and visible trade history changes others' incentives (e.g., a player who recently accumulated many blues will be more reluctant to sell them). The forecast below uses these priors to map plausible offers, likely accept/reject responses, and immediate inventory updates.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage moves: proposers test each other's willingness to trade high-value for lower-value chips. Gorilla (who most values green) tries to buy green by offering red; camel (who values red and green equally but likes blue) will accept profitable red-for-green swaps; monkey will prefer to keep/gain blue and sell low-value red or green when profitable. Expect some mutually beneficial trades where the acceptor gets chips they value more than the ones they give up.

With initial trades executed, holdings diverge and proposers refine offers. Gorilla continues to acquire green and may offer blue (which gorilla values less) for green. Camel, having gained red, targets additional blue by offering red; monkey continues to seek blue and will use low-value red to buy more blue. Acceptance patterns reflect where counterparties see net private-value gains.

Late-stage adjustments: players capitalize on remaining arbitrage. Camel continues to seek blue by converting red into blue; gorilla still prefers green and can sell red to obtain it; monkey makes a final small trade to further increase blue holding using low-value red. At this stage, inventories and recent trades make sellers of blue scarcer, so proposers target the one counterparty who is indifferent or can be made better off.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game will be driven by each player's private, per-chip valuations (RED and BLUE differ by player; GREEN is common-value at $0.50). Because each player starts with identical inventories (10 of each), the logical incentives are to trade away lower-valued chips to acquire higher-valued chips. Alligator (prefers RED >> GREEN >> BLUE) will repeatedly try to buy RED using BLUE as the currency; Otter (prefers BLUE >> GREEN >> RED) will flip low-value RED into high-value BLUE when possible; Tiger (prefers RED >> GREEN >> BLUE) will seek RED at a relatively low cost by offering BLUE. The proposer schedule (alligator, otter, tiger each round) plus immediate execution after acceptance creates path dependence: early accepted exchanges change inventories and the set of feasible profitable trades for later turns. Acceptance decisions are private and based only on each player's own valuations and holdings; if multiple players accept, the recipient is chosen at random, adding uncertainty that proposers can exploit (e.g., targeting a specific counterpart by making offers attractive mainly to that counterpart). Personas (Alligator: conciliatory, low-search; Otter: anchor-sensitive, high-search; Tiger: disciplined, low-search) make Alligator and Tiger comparatively consistent and value-driven proposers, while Otter will search and adapt to states. Overall we expect mostly value-improving, dyadic trades (selling low-value chips for higher-value ones) to be accepted, with occasional declined proposals when terms are neutral or unfavorable to potential acceptors.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players start balanced. Proposers will attempt straightforward, mutually beneficial swaps that exchange a lower-valued color for a higher-valued one. Alligator will offer BLUE for RED (BLUE low for him), Otter will convert marginal RED into GREEN or BLUE, and Tiger will try to buy RED by giving up BLUE. Because Otter initially values BLUE highly and RED weakly, Otter is a likely counterparty for Alligator's RED-seeking offers.

Inventories are now asymmetric (Alligator and Tiger have accumulated RED; Otter has more BLUE). Alligator continues to target RED using remaining BLUE; Otter, holding many BLUE and few RED, opportunistically sells scarce RED to acquire more BLUE; Tiger selectively buys RED when price (in BLUE) is favorable. Because Otter still holds some RED early in the round, many of Alligator's and Tiger's offers find a willing Otter counterparty.

Late-stage: Otter has become heavily concentrated in BLUE with very few RED remaining; Alligator holds a large RED stock but little BLUE; Tiger has increased RED but limited BLUE. Proposers confront tighter feasibility constraints (counterpart may lack the requested color) and diminishing mutually beneficial opportunities. We expect Alligator to squeeze one final small RED purchase from Otter; Otter may propose neutral or marginal trades seeking small improvements (often declined by cautious recipients); Tiger's last attempts to buy RED often fail if they require a counterpart's RED that Otter no longer holds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but very different private values for red and blue; green is common-value. Dolphin strongly prefers blue (1.00) and dislikes red (0.20), frog strongly prefers red (1.00) and values blue modestly (0.30), pig has middling preferences (red and blue ~0.40, green 0.50). Given these priors, bargaining dynamics are driven by mutually beneficial, one-sided gains: players who highly value a color will offer chips that others value less. Proposers typically request the color they value more and offer a color they value less, making acceptance tempting for specific partners (notably frog often benefits when asked to give blue for red; dolphin often benefits when asked to give red for blue). Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own values, so proposers must rely on public history and typical acceptance patterns (inferred from likely valuations and personas). Personas suggest dolphin and frog will craft relatively modest, search-oriented offers aiming to secure at least one accepting partner, while pig tends to be less exploratory and will make straightforward, inventory-feasible proposals. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers sometimes aim offers that are attractive to a single partner to avoid splitting acceptances. Over three rounds we expect a sequence of small, repeated bilateral exchanges that concentrate red with frog and blue with dolphin, and increase pig's green slightly — consistent with each actor maximizing their private chip value while responding to observed holdings changes.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers test simple favorable swaps that are likely to attract a single partner. Dolphin will try to convert low-value red into high-value blue, expecting frog (who strongly prefers red) to accept. Frog will reciprocate by buying red with blue, again likely to attract dolphin. Pig will aim to acquire green by offering red, which is attractive to frog but not to dolphin. Expect mostly 1-for-1 swaps that shift red toward frog, blue toward dolphin, and green gradually to pig when profitable.

Mid game: players exploit observed imbalances. Dolphin continues converting remaining low-value red into additional blue, targeting frog (who has many red and some blue). Frog continues to buy red using small amounts of blue when the exchange is clearly favorable to frog. Pig repeatedly pursues green when it can offer a red chunk that is attractive to frog. Trades remain modest in scale to respect inventory constraints and to produce clear gains for at least one partner.

Endgame: inventories have polarized (more blue with dolphin, more red with frog). Dolphin makes final conversions of red into blue, expecting frog to continue accepting favorable swaps. Frog opportunistically sells small amounts of blue to obtain additional red when profitable. Pig finishes by swapping a blue for a green with dolphin, as dolphin values blue heavily and is willing to trade green for blue. The sequence consolidates each player's preferred color and leaves small imbalances but no large risky offers late in the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories of red, blue, and green chips but value them differently (iguana favors blue, owl favors red, squid is more balanced between blue and green). Over three rounds (nine proposal turns) proposers will tend to ask for chips they privately value highly and offer chips they value less, subject to inventory constraints and the knowledge that any non-sender can accept if the exchange benefits them. Strategic forces: (1) proposers will craft offers that make at least one other player strictly better off given that player's private values (to induce acceptance) while keeping as much high-value inventory as possible; (2) recipients decide only from their own values and current holdings, so deals that trade away a high-value chip (for that recipient) are typically rejected; (3) when two recipients would benefit, an accepted trade will be resolved by random selection, which reduces proposers' ability to target a specific partner. Uncertainty enters because each player sees only their own valuations; proposers must infer which counterpart is likeliest to accept given publicly observable holdings and prior trades. Given the player personas, iguana is numerate and patient and makes inventory-consistent, moderately aggressive proposals; owl prefers closure and tends to accept modest positive gains; squid is relatively conciliatory and will accept clear gains, especially when trades swap low-value red for higher-value blue/green.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining. Proposers test who will trade with whom. Iguana (high blue value) targets blue by offering low-value red; owl (high red value) capitalizes on blue-rich partners by offering blue for red; squid looks to pick up blue using cheap red. Expect a mix of accepted and declined offers depending on whether the counterparty gains under their private valuation.

Midgame adjustments. Holders who gained preferred chips are more reluctant to give them up; proposers adapt by offering more attractive bundles (larger quantities or of a type the recipient prefers). Owl, holding extra red, is willing to trade greens to acquire more red; iguana continues to seek blue; squid leverages red to pick up additional high-value chips.

Endgame. Inventory constraints (some players low on a color) limit feasible trades. Proposers try smaller, low-risk exchanges that can still produce clear, private gains for at least one counterpart. Some offers are declined when no recipient gets a clear improvement; others are accepted when the exchange flips a low-value chip for a higher-value one for a potential recipient.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories but different private values for red and blue; green is common-value. Gorilla values blue very low and red moderately; Llama and Whale value blue high and red moderately. Expect Gorilla to use cheap blue as a bargaining chip to buy red (profitable for her and often attractive to others), while Llama will seek to trade low-value green for high-value blue when she can, and Whale will propose small, positive-margin trades that exploit differences between players. Acceptance decisions depend only on a recipient's own valuations and current holdings: offers that increase a recipient's personal payoff are likely to be accepted, and Whale (prosocial, low threshold) and Llama (quick-deal, anchor-sensitive) are relatively likely to accept clearly profitable offers. There is uncertainty about which counterparty will accept when multiple players benefit; in those cases a random accepting recipient is selected. Over three rounds we expect several simple one-for-one or two-for-one exchanges where Gorilla sells blue to obtain red, Llama trades green for blue with Gorilla when profitable, and Whale makes modest, low-friction proposals that sometimes succeed.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: Gorilla opens by trying to convert cheap blue into red. Llama prefers quick, profitable swaps for blue using green. Whale will test offers that could be accepted by at least one other player. When offers benefit multiple players, a random accepting recipient will be chosen.

Mid-game: players have adjusted inventories. Gorilla continues converting remaining blue into red at favorable rates. Llama opportunistically trades green for additional blue when Gorilla can supply it. Whale makes a modest swap that benefits Gorilla and is accepted.

Final round: inventories limit some proposals (e.g., Gorilla may run low on blue). Gorilla makes another blue-for-red conversion while Llama and Whale finalize small, positive-margin swaps. Some offers are attractive to multiple partners and are resolved by random selection; others are targeted and accepted by a single counterparty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for red and blue chips (green is common-value). Tiger highly values BLUE (1.00) and RED (0.80), lion has moderate value for BLUE (0.30) and RED (0.40), and duck values RED and GREEN equally (0.50) but BLUE weakly (0.20). Given these priors, exchanges that move BLUE from lower-value holders (duck, lion) to tiger and move RED/ GREEN toward duck or lion are mutually beneficial. Proposers can structure one-for-one swaps that create positive-sum gains for themselves and at least one potential counterparty; acceptance decisions will reflect each responder's private tradeoff (gain from received chips minus loss of given chips). Uncertainty arises because players know only their own valuations and must infer others' preferences from prior offers and observed acceptances. Behaviorally, duck (conscientious, low search) will propose simple, closure-oriented swaps and accept clear gains; lion (patient, cautious) will seek slightly better-than-fair exchanges and may wait for better matches; tiger (values BLUE most) will actively accept trades that convert lower-value chips into BLUE. Expect frequent one-for-one accepted swaps moving BLUE to tiger and RED/GREEN to duck or lion, with occasional multi-accept turns resolved by random selection when both non-senders benefit.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: players start with equal inventories. Duck (who dislikes BLUE) will offer BLUE to acquire RED (which duck values more). Lion will try modest improvements (buy RED with BLUE) when profitable. Tiger, who most values BLUE, will accept swaps that exchange his lower-valued RED/GREEN for BLUE. Initial offers tend to be small (1-for-1) and are likely to be accepted by the mutually benefiting party (often tiger).

Mid-game: patterns from round 0 create clearer expectations. Duck continues converting low-value BLUE into GREEN or RED (both 0.50 for duck). Lion pursues small positive gains (e.g., trading RED for GREEN). Tiger repeatedly accepts exchanges that increase his BLUE holdings. Many trades remain 1-for-1 and are accepted by the party with the larger marginal gain; multi-accept outcomes are resolved randomly.

Endgame: inventories have shifted toward tiger holding many BLUE and others holding more RED/GREEN. Duck and lion continue to propose straightforward 1-for-1 swaps that incrementally improve their private value. Tiger remains willing to trade away lower-value RED/GREEN for additional BLUE. Because each player is maximizing individual value, proposals remain narrowly tailored to personally beneficial swaps and are typically accepted when at least one counterparty gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk- and inventory-constrained players repeatedly propose 1-for-1 or small multi-for-few chip swaps over three rounds. Public information: holdings and trade history; private information: each player's per-chip values. Bear and Goose both privately value RED most (1.00) and BLUE least (0.30 / 0.20), so they are natural demander(s) of RED. Ram privately values BLUE most (1.00) and RED less (0.80), so he is a natural demander of BLUE and a willing supplier of RED. Because proposers cannot choose which accepter is realized when multiple players accept, proposers structure offers to be attractive to at least one likely counterparty (Ram for BLUE-demanding offers; Ram remains a regular acceptor when offered many BLUE). Goose's persona (low acceptance threshold) and Ram's bookkeeping + search orientation make them likely to accept small, positive gains; Bear is moderately patient and will propose repeat favorable trades to Ram. Expect many executed bilateral exchanges where Ram trades away RED for multiple BLUE, and Bear/Goose pay BLUE (or sometimes GREEN) to accumulate RED. Uncertainty centers on how generous proposers must be to entice acceptance and occasional simultaneous acceptances producing random pairing.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial round: all players start with symmetric inventories (10 of each). Bear opens by trying to convert his relatively low-value BLUE into high-value RED; Ram (who values BLUE highly) is a natural acceptor. Goose will also propose RED-seeking trades but must be generous enough to entice Ram rather than Bear. Ram, when proposing, offers small RED amounts for BLUE and often finds both Bear and Goose willing to accept, producing random matching.

After initial conversions, Ram holds more BLUE and fewer RED; Bear and Goose each have increased RED holdings and reduced BLUE. Bear repeats targeted small trades offering BLUE for RED aimed at Ram (who values BLUE). Goose, less aggressive, is willing to offer larger BLUE bundles to secure additional RED. Ram continues to propose small RED-for-BLUE swaps that look profitable to both Bear and Goose, producing occasional simultaneous acceptance and random matching.

Final round: Ram has largely converted RED into BLUE and has strong incentives to continue small one-for-one swaps (selling his remaining RED). Bear and Goose, now richer in RED, still accept small profitable swaps when they appear (they gain RED relative to their BLUE losses). Proposals remain small and inventory-feasible; simultaneous acceptances continue to produce random matching in Ram's turns.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk-neutral players privately value RED and BLUE differently while GREEN is common-value. Each starts with 10 of each chip. Octopus (high BLUE/RED value = $1) is richest and will exploit favorable swaps that convert GREEN (0.5) into RED/BLUE (1.0). Ram (BLUE 0.7, RED 0.20) values BLUE much more than RED and will trade away low-value RED to acquire BLUE when he can craft sufficiently generous offers to entice others. Lion (BLUE 0.7, RED/GREEN 0.5) prefers search and fair trades but gains only modestly from swapping RED→BLUE, so many small one-for-one proposals can be rejected if they leave recipients indifferent or better off. Because responses are private and multiple acceptances resolve randomly, proposers who need execution are likely to (a) propose exchanges that are strictly profitable to themselves and (b) sometimes overpay (offer extra low-value chips) when necessary to create a clear gain for at least one counterparty. Uncertainty comes from private acceptance thresholds (mixed/medium) and random selection when multiple partners accept. Over three rounds we expect a pattern of: (i) octopus extracting reds by selling green when profitable, (ii) ram concentrating blue by offering extra red(s) to incentivize acceptance, and (iii) lion trying to acquire blue with modest offers that are often declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are equal (10 each). Early bargaining tests trading partners and acceptance thresholds. Lion will try small one-for-one swaps to get BLUE but recipients either are indifferent (octopus) or lose value (ram), so such offers often fail. Octopus will exploit GREEN-to-RED swaps (profitable to him) and can find a taker in ram (who gains green for low-value red). Ram, knowing his RED is cheap, can propose larger sweeteners (e.g., 2 RED for 1 BLUE) that make recipients better off and thus get executed.

After the first round octopus has accumulated some REDs by selling GREEN; ram has increased BLUE via a generous RED-for-BLUE offer. Given these holdings, lion's simple one-for-one attempts to get BLUE remain unattractive to others. Octopus repeats the profitable GREEN→RED swap when it benefits him; ram continues to use small sweeteners (2 RED for 1 BLUE) that make recipients better off and thus get accepted (often by lion or octopus depending on perceived gain).

By the final round octopus is a clear beneficiary of prior trades (many high-value chips), ram has concentrated more BLUE, and lion still tries modest acquisitions but faces limited willing partners. Octopus may execute neutral swaps (RED↔BLUE one-for-one) to re-balance if it facilitates execution; ram will continue to use multi-RED sweeteners to acquire BLUE, which tends to be accepted by both lion and octopus because they strictly gain in those exchanges.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or small bundle) chip exchanges across three rounds. Each proposer offers chips of one color in exchange for chips of a different color; responders accept if the exchange raises their private value given their own valuations (they do not observe others' private values). Key incentives: players prefer to trade away chips they value least (buffalo tends to offload BLUE), and to acquire chips they value more (cat and duck both value RED highly). Green is a common but lower-value chip (0.50) that can be used as a cheap bargaining credit. Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot choose the partner when multiple players accept (recipient is random among acceptors), and each player only knows their own valuations and acceptance threshold. Given the provided personas, we expect relatively small, inventory-safe offers (1–2 chips), frequent acceptance by the low-threshold players (duck) and by players who see clear positive gains (cat), and occasional multi-accept situations that lead to random realization of the trade partner. The forecast below uses these priors to produce plausible offers and private acceptance decisions, updating holdings after each executed trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test straightforward, profitable exchanges where low-valued BLUE is used to buy RED or GREEN. Buffalo (who values BLUE least) is likely to offer BLUE for RED; cat will look for BLUE (she values it highly) and propose fair 1-for-1 swaps; duck will seek RED by offering BLUE. Early trades shift red toward players who value it highly and increase blue concentration in those who buy it.

Mid-game: holdings have shifted (buffalo accumulating RED, cat accumulating BLUE). Buffalo uses cheap BLUE to buy GREEN as a mild concession; cat uses small GREEN→BLUE trades to increase BLUE holdings; duck continues to target RED by selling BLUE. Offers remain small (1–2 chips) and are typically attractive to at least one counterparty.

Final round: proposers make small value-improving swaps to fine-tune their inventories. Buffalo again uses BLUE to pick up RED; cat presses to acquire additional RED (selling BLUE bundles); duck continues to target RED using BLUE as currency. Acceptance remains driven by private valuation gains; where both non-senders stand to gain, both accept and a random recipient is realized.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining sequence is driven by asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common moderate value for green chips (all players value green at $0.50). Kangaroo values green highest and blue very low, so it has persistent incentive to trade away blue for green. Butterfly and Squid both prefer red and blue over green, but butterfly values red slightly more than blue while squid also prefers red > blue. Expect proposers to offer chips they value relatively less (often green for butterfly/squid, blue for kangaroo) and request chips they value more (red for squid/butterfly, green for kangaroo). Because acceptance is private and proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, many offers that benefit multiple responders (e.g., kangaroo proposing blue-for-green) will be accepted by more than one player and the actual recipient will be determined at random. Strategic uncertainty arises from (1) private valuations (each player only knows their own values), (2) inventory constraints changing across rounds, and (3) random tie-breaking when multiple responders accept. Behaviorally, kangaroo is search-oriented and more likely to accept small favorable gains repeatedly; butterfly is patient and bookkeeping-disciplined and will craft small advantageous swaps; squid is anchor-sensitive and prefers structured, value-improving swaps. Overall expect a pattern of kangaroo accumulating green chips by selling blue, butterfly and squid occasionally buying red by offering green, and most trades being small (1–2 chips) and typically accepted when they create positive value for at least one counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players start with equal inventories. Butterfly, valuing red most but holding many, can profitably offer green to buy red — kangaroo, who prefers green to red, is the natural acceptor. Kangaroo will then try to swap blue for green; both butterfly and squid stand to gain by giving green in exchange for blue, so a multi-accept is likely and the recipient will be selected at random. Squid will opportunistically buy red with green when kangaroo's reservation allows it, producing another transfer to kangaroo.

Middle round: inventories have shifted (kangaroo holding more green, some players holding extra blue/red). Butterfly uses green to pick up blue (gain for butterfly, small loss for green-holder kangaroo but still beneficial overall). Kangaroo continues the blue→green strategy; when both other players can improve by exchanging, multiple acceptances are likely and the recipient will again be selected by randomization. Squid repeats a targeted green→red request that kangaroo finds marginally attractive.

Final round: players aim to lock in favorable marginal gains. Butterfly again offers green to buy red when kangaroo's marginal tradeoffs allow it. Kangaroo presses to convert remaining blue to green (sometimes selling two blue at once if inventory allows), creating another multi-accept risk. Squid uses a final green-for-red swap that is attractive enough for kangaroo to accept but unattractive to butterfly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories but different private values for red and blue (green is common-value at $0.50). Proposers will target partners who gain (or at least do not lose) from a swap: they tend to offer chips that are relatively more valuable to recipients and request chips that recipients value less. Because fox values red lowest (0.40) and all players value green at 0.50, swaps of green-for-red are the easiest mutually beneficial deals; similarly, offering red-for-blue often leaves the recipient roughly indifferent (bat) and can be used to reallocate high-value blue chips to the player who values them more (frog/fox). Player personas make bat patient, numerate, and willing to accept small or neutral exchanges; fox conciliatory and willing to accept small gains; frog focused on getting blue and moderately willing to accept neutral deals. Uncertainty arises from simultaneous accept/decline choices (multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient) and from limited inventories (repeated trades deplete cheaper chips like green), but early rounds are likely to feature safe, small-quantity (1-for-1) trades that are either mutually beneficial or neutral for the recipient. The forecast below treats players as instrumentally maximizing their private chip-value priors while respecting the personas' tendencies toward search, acceptance thresholds, and bookkeeping.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers favor low-friction, 1-for-1 exchanges. Bat starts by exploiting fox's low red value by offering green-for-red (profitable for bat, slightly profitable for fox). Fox then proposes a small beneficial exchange (red-for-green) that bat will accept. Frog aims to acquire blue by offering red to a bat who is indifferent between red and blue, so bat accepts a neutral swap.

After some chips have shifted, players continue with small 1-for-1 trades. Bat again targets fox's relatively cheap red by offering green-for-red (bat gains, fox gains slightly). Fox tries to accumulate blue by offering red-for-blue to a bat who is roughly indifferent, and frog again purchases blue from bat via a red-for-blue swap. The pattern favors safe, incremental gains for proposers and occasional neutral trades accepted by numerate, patient bat.

Endgame dynamics: inventories of green shrink and red accumulates with bat, but proposers continue to make small, high-probability trades. Bat continues converting green into red (value-improving for bat and marginally for fox). Fox persistently buys blue by offering red to a bat who is often indifferent and accepts. Frog makes a final push to increase blue holdings by trading red to bat for blue. These exchanges are conservative, 1-for-1 reallocations consistent with personas and private values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles across three rounds. Each player privately values red, blue, and a common-valued green chip; green is worth 0.50 to everyone, while blue and red differ across players (parrot and turtle prize blue highly at 1.00; dog values red and green equally at 0.50 and blue less at 0.30). Incentives therefore push parrot and turtle to acquire blue, and dog to acquire green or red while offloading blue. Because proposals ask for one color and offer another, most mutually attractive deals convert a player’s relatively low-value chips into ones they value more. Responders accept only when their private valuations predict a net gain. Uncertainty enters from (1) proposers not controlling which accepting player is selected when multiple accept, and (2) each player knowing only their own values, so acceptance decisions are myopic value checks rather than full-common-value calculations. Given the personas (dog and parrot relatively conciliatory and moderately patient; turtle somewhat selective), we forecast many simple one-for-one swaps that individually benefit proposers and occasionally benefit a single responder, with a few turns declined when the proposed exchange would reduce all responders’ private value.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test straightforward, value-improving one-for-one swaps. Dog starts by trying to trade low-value red for green (profit for dog) but both others would lose value so they decline. Parrot then attempts to convert one green into a blue (clear gain for parrot); dog prefers that swap and accepts while turtle declines. Turtle ends the round by offering green for blue (a natural move to obtain blue at low internal cost); dog accepts that advantage, so trades execute when they also improve the responder's private value.

Middle round: players build on successful simple exchanges. Dog repeatedly converts blue into green (his preferred higher-value chip). Parrot continues converting red into blue when profitable. Turtle again seeks blue by selling green. Many proposals are acceptable to dog (who is often the beneficiary as responder), and on at least one turn both non-sender players find the terms attractive, creating randomization in which accepter is chosen.

Final round: players capture remaining easy gains. Dog continues converting remaining blues into greens; parrot again trades red for blue when profitable; turtle makes a last attempt to pick up blue by selling green. Dog’s tendency to accept small improvements leads him to be the frequent counterparty, and some proposals are accepted by both non-senders, resolving by random selection.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game features asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each proposer will tend to ask for colors that are high-valued for themselves while offering colors they value less. Because green has identical value across players (0.50), green is a natural medium-of-exchange; blue and red are targets of exploitation: peacock highly values red and very devalues blue, unicorn highly values blue and devalues red, and badger prefers blue over red and green. Proposers will therefore try to craft exchange rates that extract chips they value highly while offering chips that at least one counterparty values less (so that counterparty will accept). Acceptance decisions are myopic and private: a responder accepts if the offered swap strictly improves their own total chip value given their private per-chip valuations. Multi-acceptance is possible and leads to a random recipient, which makes some proposers avoid deals that would be symmetrically acceptable to both others if they prefer a specific partner. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not control which accepting player will be matched when multiple accept, and each player observes only their own private values (so they may infer others' preferences from prior public trades). Person-level priors (badger: anchor-sensitive, low-search; peacock: disciplined, conciliatory; unicorn: conciliatory, less anchor-driven) shape likely offers: badger will make compact, anchor-driven offers to secure blue cheaply; peacock will trade away blue for red and accept advantageous swaps; unicorn will exploit peacock's low blue value to buy blue cheaply but be willing to trade red when advantageous.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round dynamics: proposers test counterpart willingness to swap low-value chips for high-value ones. Badger opens by trying to buy blue while offering green (greens are common-value and slightly cheaper for badger than red). Peacock, who devalues blue strongly, is the most likely early acceptor. Subsequent proposers exploit revealed inventories and prior trades: peacock will try to buy red by offering blue (targeting unicorn and badger who both value blue more than red), and unicorn will seek blue by offering red (targeting peacock).

Mid-game dynamics: inventories shifted after round 0 (peacock has fewer blue chips), so proposers calibrate offers to target the player who now has the low-cost supply of the chips they want. Badger continues to try to acquire blue by offering green (peacock remains the most likely acceptor). Peacock prefers disciplined, targeted swaps: rather than offering blue (which he is low on), he offers green to buy red and likely directs that to unicorn. Unicorn continues to buy blue from peacock when peacock can spare it.

End-game dynamics: remaining inventories constrain feasible offers (peacock may run low on blue), and proposers make one last set of extraction attempts. Badger again targets blue by selling green; peacock, now low on blue, trades green for red (targeting unicorn); unicorn uses remaining red to buy blue from badger when that is individually profitable for badger (offering enough red to compensate badger for the value of the blue he parts with). The randomization risk when both others would accept makes some proposers choose offers that appeal primarily to a single desired counterparty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose simple one-for-one (one-color-for-a-different-color) chip swaps across three rounds. Each player knows only their own private values for red and blue (green is common-value at $0.50). Incentives push trades that turn lower-valued holdings into higher-valued holdings for the proposer: dog (prefers red/green = $0.50, blue = $0.30) will try to trade away blue for red/green; mouse (green $0.50, red/blue $0.40) will trade lower-value red/blue for green; owl (blue $0.70, red/green $0.50) will trade away red/green to acquire blue. Because proposals are simultaneous for accept/decline, a proposal that offers a chip another player values more than the chip requested is likely to be accepted. Uncertainty remains about whether multiple non-senders will accept (triggering a random recipient) and about each player’s exact acceptance threshold, so proposers tend to pick exchange rates that are mutually beneficial (or at least weakly beneficial) to at least one plausible partner. Given the players' low-to-moderate aggressiveness and generally conciliatory personas, most proposals are modest and focused on converting each proposer’s lower-valued holdings into higher-valued holdings for themselves while offering something that is attractive to at least one counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players will test straightforward value-arbitrage trades: convert chips they value less into chips they value more. Dog (low blue value) initiates conversions of blue -> green/red; mouse continues by buying green with blue; owl responds opportunistically to acquire blue by offering green or red. Expect quick, mutually beneficial exchanges, with owl often accepting offers that convert green/red into blue.

With updated holdings, proposers continue value-driven swaps. Dog uses excess blue to acquire red (blue->red) which is attractive to owl; mouse again sells blue to buy green (beneficial to both mouse and owl); owl opportunistically buys blue by offering red (red->blue) and dog, holding some blue, accepts when it is profitable for dog to swap blue for red.

Final-round proposals are smaller adjustments as inventories become lopsided: dog executes a tiny blue->red swap to firm up red holdings; mouse continues trading red for green to reach a green-heavy portfolio; owl finishes by buying one more blue using red. Limited inventories (e.g., dog running low on blue, owl running low on green) shape who can accept each offer.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk-neutral, inventory-constrained players share the same ordinal preferences over chip colors (RED > BLUE > GREEN) but differ slightly in the cardinal values for RED and BLUE; GREEN is common-value (0.50). Because trades must be one color for one color (possibly multiple units), mutually beneficial exchanges are only possible when the proposer has a comparatively stronger relative preference for the requested color than at least one responder. Given the priors, Alligator and Buffalo value BLUE relative to GREEN less extremely than Rabbit does (Alligator/Buffalo GREEN/BLUE = 0.5/0.7 ≈ 0.714, Rabbit = 0.5/0.6 ≈ 0.833), creating a small zone where Alligator or Buffalo can offer a package of GREEN to buy BLUE that is slightly beneficial to both them and Rabbit. Those same relative-value comparisons make the reverse profitable trades (Rabbit buying BLUE with GREEN, or anyone buying RED cheaply) unlikely to be accepted. Players track inventories closely and will not offer more than they hold; they respond privately and reject trades that reduce their own total value. Expect a small number of constrained but mutually beneficial GREEN-for-BLUE multi-unit swaps early, then primarily declined proposals as feasible win-win opportunities are exhausted and inventories become imbalanced.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in play all players still hold ample chips. Alligator (disciplined, numerate) and Buffalo (conciliatory, anchor-sensitive) can craft multi-unit GREEN-for-BLUE offers that slightly benefit themselves while also improving Rabbit's position because Rabbit values BLUE less, creating a small acceptance window. Rabbit (search-oriented) is likely to accept attractive multi-unit offers that improve her own value. After a couple of such swaps Rabbit's BLUE inventory is depleted, reducing further mutually beneficial opportunities for the rest of the game.

After the two GREEN->BLUE swaps Rabbit's BLUE holdings are low and both Alligator and Buffalo have more BLUE and reduced GREEN. Opportunities for further mutually beneficial GREEN-for-BLUE exchanges are limited because potential donors now face immediate value losses. Players who propose one-sided favorable exchanges are likely to be rejected; proposers may still test small favorable swaps but responders with higher marginal value for the requested color will decline.

With inventories now asymmetric (Alligator and Buffalo holding extra BLUE, Rabbit holding extra GREEN), most single-color-for-single-color proposals are value-destroying for at least one respondent. Given players' goals (maximize own total value) and private valuations, remaining proposals are unlikely to find a consenting partner; declining to accept unfavorable offers is the dominant response.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game pits three players with different private values for red and blue chips (greens are common-value at $0.50). Each proposer tries to improve their own total value by offering one chip color in exchange for another; responders accept only if the exchange raises their own expected value given their private values and inventories. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know opponents' private values (only their own) and because multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient. Strategic incentives push participants to trade away chips they value relatively little (e.g., buffalo values blue at $0.20 and is likely to sell blue) and to acquire chips they value highly (e.g., goose values red at $0.90). Players with indifferent valuations (bear values red and blue at $0.50) are the most flexible acceptors. Given the personas, expect cautious probing offers early, opportunistic acceptance by the player who stands to gain clearly from an offered exchange (often buffalo when being offered red in exchange for blue), and occasional multi-accept situations when an exchange is mutually profitable for more than one responder. Inventory constraints (chips sold cannot exceed current holdings) will modestly shape later-turn offers as players run down particular colors (notably buffalo's blue supply). Overall, the game is driven by private-value asymmetries (goose and buffalo prefer red most; buffalo dislikes blue), patience that favors searching for favorable terms across rounds, and some randomness where multiple acceptances occur.

rounds.round_explanation: Early probing: proposers test whether opponents will part with red (the highest private value for goose and buffalo) by offering equal-value greens or lower-value blues. Expect declined initial probes and one clear mutually beneficial exchange where a proposer offers red in return for blue (buffalo prefers to receive red and is willing to give blue). Inventory constraints are not yet binding.

With early information and modest inventory shifts, proposers press favorable quantity ratios. Goose (high red value) offers extra blues for reds to accumulate red. Bear capitalizes by offering red-for-blue packages that attract buffalo (who values red more than blue). Buffalo, running down blue but still opportunistic, later offers bundles that are jointly profitable, producing a multi-accept where recipient selection is random.

Late-round adjustments reflect updated inventories: buffalo's blue is nearly exhausted, making some prior deal types infeasible and increasing the value of remaining blues to them. Proposals become more targeted: bear again recruits buffalo to trade a last blue for a red, and buffalo finishes by offering a modest red-for-blue swap that goose is willing to accept. Expect fewer declines and more inventory-driven trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical inventories (10 of each color) but different private values: everyone values RED most (peacock 0.80, otter 0.70, cat 0.50), GREEN is common-value (0.50), and BLUE is least valuable for all (peacock/0.50, cat/0.30, otter/0.20). Given these priors, bargaining dynamics are driven by repeated attempts to convert low-value BLUE (and occasionally GREEN) into higher-value RED. Proposers will offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value more; acceptors decide based only on their private valuations and current holdings. Uncertainty comes from (1) not knowing others' private valuations and (2) the rule that if multiple others accept, the actual recipient is chosen at random — so proposers who make broadly attractive terms may attract competition but cannot pick the partner. Inventory constraints (cannot offer more than held) and the three-turn-per-round schedule create incentives for moderately generous offers early (to secure trades) and some opportunistic repetition of profitable exchange rates (e.g., offering 2 BLUE for 1 RED) because that ratio tends to be attractive to at least one counterpart given their values. Conciliatory players (cat, otter) are slightly more willing to accept small gains; peacock, with moderate exploration and exploitation caution, tends to propose offers that are mutually positive but somewhat efficient for herself.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers try simple, safe exchange rates that are likely to be mutually beneficial given private values. Because RED is universally more valuable, early proposals primarily offer BLUE in exchange for RED. Proposers often pick quantities (e.g., 2 BLUE for 1 RED) that produce clear positive net gains for at least one counterparty, ensuring execution even when only one acceptor is needed.

Mid-game: players have observed the first round's pattern and adjust. Cat, who shows disciplined bookkeeping and a conciliatory style, repeats a previously successful small trade (2 BLUE for 1 RED) targeted to peacock; peacock reciprocates with similarly efficient offers. Otter continues to attempt the same profitable ratio when it benefits her, and multiple acceptances remain possible, creating occasional randomness in who becomes the recipient.

Final round: players exploit the established exchange rate patterns while occasionally using GREEN as an alternative token when advantageous. Cat uses a GREEN-for-BLUE offer to target otter (who is relatively anchor-sensitive and willing to accept small gains for BLUE), and otter finishes with another BLUE-to-RED conversion. Because the game ends after this round, proposers may prioritize securing positive trades rather than testing new ratios.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations: all players value GREEN at $0.50 (common value), but RED and BLUE differ by player. Squid highly values BLUE ($1.00) and dislikes RED ($0.10). Badger values RED and BLUE equally and highly ($1.00 each). Camel sits between them (BLUE $0.80, RED $0.40). Proposers will tend to offer chips they privately value least and request chips they value most; recipients accept only when the exchange increases their own total value. Because accept/reject responses are private and multiple acceptances trigger a random recipient selection, proposers sometimes offer terms that create positive-sum opportunities for several players and then face random allocation risk. Expect early probing offers (cheap-to-propose, attractive-to-some trades), opportunistic conversions of low-value RED into high-value BLUE by offerers who hold many RED, and repeated small-value trades that exploit valuation asymmetries (e.g., squid selling RED to obtain GREEN or BLUE, badger selling BLUE to accumulate RED, camel occasionally creating neutral/near-neutral exchanges). Uncertainty arises from private values (players only know their own valuations) and from random selection when multiple players accept, so proposers trade off more generous terms to increase acceptance probability against retaining surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 functions as initial probing. Squid opens with an aggressive ask for BLUE using cheap RED as currency; recipients decline because it's strongly unfavorable to them. Badger then proposes a favorable swap (offer GREEN for RED) that appeals to a RED-preferring recipient (squid), producing the first executed trade and shifting inventories. Camel follows by converting excess RED into BLUE with a one-for-two RED->BLUE proposal that is attractive to badger (who values RED highly), so camel sells RED to accumulate BLUE.

With inventories changed, Round 1 shows more targeted, mutually beneficial swaps. Squid leverages his low RED value to buy GREEN (a small profitable conversion) and badger accepts because gaining RED for GREEN is profitable for him. Badger then makes a leverage play: selling BLUE to acquire RED (using BLUE as currency to increase RED holdings). The offer is attractive enough that both remaining players accept; random selection awards the trade to squid. Camel then repeats a RED->BLUE conversion, using extra RED to obtain BLUE from badger, who benefits from receiving RED.

In the final round players exploit the inventory asymmetries created earlier. Squid again trades away low-value RED for GREEN, which badger accepts to gain RED. Badger then offers BLUE for RED (selling a scarce BLUE to acquire RED) in a way that looks attractive to both others; both accept, and random selection grants the trade to camel. Camel finishes by converting remaining RED into BLUE with another two-for-one RED->BLUE offer that badger accepts, leaving badger with a large RED holding and camel with additional BLUE.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 of RED, BLUE, and GREEN. GREEN has a common value ($0.50); RED and BLUE have different private values per player. Players maximize their own chip-value. Across three rounds (three proposer turns per round) proposers will try to trade away chips they value less to obtain chips they value more; responders will accept only when the trade increases their personal total value (or is sufficiently attractive given uncertainty about who will be chosen if multiple accept). Ram (highly exploratory, moderately patient, values RED most) will repeatedly try to convert lower-valued chips into RED. Dog (conciliatory, values GREEN most) will frequently accept or propose BLUE-for-GREEN trades because those are large gains for dog. Butterfly (bookkeeping-disciplined, quick-deal oriented, values RED fairly highly) will also aim to accumulate RED but will often trade GREEN when counterparts with low RED value (Dog) are available. Uncertainty arises when multiple responders could accept a proposal; proposers who gain regardless of which partner is selected (they receive requested chips and give the same offered chips) are willing to make offers that multiple players will accept. Over the game we expect a number of small 1-for-1 trades: GREEN is often used as a margin (since it is common-value and intermediate for many), DOG will frequently be the trading partner when RED is requested in exchange for GREEN, and Ram will steadily accumulate RED by offering GREEN or BLUE to players who value RED less.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round exploration: Ram opens by trying to convert a BLUE into a RED (he values RED most). Dog, who values RED modestly more than BLUE, and Butterfly, who also prefers RED to BLUE, are both likely to accept a 1:1 BLUE-for-RED. Dog then proposes a BLUE-for-GREEN trade (favors dog); Ram, who values BLUE more than GREEN, accepts to improve his holding. Butterfly finishes the round by buying a RED with GREEN from Dog (Dog's RED value is low so he accepts). These moves reflect Ram's search for RED, Dog's appetite for GREEN, and Butterfly's opportunistic RED accumulation when trading with Dog.

After initial exchanges, Ram continues to seek RED and trades GREEN for RED with Dog (Dog still values GREEN more than RED). Dog persists in acquiring GREEN by offering BLUE; Ram accepts when receiving BLUE in exchange for GREEN because that increases Ram's value. Butterfly again targets RED by offering GREEN; Dog, who has low RED value, accepts. The pattern is repeatable 1:1 exchanges where Dog consistently trades away low-value RED to obtain GREEN, while Ram and Butterfly exploit that gap to acquire RED.

Final-round completion: Ram continues the same pattern and extracts another RED by offering GREEN; Dog again accepts because the swap benefits him. Dog then uses his BLUE to obtain more GREEN (Ram accepts because he prefers BLUE to keeping that GREEN). Butterfly uses a GREEN-for-RED offer to secure one more RED from Dog. Across rounds Dog ends up accumulating GREEN while Ram and Butterfly accumulate RED, consistent with their private valuations and players' conciliatory/quick-deal tendencies.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game centers on a common-value green chip (0.50 to everyone) and heterogeneous private values for red and blue. Iguana and Alligator both value BLUE highly (0.70) while Lion values RED and BLUE low (0.20) and likes GREEN (0.50). Given players maximize their own chip value, trades will tend to move GREEN toward players who value it more (Lion) and BLUE toward those who value it more (Iguana, Alligator). Because players only know their own values, proposals are constructed to look attractive to likely sellers (e.g., offering multiple low-value reds to buy a blue) or buyers (offering blue to buy green). Uncertainty about others' private values and the random resolution when multiple players accept means proposers favor terms that create positive-sum gains for potential counterparties (so they accept) while still improving the proposer’s own value. Inventory constraints (cannot sell more than held) and the three-turn structure favor early exploration by patient/search-oriented Lion and repeated targeted blue-acquisition by Iguana and Alligator. Acceptance behavior reflects personas: Lion is patient and exploratory (sometimes declines indifferent deals), Iguana is opportunistic for blue (often accepts favorable swaps), and Alligator is cautious but will accept clear value gains. When two players would accept, the recipient is selected at random, adding probabilistic risk to proposers' partner outcomes.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players reveal initial preferences via simple, value-improving swaps. Lion (patient, search-focused) tries an aggressive green-for-red request that others decline. Iguana quickly targets blue by offering green (blue is her highest-valued chip) and succeeds in getting a blue from Lion. Alligator then competes for blue using red as currency (offering multiple low-value reds for one blue), creating a situation where more than one responder finds it attractive; the recipient is randomly selected.

Midgame: holdings are now imbalanced (Iguana richer in reds, Alligator richer in blues). Lion exploits his ability to attract sellers by offering blue for green (giving low-value blue to get green). Iguana continues to accumulate blue by offering green, targeting Lion as a willing seller. Alligator again uses red-for-blue bundles to secure additional blue, favoring terms that produce clear gains for potential donors.

Final round: players make last-value-improvement moves. Lion repeatedly uses low-value blue as a lever to pick up green. Iguana continues targeted blue purchases, often from Lion. Alligator again bids with low-value reds for extra blues; because multiple counterparts often find these terms attractive, random selection determines some final partnerships. With the end approaching, players accept clear, immediate value gains rather than hold out for uncertain future deals.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose bilateral chip swaps in a constrained three-round, three-turn-per-round sequence. Each proposer asks for chips of one color and offers chips of a different color; non-senders accept or decline privately. All players aim to maximize their own portfolio value given private per-chip valuations (green = 0.50 for all; red/blue vary). This creates clear directional incentives: players with low-value chips (blue or cheap red) tend to offer those to acquire green, while the red-loving player (whale-red-86) often sells lower-value chips to accumulate red. Uncertainty comes from private values and the random selection rule when multiple players accept; proposals therefore try to be individually profitable for the proposer while being attractive enough to at least one counterparty. Inventory constraints (cannot offer more than held) and modest acceptance thresholds (players vary from receptive to cautious) shape modest, incremental trades rather than extreme leverage plays. Observed behavior in the forecast reflects (a) frequent proposer attempts to convert low-value chips into green, (b) whale's opportunistic accumulation of red, and (c) duck's consistent desire to gain green. When multiple players would benefit from the same offer, the forecast resolves the random recipient by naming one plausible realized recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial distributions are equal (10 of each color). Early proposals target straightforward improvements: convert lower-value types into green (the common, high-value chip). Proposers make modest exchanges that are strictly profitable for themselves; acceptances occur only when the recipient's private value increases. Because players don't know others' private values at the table, proposals are conservative (small quantities) and intended to be appealing to at least one likely counterparty.

Holdings have shifted: whale increased red, duck gained some green, dolphin accumulated a couple red from an earlier acceptance. Players now optimize with the updated inventories. Dolphin continues to convert low-value blue to green in small lots; whale exploits blue-to-red swaps to build red; duck focuses on small, efficient trades to increase green holdings. Acceptance patterns depend on immediate private gains rather than group efficiency.

Endgame incentives matter: with limited remaining rounds, proposers push small, clearly value-improving swaps. Dolphin still attempts to upgrade blue to green but faces partners who are unwilling to give up green cheaply. Whale continues to harvest red by offering green or buying red when profitable. Duck persistently trades low-value types for green. Random resolution when multiple acceptors can still allocate trades to any accepting partner, which affects final allocations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private chip values that create clear trading motives: zebra (high value on RED) will repeatedly try to acquire RED chips, turtle (high value on BLUE) will try to convert low-value RED into BLUE, and gorilla (prefers GREEN and BLUE over RED) will often trade away RED to pick up BLUE or GREEN. Because green has a common known value and red/blue values differ by player, proposals that move low-valued chips for a recipient into higher-valued chips for the proposer will be attractive to particular partners. Uncertainty comes from two sources: (1) each player knows only their own private values and must infer whether a proposed swap helps a potential partner, and (2) when both non-senders accept a proposal the actual recipient is chosen at random, so proposers cannot target a specific partner directly. Personas bias behavior: zebra and gorilla are relatively conciliatory and likely to accept modest positive gains, turtle is quick-deal oriented and often accepts straightforward, clearly profitable swaps. Expect frequent one-for-one or small multi-unit swaps that convert each player's low-value chips into higher-value ones, with many trades executed early because turtle's low search/quick-deal tendency and both conciliatory tendencies of zebra/gorilla lower holdout rates.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players have symmetric initial inventories (10 of each chip). Zebra will open trying to get RED (their top value) by offering a small amount of a chip others might accept; gorilla will try to convert red into blue since gorilla prefers blue/green to red; turtle will trade cheaply away RED to pick up blue. Early trades are relatively generous to entice acceptance because proposers cannot pick which acceptor is realized.

After initial exchanges inventories diverge (some players now hold more of their preferred chips). Zebra continues to aim for RED, offering green when that is efficient; gorilla continues converting remaining RED into BLUE; turtle stays dispositionally willing to accept offers that make immediate gains. Proposers tailor quantities to be attractive to the likely recipient given holdings and the private-value structure.

Final round: inventories reflect earlier swaps (zebra has accumulated RED, turtle many BLUE, gorilla trimmed RED). Proposers make smaller, precise trades to marginally improve their portfolio value. Turtle's low search and quick-deal orientation makes them an easy partner for modestly favorable swaps; zebra and gorilla accept only when the immediate value change is non-negative or slightly positive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn forecast emphasizes motivated, inventory-constrained swapping driven by each player's private chip values: iguana strongly prefers RED, zebra strongly prefers BLUE, and cat prefers BLUE but is numerically disciplined and quick to secure profitable one-for-one swaps. Greens are common-value (0.50) and therefore serve as a convenient bargaining medium to move value when direct RED↔BLUE swaps would be costly for the counterparty. Uncertainty enters because each responder only knows their own values and must decide whether a proposed swap improves their own portfolio; proposers exploit likely value differences (e.g., offering GREEN to acquire BLUE from a BLUE-preferred player, or offering RED to obtain BLUE when the recipient values BLUE highly). Persona priors shape behavior: cat tends to make low-search, bookkeeping-consistent proposals that are immediately attractive to at least one partner; iguana repeatedly targets RED but uses minimally costly offers (GREEN→RED) to entice an indifferent or slightly-positive counterparty; zebra steadily trades RED for BLUE when it yields a clear personal gain. Given the three-player setting, proposers often craft simple 1-for-1 exchanges that create a clear positive payoff for at least one other player, and ties (multiple accepts) are avoided in this forecast by making offers that are attractive to a single counterpart. The result is a sequence of small, mutually beneficial swaps that increase each player's holdings of their preferred chip over the game while respecting inventory constraints and private-value uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening exploration: proposers test who values which chip. Cat opens by targeting BLUE (cat values BLUE > others), offering RED (which zebra values less). Iguana then tries a conservative GREEN→RED request to pick up RED cheaply. Zebra finishes the round by offering RED to obtain BLUE from the player who still gains from the swap.

Players respond to the updated holdings. Cat continues with low-search, high-bookkeeping moves that are attractive to a BLUE-weak player (offers GREEN to get BLUE). Iguana again uses GREEN→RED swaps to accumulate RED without overpaying. Zebra persistently converts RED into BLUE via simple 1-for-1 proposals that are appealing to cat.

Endgame rebalancing: cat extracts additional BLUE by offering GREEN (attractive to iguana, who values GREEN more than BLUE). Iguana takes one last GREEN→RED swap to further increase RED holdings. Zebra finishes by converting a remaining RED into BLUE via a straightforward 1-for-1 offer that cat accepts for a small gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose single-color-for-single-color one-for-one style trades over three rounds. All players start with equal inventories (10 of each color) but hold different private valuations: bear values BLUE highest (1.00) then RED (0.70) then GREEN (0.50); swan values BLUE high (0.90) then GREEN/RED (0.50); peacock values RED (0.60) slightly above GREEN (0.50) and much less for BLUE (0.40). Given these priors, the dominant bargaining dynamic is repeated, small profitable swaps that move BLUE toward the two high-BLUE players (bear and swan) and RED/ GREEN toward the player who puts lower marginal value on BLUE (peacock). Proposers typically offer a low-value chip (from their perspective) in exchange for a higher-value chip; recipients accept if the exchange raises their own private total value. Uncertainty arises when both non-senders would gain from the same proposal — then random selection determines the realized partner — and from private-value asymmetries that mean a deal that is obviously attractive to the observer may be rejected by a high-value holder. Behavioral priors (bear: moderately patient and numerate; peacock: conciliatory and search-oriented; swan: search-oriented and patient) encourage repeated small (1-for-1) proposals rather than large multi-chip gambits. The forecast therefore expects a sequence of one-for-one trades in each turn, mostly exchanging RED or GREEN for BLUE; acceptance decisions follow private-value comparisons, with random tie-breaking if multiple players accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple 1-for-1 swaps. Bear (who values BLUE most strongly) will offer RED to acquire BLUE; peacock (who values BLUE least) is the most willing BLUE seller and thus likely recipient. Peacock as proposer then uses his relatively low BLUE valuation to buy RED from a higher-BLUE holder; both bear and swan would find such a swap attractive, so a random acceptance among them is possible. Swan finishes the round by trying to buy BLUE using GREEN, which is attractive to peacock (BLUE low for him) but not to bear.

Mid game: patterns established. Bear continues to trade his RED for BLUE one-for-one because BLUE yields him the largest marginal return and peacock remains the cheapest BLUE source. Peacock repeatedly converts surplus BLUE into RED (and gains RED stock) because it improves his private value. Swan continues to acquire BLUE by offering GREEN; peacock often accepts those swaps because BLUE is his weakest color. Multiple-accept events remain possible when both non-senders would gain, producing random partner selection.

End game: inventories have shifted so bear and swan hold more BLUE and peacock holds more RED. Proposals remain conservative 1-for-1 exchanges. Bear continues buying BLUE with RED from peacock; peacock continues to offer BLUE for RED to rebuild RED stock; swan makes a final attempt to pick up an extra RED by offering BLUE. Acceptance decisions follow private-value gains; peacock is the frequent BLUE supplier and bear the frequent BLUE acquirer.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player chip bargaining with private values creates repeated, inventory-constrained trades where proposers seek partners who value the offered color relatively more than the requested color. Green is common-value (0.5 each) and functions as a neutral medium; red and blue create complementarities (gorilla and monkey highly value red; monkey and camel value blue). Proposers therefore try to buy a color that others undervalue relative to what the proposer will give. Uncertainty stems from private valuations and from not being able to choose the accepting partner when multiple players accept. Across three rounds (each with three proposers), we expect proposers to craft offers that (a) exploit opponents who place lower marginal value on the requested chips and (b) avoid offers that make the proposer worse off. Because players are loss-averse and inventory-aware (bookkeeping priors), many trades will involve moving low-value chips for higher-value chips for the proposer, and declined proposals occur when no counterparty stands to gain. Random selection matters when multiple players would benefit from the same deal.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage exploration and exploitation: proposers identify opponents who value the requested color less than the offered color. Gorilla (patient, prefers large gains) targets camel's low red valuation by offering blue for red. Camel then offers an aggressive bundle to acquire blue (which he values more than red) and both accept; monkey selectively accepts trades that raise his own value.

Mid-game recalibration: inventories have shifted (camel low on red), so some previously profitable trades are infeasible. Gorilla again targets camel's remaining red with a small, high‑gain swap. Camel and monkey face limited profitable buying opportunities and attempt an ambitious buy of red that is declined because potential partners cannot or will not give red at that price.

Endgame trades favor exchanges that are mutually beneficial given current inventories. Gorilla purchases green (a stable medium) by offering blue; both other players could benefit so multiple acceptances occur and a random accepting player is selected. Camel then trades green for blue with gorilla (who accepts because blue is relatively low value for gorilla). Monkey finds limited personally profitable opportunities and a small final proposal fails.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red and blue chips (green is common-value). Each player tries to maximize their own total chip value; proposers offer to swap a positive quantity of one color for a different color. Because blue is the highest-valued color for all players (especially otter and tiger) and red has very different private values (very low for alligator, moderate for otter, high for tiger), we expect repeated patterns: players with many reds will try to convert them into blues, and players who strongly prefer green over red will accept trades that exchange red for green. The persona priors predict relatively low initial aggressiveness across players, but otter’s higher search/anchor sensitivity and tiger/alligator’s bookkeeping discipline make multi-turn exploitation possible: proposers will typically offer small bundles that are profitable to them but sometimes attractive to a single counterparty (leading to single-acceptor trades). When a proposal gives recipients a clear private-value improvement (for example, exchanging someone’s red for green, or offering multiple reds for a single blue), multiple acceptances can occur and the recipient is then randomly selected. Uncertainty arises because each responder only knows their own private values; however, observable history (who accepted previous trades) helps infer which players are willing to accept particular exchange rates. Overall, the forecast emphasizes repeated small, inventory-constrained swaps in which tiger often accepts generous red-for-blue or red-for-green offers, otter accepts when the exchange clears her higher anchor for blue, and alligator tends to sell low-value red and accept green when clearly beneficial.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple, inventory-light swaps. Alligator and Otter are likely to try turning low-valued red into blue (they offer multiple reds for a single blue). Tiger, who values blue highly and red moderately, is the natural acceptor of those offers. Toward the end of the round tiger tests buying red by offering green; because green is strictly better than red for the other two players, this often yields multiple acceptances and a randomly-selected recipient.

Middle round: patterns that worked in round 0 are repeated. Alligator and Otter continue to try trading low-valued red for blue; Tiger, now holding more red, repeatedly accepts those offers. Tiger continues to use green-for-red proposals as a way to extract surplus—these are attractive to both non-senders because green > red for them, so multiple acceptances appear occasionally.

Final round: players double down on profitable, previously-tested swap rates. Alligator and Otter again offer small bundles of reds for a single blue and Tiger, with a large red stock, continues to accept. For variety in final play, Tiger makes a larger red-for-blue offer that is generous enough to attract both non-senders; the recipient is randomly selected, concluding the game with modest redistribution toward the proposer’s favored holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on trades of three chip colors where GREEN is a common high-value chip for everyone (0.50) while RED and BLUE values differ by player. Dolphin strongly prefers GREEN and treats RED/BLUE as low-value tokens; Frog highly values RED and values BLUE equal to GREEN; Pig values GREEN highest, RED slightly above BLUE. Given private valuations, proposers will offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value more. Because acceptance decisions are private and proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, offers tend to be conservative: proposers craft terms that are likely to attract at least one counterparty while minimizing their own cost. Uncertainty arises from not knowing others' private values and from the random selection when multiple accept. Behaviorally, Dolphin (search-oriented, moderate acceptance threshold) will pursue GREEN-rich swaps and test the market; Frog (conciliatory, low-search) will often trade BLUE for RED; Pig (low-search, mixed thresholds) will accept small positive gains and occasionally trade BLUE for GREEN. Early rounds see small one-for-one swaps that create inventory asymmetries; later proposals exploit those inventories (e.g., Frog leveraging excess BLUE to acquire RED). Overall, accepted trades are those that generate clear, private-value gains for the recipient; neutral or negative-value offers are typically declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves. Proposers test the waters: Dolphin seeks GREEN by offering low-value RED; Frog uses BLUE to acquire RED (a clear private gain for a recipient with lower RED value); Pig tries to convert extra BLUE into GREEN. Recipients accept only when the swap increases their private total value or is neutral and acceptable under their persona.

Middle round. Inventories shifted: Frog accumulated REDs and Pig gained BLUEs earlier. Dolphin continues to prioritize GREEN and offers low-value chips for it; Frog keeps trading BLUE for RED at one-for-one terms; Pig opportunistically converts BLUE into RED when profitable. Acceptances occur when recipients privately gain (or are indifferent and conciliatory).

Final round. Players exploit the inventories created earlier. Dolphin again trades a low-value chip for GREEN and secures a trade with the conciliatory Frog.
