Frog makes a larger-value play: selling a GREEN to acquire two REDs (high private gain), which is attractive to Dolphin (who benefits modestly by giving REDs for GREEN). Pig finishes by buying an extra GREEN with BLUE, accepting a modest private gain when Frog, who values BLUE and GREEN equally, agrees.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin symmetric endowments but hold different private values for red and blue chips; green chips are common-value. Each proposer will typically try to convert low-value holdings into higher-value ones for themselves, while recipients decide based only on their private valuations. This creates repeated asymmetric offers where proposers who value green most (iguana) tend to request green and offer blue or red, and players who value red highly (owl, squid) may trade blue for red when offered sufficiently many blues. Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot pick the partner when multiple players accept and each responder only knows their own values; this makes proposers sometimes overpay (offer extra chips) to attract at least one acceptor. Behavioral priors (iguana: disciplined, patient; owl: closure-seeking; squid: conciliatory) make iguana propose value-increasing but moderately generous deals, owl propose simpler fair-looking exchanges to secure a quick acceptance, and squid often accept modestly positive exchanges. Over three rounds we expect a sequence of small, value-improving one-for-one or two-for-one swaps where iguana steadily accumulates greens, owl and squid opportunistically swap blues and reds when beneficial, and many deals are bilateral (exactly one accept) though occasionally multiple acceptances are possible and resolved randomly.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test the market. Iguana, who privately values green highest, opens with an offer that trades away low-value blue for green hoping someone will accept. Owl prefers quick, decisive offers and will attempt a compact blue-for-red exchange that looks attractive to at least one partner. Squid is conciliatory and will propose trades that raise their own value but also are acceptable to iguana.

Mid game: players have observed profitable swaps. Iguana continues to target greens and may pay extra blues to attract a partner. Owl, preferring closure, again posts a blue-for-red rate that yields small gains for either responder. Squid looks for straightforward value increases and will reproduce the red-for-green exchange if it benefits them.

Endgame: inventories have shifted and players refine offers. Iguana still seeks greens and posts straightforward one-for-one swaps that many find acceptable. Owl repeats the pattern of offering two blues for a red to secure incremental gains, and squid again buys reds using greens when that increases their private payoff. Because responders act simultaneously and only know their own valuations, proposers sometimes make slightly generous offers to ensure at least one acceptance.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly negotiate pairwise chip swaps (each proposal requests one chip color and offers a different one). Private per-chip values create predictable directional incentives: gorilla (high RED value) repeatedly tries to accumulate RED by offering BLUE; llama (high BLUE value) repeatedly sells RED to acquire BLUE; whale (prefers RED over GREEN and especially over BLUE) also tries to convert BLUE into RED when profitable. Because GREEN has identical middle value for everyone, it plays little role. Uncertainty arises from (1) which non-sender will accept when more than one would benefit (resolved here by random selection), and (2) limited inventories that make later trades constrained (llama gradually runs low on RED). Given personas, gorilla and whale push for value-improving RED purchases by offering lower-valued BLUE; llama, anchor-sensitive and Blue-preferring, sells RED for BLUE as a quick, repeatable way to increase her highest-valued chip. Acceptance decisions follow private-value gains: recipients accept only when their own chip-value change is non-negative and consistent with their acceptance thresholds. Over three rounds this produces a series of small, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 (and one 2-for-2) swaps, mostly transferring RED toward gorilla/whale and BLUE toward llama, until llama's RED stock is nearly exhausted.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round sorting: gorilla opens by offering BLUE for RED (she values RED most). Llama, who values BLUE most, accepts because trading RED for BLUE raises her value. Llama then offers a larger RED->BLUE trade (2-for-2) to get more BLUE; both other players would gain from that exchange and multiple accepts occur, so a random recipient is chosen (gorilla). Whale follows by converting a BLUE into RED from the now-RED-light llama when that is beneficial for whale.

Reinforcing pattern: gorilla again offers BLUE for RED and gets RED from llama; llama keeps selling isolated RED for BLUE in small 1-for-1 trades that increase her high-value BLUE holdings (one such offer is accepted by whale this round via random selection among beneficiaries); whale continues buying RED when he can trade his low-valued BLUE to a BLUE-preferring recipient.

Endgame pressure and inventory effects: gorilla continues to purchase RED one-for-one from llama (who has accumulated a large BLUE stock and dwindling RED), llama keeps executing small RED->BLUE swaps when profitable, and whale takes any remaining opportunities to convert BLUE into RED. Llama's RED holdings are drained by repeated sales, reflecting her preference for BLUE and the others' willingness to buy RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal chip counts but heterogeneous private values for red and blue (green is common-value at $0.50). Proposers can trade one color for another; because greens are the cheapest common asset, most feasible trades involve the proposer offering green (or asking for green) to shift high-value red/blue chips toward themselves. Each responder decides only on private payoff change (gain in their own valuation minus loss), so proposals that look profitable to the proposer must be generous enough to create private gains for at least one counterparty. Uncertainty enters because each player sees only their own valuations; observers infer likely proposals that trade low-value tokens (green or blue when the recipient values blue low) for high-value tokens (red or blue to the proposer) and likely acceptances where the recipient's private valuation implies a positive (or at least nonnegative) net gain. Random selection when multiple players accept creates risk for proposers who cannot choose the recipient, so proposers tend to make offers that secure a single likely acceptor rather than overpay to attract both. Using the provided personas, duck (disciplined, low-search) will propose straightforward, bookkeeping-friendly swaps, lion (moderately patient/cautious) will aim for trades that produce modest gains and avoid overpaying, and tiger (low-search, fairly disciplined) will trade aggressively for red when he can exploit others' low valuation of blue.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round play: proposers try to convert low-value greens or other players' low-value blues into tokens they privately value highly. Duck leads with a modestly generous green-for-red offer intended to entice lion; lion follows by buying blue from the player who values blue least (tiger); tiger ends the round offering a blue-heavy package to buy red, large enough to be worthwhile for both potential sellers, producing competing accepts and a random recipient.

Mid-game: holdings have shifted so tiger's green stock is larger and his blue stock smaller. Duck continues with safe, bookkeeping-friendly trades (green for blue) that are attractive to tiger (who values blue low), lion opportunistically buys blue from tiger with a small green outlay, and tiger pursues red again by offering greens in a package that yields a small gain to the seller with the highest marginal willingness to part with red.

Endgame: with inventories more skewed, duck favors small, low-friction swaps to lock in value; lion attempts a standard green-for-red request but faces no private-gain responses and the offer fails; tiger makes a last attempt to secure a red by offering greens at a rate attractive to lion, producing one final accepted trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player chip bargaining where each player starts with 10 of each color unfolds as repeated, inventory-constrained bilateral offers. All players value GREEN identically (0.50), while RED and BLUE values differ: bear strongly prefers BLUE (1.00) and dislikes RED (0.20); goose values RED and BLUE equally (0.80) and is the most conciliatory; ram favors BLUE (0.80) over RED (0.50). Given these priors, proposers who want BLUE will typically offer RED (cheap to bear, moderate to ram) or GREEN (neutral) to attract partners. Conciliatory goose often accepts neutral or slightly favorable swaps; ram accepts deals that improve his own value and crafts simple, bookkeeping-friendly proposals; bear pushes for favorable BLUE-for-RED swaps but is somewhat patient. Uncertainty arises because acceptances are private and when multiple players accept the proposer cannot choose the recipient (random selection). This makes proposers balance generosity (to attract at least one acceptor) against preserving inventory/value, and makes players with low acceptance thresholds (goose, ram) sometimes accept deals that mostly benefit the proposer if their own payoff is nonnegative.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers probe with simple 1-for-1 swaps. Bear, who most wants BLUE, tests a cheap RED->BLUE swap that looks attractive to both others. Goose tends to propose deals that are neutral to herself but attractive to others to secure a trade early. Ram looks for straightforward beneficial swaps (RED->BLUE) that increase his BLUE holdings.

After initial reallocations, proposers repeat the pattern: bear again asks for BLUE offering RED (targeting goose who is accepting), goose proposes a BLUE->RED swap that is attractive to potential recipients, and ram continues to buy BLUE by offering RED when counterparts have incentives to accept.

Endgame behavior: players continue small 1-for-1 swaps. Bear persistently targets BLUE with low-cost RED offers that goose, with low acceptance thresholds, often takes; goose offers BLUE-for-RED swaps that make others better off and occasionally gets matched to bear; ram continues simple RED->BLUE trades that raise his BLUE holdings when goose is willing to give BLUE.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or unequal-quantity) chip swaps across three rounds. Each player privately values BLUE, GREEN, and RED differently: ram values BLUE highest (1.00), lion moderately (0.60), and octopus very low (0.10); all value GREEN at 0.50; RED sits between for players (lion 0.50, octopus 0.20, ram 0.30). Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot name the recipient and acceptance is simultaneous and private; players rely on simple inventory-aware trades (e.g., swapping low-value blue from octopus for red or green) and are constrained by immediate holdings. Over the three rounds we forecast repeated small, inventory-aware swaps where octopus routinely sells BLUE (low value to him) for RED or GREEN, ram aggressively seeks BLUE by offering RED, and lion interleaves modest purchases of BLUE using RED or GREEN while being relatively willing to accept small gains when offered.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round probing and straightforward value-improving swaps. Lion opens by targeting octopus's low-valued BLUE, octopus then trades BLUE for RED (profitable for him), and ram uses RED to pick up additional BLUE. Multiple acceptances occur when both non-senders benefit from the proposed swap, leading to random selection of the recipient.

Players respond to updated inventories. Lion again targets BLUE, this time using GREEN as the offering chip (small concession) and succeeds with octopus. Octopus continues converting his low-value BLUE into RED via proposals that both other players find acceptable; randomization chooses lion in that turn. Ram stays focused on accumulating BLUE by offering RED, and octopus accepts when it improves his own holdings.

Final-round fine-tuning. Lion again purchases BLUE from octopus when the exchange is value-improving for octopus. Octopus briefly pivots to buy GREEN (high common value) by selling BLUE; both others find this attractive and ram is randomly selected as recipient. Ram finishes by buying one more BLUE from octopus, consolidating his stock of high-value BLUE.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with identical inventories (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue (green common-value at $0.50). Buffalo strongly prefers red and is willing to give up blue; Duck strongly prefers blue and is willing to give up red or green when profitable; Cat has intermediate preferences and often trades away lower-value blue to acquire higher-value green or red. Proposers ask for a color they value more and offer a color they value less; responders accept if the expected private-value change is positive (accounting for random selection if both accept). Uncertainty arises from (a) private valuations unknown to others, (b) the risk of competing acceptances (random partner selection), and (c) limited inventory making future proposals path-dependent. Early rounds feature targeted 1-for-1 or 1-for-2 exchanges that exploit asymmetries (e.g., buffalo offering blue for red to duck), and later rounds retarget holdings left after earlier trades. Because players maximize their own chip-value rather than group surplus, many proposals are opportunistic and accepted only by players who gain immediate private value.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are symmetric but private values differ. Buffalo (high red value) will aggressively offer low-value blue to acquire red; Cat will look to upgrade low-value blue into higher-value green; Duck (high blue value) will opportunistically sell red or green when it yields positive net value in blue. Buffalo continues to trade its low-value blue for red; Cat still prefers swapping blue for green; Duck seeks additional blue by offering red when the exchange yields a net gain to a responder.

Final round trading focuses on converting remaining low-value colors into preferred colors given updated inventories. Buffalo continues exchanging blue for red; Cat again trades blue for green when profitable; Duck makes a final attempt to acquire additional blue by offering green, which yields immediate positive gains to potential sellers of blue. Competitive acceptance (both responders accepting) produces random partner selection in at least one turn.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories (10 of each color) but very different private values for red and blue; green is a common-value chip. The bargaining dynamic will therefore center on transfers that move blue toward squid (who values blue highly) and red toward kangaroo (who values red highly), while butterfly patiently trades away low-value blue to accumulate green. Proposers will offer colors they personally value less and request colors they value more; recipients accept when their private value gain (value received minus value given) is positive. Because acceptances are simultaneous and the proposer cannot pick among multiple acceptors, many profitable offers attract multiple accepts and lead to random partner selection. Uncertainty arises from three sources: private valuations (each player only knows their own values), stochastic resolution when multiple accept, and modest heterogeneity in acceptance thresholds (players have mixed willingness to accept marginal deals). Given the personas, butterfly tends to make modest, structured offers and is tolerant of unequal outcomes; kangaroo searches for deals that increase red holdings; squid is anchor-sensitive but will eagerly accept trades that increase blue rather than green or red. Across three rounds (nine proposal turns), I forecast repeated small exchanges: butterfly sells low-value blue to buy green (accepted mainly by squid), kangaroo offers blue for red (accepted by squid), and squid repeatedly offers red for blue to accumulate blue (often accepted by the other two, with random partner selection when both accept).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple, mutually beneficial 1-2 chip swaps. Butterfly (low blue value) tries to buy green by offering blue; squid (high blue value) is the natural supplier of green in exchange for blue and accepts. Kangaroo pushes to buy red by offering blue; squid again accepts because blue > red for squid. Squid then attempts a reciprocal exchange (selling red for blue) that is attractive to both others, producing multiple accepts and a random selection of the recipient.

Players continue with the same pattern but scale down to 1-chip offers to conserve inventory. Butterfly again sells blue to buy green; kangaroo again targets red by offering blue; squid repeats buying blue with small red offers. Most offers are individually attractive to squid (who values blue highly), so squid frequently accepts. When squid proposes to buy blue, both other players typically accept, and the trade partner is determined randomly.

Final round: players make low-risk, high-gain marginal trades. Butterfly focuses on converting remaining low-value blue into green. Kangaroo continues to accumulate red by offering blue. Squid remains the primary acceptor of blue-for-other-color offers and also uses red-to-blue offers to shift blue holdings further toward themselves. Multiple-accept situations persist when an offer benefits both non-senders.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with different private values over red and blue (green common-value) will use low-value chips in their own portfolio to extract higher-value chips. Bat (low red value) will preferentially sell red to acquire blue/green; Fox (very high blue value) will try to buy blue using red or green as currency; Frog (high red value) will accumulate red by offering blue/green. Because acceptances depend on each recipient's private valuations, many proposals that look profitable to the proposer will be accepted only by the counterparty for whom the offered chip is relatively more valuable than the requested chip. Strategic uncertainty arises from (1) the proposer not choosing the recipient when multiple players accept, and (2) incomplete information about others' private valuations; hence proposers often structure trades so at least one other player is clearly better off accepting. Given player personas, expect low-aggression, patient offers from Bat and Fox that favor mutually attractive swaps, and modestly opportunistic moves by Frog to convert low-value blue into high-value red. Over three rounds holdings will shift incrementally: red will flow toward Frog, blue toward Fox and Bat when convenient, and green will move only when it helps secure a higher-value chip in exchange.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round testing: proposers make small, inventory-constrained swaps to probe who will accept. Bat uses low personal value of red to buy green; Fox asks for blue offering red; Frog tests buying red by offering blue. Proposers favor trades that are modestly positive for themselves and clearly beneficial to at least one counterparty to secure acceptance.

After initial reallocations, proposers exploit clearer comparative advantages. Bat continues to convert comparatively cheap red into green; Fox purchases an extra blue by offering green (targeting the player who values green less than blue); Frog opportunistically trades a green for red when only one counterparty has surplus red and will benefit.

Endgame: proposers tighten offers and target obvious bilateral gains. Bat again uses cheap red to acquire blue; Fox repeats a straightforward red-for-blue swap when beneficial to at least one counterparty; Frog concentrates on converting his remaining low-value blue into additional red, structuring the offer so multiple others would accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on trading chip types with heterogeneous private valuations for red and blue chips and a common-value green chip. Dog and Turtle both highly value BLUE (1.00) and view RED and GREEN similarly (0.50), while Parrot values RED most (0.60), GREEN next (0.50), and BLUE least (0.30). Because players only know their own valuations, proposals and acceptance decisions are driven by perceived immediate gain: traders who value blue highly will try to acquire it by offering red or green; players who value blue less (Parrot) are willing to sell blue to obtain higher-valued chips. Greens act as a convenient medium (common value 0.50) that can be used to move value without large private-value distortions. Strategic uncertainty arises mainly from (1) not knowing others' private ranks directly and (2) the random resolution when multiple players accept a proposal. Given the provided personas, Dog and Parrot are conciliatory and somewhat willing to accept mutually beneficial 1-for-1 swaps; Turtle is more selective but will accept clear, value-improving exchanges. Expect many small 1-for-1 trades where players with low marginal value for a chip surrender it to players with higher marginal value, particularly Parrot selling BLUE to Dog/Turtle and Parrot buying RED from dog/turtle when the exchange improves Parrot's valuation. Because players maximize their own chip-value, proposals will focus on favorable one-for-one exchanges and acceptance behavior will track straightforward private gains (accept when the offered chip is worth more than the surrendered chip under the responder's private values). Random tie-breaking when both non-senders accept injects risk for proposers who cannot select recipients.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players are fully stocked (10 each). Dog and Turtle value BLUE highly, Parrot values RED more than BLUE. Early proposals will seek straightforward value gains: Dog likely tries to trade a medium-valued GREEN (common 0.50) to obtain a BLUE (1.00); Parrot will exploit opponents who value BLUE more by offering BLUE for RED; Turtle will try to pick up BLUE by offering RED or GREEN. Final round: inventories are polarized (Dog and Turtle hold many BLUEs; Parrot holds many REDs and GREENs but few BLUEs). Dog pushes to acquire one more BLUE using GREEN; Parrot continues to monetize its remaining BLUEs for additional REDs; Turtle opportunistically buys BLUE from Parrot when the exchange is clearly profitable. With limited rounds left, players accept straightforward, clearly improving swaps even if they risk the random selection when multiple accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Early probing: proposers test simple exchange rates to acquire GREENs. Badger likely starts with an underpaying anchor (offers too little), peacock uses disciplined, near-fair terms that attract acceptance, and unicorn trades from a conciliatory short-horizon perspective, exchanging surplus BLUE for RED. Endgame trading: proposers make offers that are easy to value and thus likely to be accepted (e.g., trading multiple low-value BLUE/RED units for one GREEN). parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue (green is common-value at $0.50). Given player personas (low-search but conciliatory dog; deal-ready, numerate mouse; bookkeeping-disciplined, exploratory owl), we expect repeated, modestly self-interested proposals: proposers will prefer simple 1-for-1 or small multi-chip swaps that yield clear private gains, accept if the realized payoff is positive (often only one counterparty will accept), and occasionally use larger packaged offers when a single counterparty can be targeted for acceptance.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players probe for simple, value-increasing swaps. Dog (who highly values blue) will attempt to obtain blue early, even if that risks creating competition for blue. After initial swaps the distribution shifts: dog accumulates some blues and reds, mouse sheds some red into green, and owl has mixed holdings. Endgame dynamics: inventories have diverged (dog holding many reds and some blues, mouse reduced red holdings, owl seeking blue). rounds.round_explanation: Early-round signaling and value capture. parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories but different private values for red/blue/purple (green common-value = $0.50). Cat (high value on red/blue/purple) is inclined to trade away lower-valued chips (purple/green) to accumulate red/blue. Otter has low value on blue and moderate value on red/green, so he is willing to sell blue to acquire red. Badger values red the least and purple/green more, so she is a natural seller of red and buyer of purple or green. Because proposals require the proposer to both request and offer chips, most mutually beneficial trades are ones where a low-value holder (often badger or otter for blue/red) accepts a higher-value chip in exchange for a low-value chip. Uncertainty arises from private valuations and the random-selection rule when multiple recipients accept; proposers therefore favor simple 1-for-1 swaps that give each side a clear private gain. Over the three rounds we forecast repeated small swaps: cat gradually trades away lower-priority purple/green to pick up red/blue; otter repeatedly exchanges blue for red; badger offloads red in return for purple/green. These trades tend to be beneficial for both proposer and at least one counterparty, so most proposals are accepted. Strategic considerations include inventory constraints (cannot sell what you don't hold), the proposer’s need to make deals that increase their own value, and the possibility that multiple acceptors produce random partner assignment.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple 1-for-1 swaps. Cat favors acquiring red from a low-red-value counterparty (badger). Otter leverages his low blue value to buy red. Badger uses low red value to buy purple from cat. Early trades are small and mutually beneficial, establishing a pattern where red flows toward higher-valuing players.

Mid game: patterns reinforce. Cat continues swapping lower-priority purple for blue. Otter repeats a profitable blue->red trade. Badger, still holding low-value red, swaps red for green (which she values more). Multiple acceptances are possible on balanced 1-for-1 offers; when only one player benefits, acceptance is unique.

Endgame: proposers consolidate value. Cat trades green for blue to increase high-value chips; otter again sells blue to buy red; badger converts remaining low-value red into purple. With inventories somewhat shifted, the same incentive structure (badger willing to sell red, cat/otter willing to buy) persists and small 1-for-1 trades complete the forecast.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly negotiate pairwise chip swaps in a constrained, inventory-updating setting. Each proposer requests one color and offers a different color; responders accept only if the exchange raises their own private chip-value. Green is common-value (0.50) for all; red/blue/purple values differ by player: camel and otter highly value purple (0.8/0.9) while badger values purple/blue low (0.10) and green relatively high (0.50). Given these priors and the provided personas, the outsider forecast expects camel and otter to consistently target purple, offering lower-valued chips (red or blue) that hurt them less. Badger, who assigns low value to purple and blue, is the most frequent seller of purple and of blue in exchange for higher-valued chips (red or green) and is relatively quick to accept straightforward, beneficial offers. Camel, anchor-sensitive and cooperative, tends to post simple 1-for-1 offers (red for purple) that extract purple from badger at small cost. Badger often accepts such deals because they gain (or are indifferent) on the margin; otter resists selling purple but will buy it when proposing. Badger also occasionally offers bundles (e.g., 2 blue for 1 green) to secure green, which both camel and otter find attractive. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others' likely acceptance; randomization applies when multiple responders accept. The following play-by-play uses these priors and inventory constraints to produce a single plausible realization of the full three-round game.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: camel opens by trying to acquire purple cheaply; badger—who values purple little—is the natural acceptor. Badger then posts a generous offer converting low-value blue into green, which both other players find acceptable; camel receives the green. Otter ends the round by attempting to buy purple from the player most likely to sell (badger). The pattern is camel/otter as buyers of purple, badger as recurring seller and occasional green-buyer.

Middle round: camel continues to target purple using low-cost red. Badger again attempts to convert surplus low-value blue into red (a modest premium from the others' perspective), drawing acceptance from both camel and otter—one is selected at random as recipient. Otter follows by buying another purple from badger. Inventories shift toward more reds for badger and more purples for camel/otter.

Final round: camel again offers red for purple and extracts another purple from badger. Badger repeats a 2-blue-for-1-green conversion that is attractive to both camel and otter; camel is selected as recipient in this realization. Otter finishes by buying one more purple from badger. Across the three rounds the predictable pattern—camel and otter accumulating purples, badger accumulating reds/greens and shedding blue/purple—continues, reflecting the private valuation asymmetries and quick-deal tendencies.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles across three rounds. All start with equal inventories but different private values: lion strongly prefers RED/BLUE and dislikes PURPLE, butterfly strongly prefers PURPLE and values RED least, camel prefers BLUE and is indifferent between RED and PURPLE. That creates a stable pattern: lion repeatedly offers low-value PURPLE to acquire BLUE/RED, butterfly repeatedly offers low-value RED to acquire PURPLE, and camel trades low-value RED (or green) to acquire BLUE when profitable. Because acceptance is determined privately, some proposals that are generous to the proposer (e.g., offering a lot of red or purple) will attract multiple potential partners and the actual trading partner is randomly chosen among acceptors. Uncertainty about others' private valuations and the random partner selection lead players to propose clear value-improving swaps (give a chip they value little, request a chip they value more) and to accept only when a trade strictly increases their own total chip value. Personas bias behavior modestly: lion is relatively conciliatory and proposes modest, socially-tuned swaps; butterfly is disciplined and seeks purple persistently; camel is bookkeeping-oriented and anchor-sensitive, preferring simple one-for-one or two-for-one exchanges that improve their private payoff.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are balanced. Expect lion to sell purple (his least-valued chip) for blue/red. Butterfly will accept trades that convert her low-value blue/red into high-value purple. Camel will accept profitable blue-for-red swaps but decline trades that reduce his blue holdings. Early trades thus let butterfly accumulate purple while lion swaps out purple into blue/red and camel tests blue-for-red exchanges.

Inventories have shifted: butterfly holds more PURPLE, lion has accumulated some RED, camel has gained a bit of BLUE. Lion continues to trade away PURPLE for BLUE; butterfly continues trading RED for PURPLE. Camel trades GREEN for BLUE when it gives a modest gain and occasionally trades RED for BLUE when the terms are attractive to potential partners.

Endgame incentives push players to secure straightforward private gains. Lion continues to offload remaining PURPLE for BLUE. Butterfly keeps converting RED into more PURPLE. Camel looks for one final blue acquisition by giving up RED when the terms are compelling for at least one partner. Random selection when multiple accept continues to inject partner uncertainty into outcomes.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose single-color-for-single-color trades under inventory constraints and private chip values. Each player tries to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more; green is common-value (0.50) while red/blue/purple differ across players. Squid tends to offer low-value chips (red or purple) to acquire blue/green and has lower search, so they repeat straightforward, low-risk offers. Camel is bookkeeping-disciplined and will systematically trade away their relatively weaker chips (red) to obtain blue/purple when partner terms are at least neutral. Lion strongly values red and blue and is patient/search-oriented: they accept deals that are neutral or slightly positive and will trade away mid-value green/purple to accumulate reds/blues. Uncertainty arises because acceptors act simultaneously and a proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors; ties and near-ties (zero net gain for the acceptor) are resolved stochastically in practice, but here are forecast deterministically. Across three rounds, expect repeated patterns: squid repeatedly sells very low-value chips (purple or red) for green/blue, camel repeatedly offers red to buy blue, and lion often accepts tie or slightly positive trades and uses green/purple as the currency to accrue more reds/blues. Each trade is evaluated by the recipient as the change in their own chip-value, and players accept if the trade is nonnegative or slightly positive by their private valuation and behavioral priors.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-game pattern formation: squid opens by trying to convert low-value chips (purple) into green; camel then targets blue by offering red; lion uses green to buy red to accumulate high-value red. Lion often accepts neutral or slightly positive deals, so squid's and camel's offers are crafted to be acceptable to lion.

Players repeat successful, low-friction trades. Squid again trades a low-value chip (purple) for green which lion finds slightly favorable. Camel again sells red to obtain blue; lion accepts neutral tie trades to maintain or rebalance inventory. Lion uses green to buy additional red, again attracting multiple acceptors and resulting in a random recipient (forecast: squid).

Final-round consolidation: squid continues to trade lower-value red/purple into green to improve own value; camel repeats the pattern of offering red for blue when lion is willing to accept ties; lion uses purple as a lever to convert mid-value chips into additional high-value red, securing marginal gains before the game ends. Acceptance decisions remain driven by private marginal changes; many offers are accepted when they are neutral or slightly positive for at least one acceptor.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game will be driven by each player's private ordinal preferences over chip colors and their endowment constraints. All players start with equal nominal chip counts, but their private values differ: peacock most highly values BLUE (0.90), squid and alligator value BLUE and RED highest (0.70), while PURPLE is the cheapest for squid (0.30) and very cheap for alligator (0.10). GREEN is common-value (0.50) for everyone and so typically plays a smaller role as a leverage good. Proposers are likely to offer chips they personally value relatively little (PURPLE or RED for peacock) in order to acquire chips they value more (primarily BLUE or RED). Recipients will accept only when the incoming trade increases their own total value given their private prices; because proposers cannot pick among multiple acceptors, some offers are made narrowly to target a single profitable counterparty, while others are generous enough to attract multiple acceptors and yield a random recipient. Behavioral priors (squid: patient and anchor-sensitive; peacock: disciplined, search-oriented; alligator: conciliatory, low acceptance thresholds) imply: squid will often test smaller, anchor-driven offers and wait for favorable moves; peacock will make carefully calculated offers to gain BLUE; and alligator will frequently accept small or neutral gains. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others' incentives from public trades and holdings; proposers balance generosity (to induce acceptance) against the risk of creating multi-acceptor competition and losing the partner to chance.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage discovery: proposers experiment with profitable one-for-many exchanges to convert low-value PURPLE into higher-value BLUE or RED. Because everyone started equal, initial offers test who values what. Squid's opening is a modest exchange of low-value PURPLE for BLUE; peacock responds by aggressively buying BLUE using low-value RED; alligator uses cheap PURPLE to pick up BLUE when available.

After initial trades adjusted holdings, proposers refine targeting. Squid tests a smaller, anchor-driven swap (PURPLE for BLUE) and gets declined; peacock uses a modest RED-for-BLUE swap that is just acceptable to alligator; alligator then targets peacock with a compact PURPLE-for-BLUE deal to pick up another BLUE.

Late-stage adjustments: proposers either consolidate gains or attempt precise, partner-targeted trades. Squid makes a slightly more generous PURPLE-for-BLUE offer to entice peacock and succeeds. Peacock then makes a larger RED-for-BLUE offer that attracts multiple acceptors and randomly lands with squid. Alligator finishes by converting low-value PURPLE into RED via an attractive PURPLE-for-RED bundle, which peacock accepts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose simple one-for-one (or small multi-for-one) chip trades over three rounds. Each proposer requests a positive quantity of one color and offers a positive quantity of a different color. Players privately value red/blue/purple differently while green is a common-value chip (all value = $0.50). This creates predictable bilateral gains: alligator values red and blue highly (RED=1.00, BLUE=0.90), ram values red most (RED=1.00) and is indifferent among others (0.50), and owl values purple highest (PURPLE=0.70) and red secondarily (RED=0.60). Given these priors, proposers repeatedly craft small, inventory-feasible exchanges that convert lower-valued holdings into higher-valued ones for themselves, while making the offers attractive to at least one counterparty. Because multiple players can accept simultaneously, some proposals are made at terms that create positive surplus for more than one counterparty (producing multiple acceptances and a random recipient). Players are relatively conciliatory but differ on patience and search: owl is patient and numerate, alligator favors quicker deals, and ram is more selective. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own values and cannot tailor offers to others' exact valuations; thus proposers often use simple favorable swaps (e.g., offer a low-valued color for a high-valued color from their perspective) that are likely to appeal to at least one other player. Over three rounds we expect repeated small profitable swaps (primarily exchanges of PURPLE↔RED and BLUE×2↔RED) with some turns attracting multiple acceptances and others attracting just one or none.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test straightforward, inventory-feasible swaps that convert each proposer's relatively low-value chips into higher-value ones. Owl, who prizes purple, starts by offering a red for a purple (a mild improvement for her) — this is highly attractive to both alligator and ram. Alligator then attempts a low-cost way to increase red holdings by selling a low-valued purple for red, which is attractive to owl. Ram uses blue as currency to buy red (offering two blues for a red) to make the deal attractive to alligator.

After the initial profitable swaps, players repeat similar small exchanges. Owl again targets purple by offering red for purple; this time ram takes that deal. Alligator again sells a low-valued purple to acquire another red and owl accepts. Ram repeats the blue-for-red conversion, and alligator accepts the multi-blue offer again because it increases alligator's total value.

Final round: players use the same profitable micro-trades to fine-tune inventories before the game ends. Owl again trades red for purple; alligator accepts this time. Alligator in turn repeats selling low-valued purple to buy red and owl accepts. Ram finishes by converting blue into red using a two-blue offer that alligator accepts, consistent with alligator's high blue valuation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories (10 of each color) but privately value red/blue/purple differently; green is a common-value chip. Each proposer will try to trade away chips they (privately) value relatively less in exchange for chips they value more. Responders decide only with their own private values, so some proposals will attract specific players (those who gain) and be rejected by others. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient, proposers sometimes propose terms that appeal to more than one counterparty to increase the chance of execution even though they cannot choose which accepter is selected. Inventory constraints and earlier executed trades shift who is willing to trade in later turns (both because inventories change and because marginal value differences shift). Personas matter: butterfly tends to make modest, bookkeeping-savvy offers and accept fair/gainful trades; badger protects resources and prefers clearer gains; cat searches actively for mutually beneficial exchanges and accepts trades that increase her value. There is uncertainty about others' private values, so proposers favor proposals that are likely to be accepted by at least one counterparty (targeting players who, given public holdings and typical priors, would benefit). The forecast below uses these priors to predict specific one-chip-for-one-chip proposals and the resulting accept/decline responses and inventory updates over three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round play: proposers test partners with small 1-for-1 swaps that exploit known cross-player value differences (e.g., cat dislikes purple and likes blue; badger highly values purple). Butterfly makes a low-friction offer that likely attracts cat; badger then targets red (which he prefers to blue) in a deal attractive to cat; cat uses her turn to convert low-value purple into higher-value blue, producing some multi-accept situations resolved by random selection.

With inventories shifted, players make targeted 1-for-1 swaps that exploit marginal gains. Butterfly again converts some lower-valued blue into higher-valued red (appealing primarily to cat). Badger targets purple (his highest-valued non-green chip) by offering blue; that trade is attractive to butterfly. Cat uses her turn to trade a red for a blue to increase her own value; multiple responders may find such a swap acceptable, and a random selection determines the partner.

Final round: players make small, efficient swaps to convert residual lower-value chips into higher-value ones. Butterfly again targets purple by offering blue (appealing to cat). Badger makes another focused attempt to gain purple by offering red; given current inventories, that is attractive to cat as a counterparty. Cat closes the game by trading away low-value purple to pick up red, a deal that is attractive to badger.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with different private valuations repeatedly propose one-for-one (or multi-for-one) chip exchanges over three rounds. Each proposer will try to convert chips they value relatively less into chips they value more, while responders accept only when the trade increases their own private payoff. Because green chips have a common value (0.50) and the other colors differ in rank across players, most mutually attractive trades convert low-value red or blue into higher-value purple or blue (depending on the player). Strategic considerations: proposers must respect inventory constraints and anticipate that profitable offers for responders will be accepted (possibly by more than one player, in which case the partner is chosen at random). Uncertainty arises from private valuations and the random tie-resolution when multiple players accept. Given the provided personas (moderately patient, low aggression, decent bookkeeping), we forecast modest, value-improving proposals rather than extreme price demands; responders tend to accept small positive net gains but will decline small losses.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple, small exchanges to reallocate from their weakest colors to stronger ones. Early proposals are conservative: proposers try low-quantity swaps that create clear private gains. Because all players start with equal inventories, acceptances depend only on private value differences.

After initial small reallocations, proposers target more efficient conversions (e.g., trading low-value blue for higher-value purple). Some offers are attractive to multiple responders and can result in randomized partner selection; other offers are narrowly attractive to one counterpart only.

Final round: players use inventory information revealed by past trades to extract remaining gains. Offers become more targeted (e.g., multi-for-one swaps) to squeeze leftover arbitrage. Random selection matters when offers are attractive to multiple responders; some last-minute, small profitable swaps get accepted to finalize individual payoffs.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players negotiate over four chip types across three rounds, with each round giving each player one opportunity to propose a single two-sided exchange (offer one color, request another). Green chips are common-value (0.50 for everyone); red/blue/purple are private-value goods and differ across players. Iguana values BLUE (0.60) most, then GREEN (0.50), PURPLE (0.40), RED (0.30). Whale values RED and GREEN (0.50 each) above BLUE and PURPLE (0.40). Duck strongly prefers BLUE (0.90), is neutral between RED and GREEN (0.50), and dislikes PURPLE (0.20). Proposers tend to offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value more; responders accept only when a proposed exchange raises their own total value given private valuations. Behavioral priors suggest iguana is relatively accepting of small benefits, whale is conciliatory and patient (willing to accept near-neutral swaps), and duck is strategically patient and focused on accumulating BLUE. Because acceptances are simultaneous and multiple acceptances are resolved at random, proposers tend to craft terms that will attract at least one partner (often the one who values what the proposer offers most). Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others’ likely responses from past offers and observable holdings. The forecasted path below uses those priors to predict plausible, inventory-feasible proposals and the most likely response pattern.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test partner preferences with small, profitable-for-proposer swaps. Early proposals are modest (small quantities) to probe acceptance thresholds. Iguana and Duck look to accumulate GREEN or BLUE respectively, while Whale looks to convert GREEN into RED when advantageous. Responders use private values and small gains/losses to decide.

Mid-game: agents adjust to updated holdings. Iguana continues trading up from PURPLE into BLUE; Whale selectively converts BLUE into RED when profitable; Duck tries larger BLUE acquisitions but faces resistance because others value BLUE highly. Acceptance behavior reflects small positive gains for recipients (or indifference for conciliatory Whale).

Final round: players make small, high-probability trades to fine-tune portfolios. Iguana and Whale continue to execute near-neutral or slightly beneficial swaps; Duck targets one more BLUE but often faces rejection because trading partners value BLUE. Conciliatory Whale accepts some near-even swaps, enabling a few final exchanges.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories but different private valuations for red, blue, and purple chips (green is common-value). Bargaining incentives center on converting lower-valued holdings into higher-valued ones from each player's private perspective (e.g., Ram values red/blue most, Lion values purple most, Owl values blue most). Because proposals are bilateral swaps (one color for another) and acceptances are private, proposers will typically request a color they value more than the color they offer, while targeting counterparts who — by their private values — would find the swap beneficial. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not control partner selection when multiple players accept, and because each responder only knows their own values (so acceptance thresholds vary). Over three rounds with repeated opportunities, players with high bookkeeping/search (Lion, Owl) are likely to execute several small, value-improving swaps; Ram, who is conciliatory and moderately cautious, tends to make modest profitable requests that attract a single accepting partner. Expect mostly one-for-one small trades that transfer low-value chips (for the recipient) to the player who values them more, with most trades accepted when they create positive net value for the recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-move dynamics: Ram opens with a modest improvement seeking blue (his top value) in exchange for purple; Lion (who values purple substantially more than blue) is the natural acceptor. Lion then uses his turn to convert a green (mid value) into another purple for himself; Owl, who is indifferent between green and purple, accepts. Owl closes the round by trading a nearly worthless red for a high-value blue, which is attractive to Lion (who gives up low-value blue), so Lion accepts. These first-round trades quickly reallocate chips toward each player's privately-high colors.

With some inventories shifted, Ram again targets blue by offering green (a lower relative value for him) — Lion, who values green more than his remaining blue, accepts. Lion then capitalizes on his turn to swap a red for another purple; Owl gains from that exchange and accepts. Owl finishes the round by offering a purple to get another blue (his top color); Lion — who values purple more than his remaining blue — accepts. The pattern is repeated one-for-one value improvements, with Lion often the marginal acceptor because many proposed swaps convert blue or green into purple (which Lion values highly).

Endgame adjustments: Ram again seeks small, profitable blue gains by offering purple; Lion, who continues to value purple strongly relative to his remaining blue, accepts. Lion then uses his turn to convert a red into yet another purple (a favorable swap for him), which Owl accepts since red is low value for Owl. Owl closes the game by buying one more blue with a red — a very favorable move for Owl — and Lion accepts because the swap improves Lion's portfolio from his perspective. The final trades concentrate blues with Owl and purples with Lion, consistent with private valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposal-per-round bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red/blue/purple chips and a common-value green chip. Owl-yellow (high BLUE and high PURPLE) will compete for BLUE, Ram-green (high PURPLE) will compete for PURPLE, and Butterfly-purple (moderate BLUE, low PURPLE/RED) will try to obtain BLUE cheaply by offering chips she values less. Because acceptance is simultaneous and proposers cannot pick among multiple acceptors, proposers target partners who are more likely to accept (Ram has low acceptance threshold; Butterfly is search-oriented and disciplined). Uncertainty arises from private valuations: proposers can predict approximate incentives from public holdings and past trades but not exact acceptance thresholds. Expect many small 1-for-1 exchanges where players trade away lower-value chips (for them) to acquire higher-value chips, and some trades where both non-senders accept (leading to a random recipient). Over the game, Blue consolidates toward Owl and Butterfly, Purple consolidates toward Ram and Owl, and Ram often accepts deals that exchange low-value BLUE for high-value PURPLE, while Owl selectively gives up PURPLE only for strong gains in BLUE.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves: Butterfly opens by trying to buy BLUE using a low-value PURPLE to exploit Ram's relatively low BLUE value; Ram responds by buying PURPLE using BLUE (he pays with low-value BLUE to get his high-value PURPLE); Owl seeks additional BLUE by offering RED (which Ram values slightly more than BLUE). Expect Ram to accept trades that convert his low-value BLUE into PURPLE, Owl to accept good BLUE-for-PURPLE or RED-for-BLUE deals, and Butterfly to focus on accumulating BLUE.

Mid-game: Butterfly presses again for BLUE by offering PURPLE (still a weak chip for her) and targets Ram as a likely acceptor. Ram continues to convert RED or BLUE into more PURPLE by proposing straightforward 1-for-1 exchanges; Owl will opportunistically swap GREEN for PURPLE when giving up green (common-value) is relatively attractive versus keeping purple. Expect some trades to be single-acceptor deals (Ram or Owl) and some to draw multiple accepts where recipient is selected at random.

End-game: Players consolidate holdings. Butterfly continues to buy BLUE using low-value RED to edge toward more BLUE; Ram keeps bidding BLUE to obtain additional PURPLE; Owl, who most highly values BLUE, purchases more BLUE by offering RED (which some partners value slightly more than their BLUE). Acceptance patterns remain driven by private chip valuations: trades that exchange a player's low-value chips for higher-value chips tend to be accepted quickly, often by Ram when BLUE is cheap for him.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with heterogeneous private valuations repeatedly propose one-for-one or many-for-one chip exchanges. Each proposer seeks trades that raise their own total value given their private prices (dolphin favors BLUE, whale favors RED, owl is search-oriented and receptive to small gains). Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private valuations and from possible competition for a profitable counterparty (if both non-senders would gain, the recipient is chosen at random). Players tend to prefer clear value-improving swaps; owl's low acceptance threshold makes zero- or small-net deals more likely to be accepted by them, while dolphin and whale require clearer positive gains. Over three rounds, expect proposers to offer low-value chips (e.g., PURPLE or GREEN) to obtain higher-valued chips (BLUE or RED), and for trades to often be accepted only by the party for whom the exchange yields a net gain.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round testing: proposers probe profitable one-for-one or small multi-for-one swaps. Dolphin opens with an aggressive multi-for-one (aiming to convert low-value PURPLE into high-value BLUE) and is likely declined; owl and whale then pursue targeted swaps that match one other's valuations, producing single-party acceptances.

With updated holdings, dolphin and owl make small, value-improving one-for-one offers. Owl continues to accept or propose marginal swaps (zero or small net gains for them). Whale remains a frequent acceptor when offers trade their BLUE for RED.

Endgame consolidation: dolphin and owl again target BLUE (dolphin to capture BLUE, owl to trade away scarce RED for BLUE), and whale continues to collect high-value RED by offering lower-valued chips (GREEN or BLUE). Owl's willingness to accept small positive or zero gains enables whale to extract additional RED.

Red is the most contested asset (high value for dolphin and duck), purple is asymmetric (very cheap for dolphin, valuable for duck and alligator) and becomes the low-cost bargaining currency in several offers; green is common-value and moderate. Proposers will tend to request colors they value highly and offer colors they value less, while responders accept if the exchange raises their personal total given their private values. Uncertainty arises because each responder knows only their own valuations and must infer whether a profitable-looking deal might leave them short of other high-value chips in later rounds; proposal order and limited rounds encourage early extraction of high-value chips by proposers with disciplined bookkeeping.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining focuses on extracting red by offering purple (cheap for dolphin) or trading off blue. Proposers test each other's thresholds using favorable exchange rates; responders accept if the immediate net value for their private valuations is positive.

After initial reallocations, players have adjusted inventories. Dolphin continues to prioritize red, often offering blue; duck trades to accumulate blue while conceding some green; alligator looks to convert green into purple. Responders remain driven by immediate net gains given their private valuations.

Late-round play is more transactional: dolphin uses cheap purple to extract additional red, duck continues to convert abundant blue into red when profitable, and alligator accumulates purple. Acceptance largely follows immediate personal gain calculations; some offers attract multiple acceptors and are resolved by random selection.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or one-for-two) chip exchanges over three rounds. Each player privately values red/blue/purple differently while green is common-value; proposals therefore trade chips that are relatively cheap to the proposer for chips that are relatively valuable to them. Proposers will offer bundles that create clear private gains for themselves, but other players accept only if the offered bundle increases their own private value. Uncertainty comes from (1) private values (each player only knows their own valuations), (2) the risk that multiple partners accept and the proposer cannot choose which acceptor is realized, and (3) future opportunity costs across the three rounds. Given the personas: duck tends to make moderately conciliatory, low-search offers that are personally efficient; swan is anchor-sensitive, state-disciplined and relatively deal-ready (lower acceptance thresholds) so will accept small gains; whale is patient and search-oriented, preferring exchanges that yield clear private gains and sometimes waiting to extract better margins. Observed trades therefore favor proposer-self gains; some offers are declined when they are too one-sided for potential recipients, while marginally profitable offers for recipients (especially to swan) are often accepted even when gains are small.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers try to convert chips they value less into chips they value more. Early proposals test willingness to trade green and blue; some offers are so favorable to the proposer that others decline, while marginally beneficial exchanges get accepted. Swan's lower acceptance threshold makes him a frequent acceptor of small gains; whale looks for clearer improvements.

Players update beliefs from round 0 trades. Duck continues to trade away low-valued purple for higher-valued blue to improve his portfolio; swan remains willing to accept small positive swaps. Whale again offers clear private gains (purple -> blue) and finds a ready acceptor in swan. Some marginal offers (e.g., purple -> green from swan) are rejected if they leave potential recipients worse off.

Endgame considerations: proposers can try to lock in remaining personal gains. Duck makes a more aggressive multi-chip request that others correctly decline. Swan uses a small, clearly beneficial swap (red -> green) that duck accepts to secure incremental value before the game ends. Whale again trades purple for green when swan is willing to accept a small gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining sequence is driven by asymmetric private values: pig-green-499 strongly prefers BLUE (value 1.00) and treats PURPLE as near-worthless, zebra-red-3364 most strongly values RED (1.00) and also likes PURPLE (0.80), and swan-purple-7217 values RED (0.80) more than other colored chips. GREEN is common-value (0.50 for everyone) and so is rarely the focal currency for mutually beneficial trades. Given these payoffs and the player personas, predictable patterns emerge: pig will repeatedly try to acquire BLUE while shedding low-value PURPLE and occasional RED; zebra will try to convert other colors (especially BLUE) into RED; swan will attempt straightforward exchanges to obtain RED (often offering BLUE) because she prefers closure and has low search. Because each proposal requires exchanging one color for a different color, most mutually beneficial trades involve transferring BLUE toward pig and RED toward zebra/swan. Non-sender players will accept only when the immediate exchange increases their own private chip-value sum (or is at least weakly non-worse and fits their acceptance tendency). Uncertainty arises because each responder knows only their own valuations during play; from the observer perspective we use the known private values to forecast which offers create clear gains for which responders. Where an offer benefits multiple responders, the realized recipient is random; where it benefits none, the trade is declined. Over the three rounds we therefore forecast repeated, simple 1-for-1 trades: pig steadily accumulates BLUE by offering PURPLE or RED, zebra steadily accumulates RED by offering BLUE, and swan attempts to trade BLUE for RED when that yields a clear counterparty gain. The bargaining dynamic is sequential and opportunistic: proposers target the specific counterparties who would profit from the swap (even though they can't choose among acceptors), producing a steady reallocation aligned with private values rather than maximizing total group surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players start with 10 of each color. Pig immediately targets BLUE (his highest-value chip) and is likely to propose a very favorable, simple 1-for-1 deal (offer RED for BLUE or PURPLE for BLUE) because pig's bookkeeping and low-search tendencies favor clean, easy trades. Zebra will use her first proposer turn to request RED (her top value) by offering BLUE (which zebra values less); that offer is attractive to pig and likely to be accepted. Swan will try a straightforward trade to get RED, typically offering BLUE, because that is a clean way to obtain her preferred RED and she prefers closure.

Middle round: holdings have shifted—pig has more BLUE, zebra has more RED and some extra PURPLE, swan has gained some RED. Proposers will exploit the newly unequal inventories. Pig continues converting low-value PURPLE into additional BLUE (small, attractive 1-for-1 deals). Zebra again requests RED and offers BLUE, targeting pig as the accepting partner because pig gains by swapping RED for BLUE. Swan again targets RED with a simple BLUE-for-RED offer. These incremental, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 trades reinforce the emerging specialization: pig accumulates BLUE, zebra accumulates RED.

Final round: role specialization is pronounced. Pig is now heavily invested in BLUE and continues efficient one-for-one trades converting low-value PURPLE into more BLUE (he proposes PURPLE→BLUE trades that attract zebra). Zebra continues converting BLUE into RED by offering BLUE1 for RED1; pig repeatedly accepts because it increases pig's total value. Swan, as before, proposes a simple BLUE→RED exchange aimed at pig. Because GREEN is common-value (0.50) for all players, it rarely serves as a profitable currency in these exchanges and is not a focal part of the predicted deals. Final moves consolidate the distribution: pig ends up with many BLUE, zebra with many RED, swan moderate RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories but different private values for red, blue, and purple chips (green is common-value). Bargaining centers on converting lower-value inventory into higher-value chips for each player: zebra and turtle favor blue (value 1.00 for each), turtle also highly values red (0.90), while swan places lower value on blue (0.40) and is relatively indifferent among red/purple/green (0.50). Proposers will tend to offer chips they value relatively less (purple or green, or occasionally blue for swan) in exchange for chips they value more. Responders accept when the trade increases their own private total-value (or is near-neutral and their persona predicts conciliatory acceptance). Uncertainty arises because acceptors know only their own values and must forecast others' likely accept/reject behavior; when both non-senders accept, the actual partner is chosen at random, creating risk for proposers who may prefer one partner but cannot choose. Over three rounds, inventories shift modestly as players trade one chip at a time; early trades target high-variance preferences (blue, red), while later trades refine holdings toward each player's preferred colors. Strategic patience and bookkeeping (especially for zebra) lead to modest, low-aggressiveness proposals that are likely to be accepted by at least one counterpart, while swan's conciliatory tendencies produce several mutually acceptable, borderline-neutral swaps.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial stage: proposers target obvious mutually beneficial swaps. Zebra, favoring blue, offers a lower-valued red to obtain blue; swan looks to upgrade from blue to red; turtle attempts to buy blue by offering low-value purple. Acceptances follow private-value gains: swan and zebra accept exchanges that increase their own values, turtle is selective.

Holdings have shifted toward blue and red for the high-value players. Zebra continues conservative, bookkeeping-driven moves (trading a green for a red). Swan again courts modest upgrades (selling blue to acquire purple), creating trades that both zebra and turtle find attractive. Turtle exploits low valuation of purple to buy red at favorable rates, and joint acceptances occur when the swap is mutually improving.

Final round: players fine-tune portfolios. Zebra again targets extra blue by offering green (swan, who values green more than her blue, accepts). Swan proposes another blue-for-red swap that benefits both potential partners; turtle finishes by purchasing an extra blue from swan with a red, exploiting swan's relative tolerance for trading blue away.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with equal chip counts but different private values for red, blue, and purple; green is common-value. Bargaining incentives focus on trading low-value chips (for the holder) to acquire chips that a proposer privately values more. Key dynamics: (1) Proposers tend to request chips they value highly and offer chips they value less; (2) Recipients decide based on their private marginals (accept if the offered chip is worth more than the requested chip to them); (3) Multi-acceptance outcomes are resolved by chance, so proposers sometimes design offers that multiple players will accept to increase execution probability; (4) Inventory constraints evolve across rounds, shifting feasible proposals and acceptance incentives. Uncertainty: each player observes public history but knows only their own valuations, so they infer others' preferences indirectly from which trades occur. Given the stated valuations and personas, we expect many mutually beneficial, small-quantity swaps (1 chip each) in which players who undervalue a chip sell it for chips they value more, and conciliatory players accept modest gains readily while more disciplined players selectively accept only clear improvements.

rounds.round_explanation: Early exploratory round. Proposers test where others place low value (likely purple for two players, blue prized by frog), offering common-value green or low-value chips in exchange. Expect multiple acceptances when offers clearly benefit recipients (e.g., exchanging purple for green). Proposers prioritize positive expected returns given acceptance probabilities; recipients accept when the incoming chip exceeds the value of the chip they would give up.

After initial exchanges, holdings shift (some players accumulate purple or blue). Proposers exploit mismatches: those who value blue highly try to buy it by offering low-value purple; those who value purple highly ask for it by offering moderate chips. Acceptance is guided by clear, local gains: recipients trade away chips they value less than the offer.

Late-round efficiency play: players consolidate desirable holdings (frog keeps accumulating blue; kangaroo collects purple). Proposals are targeted (single-chip swaps) and often accepted by the player who views the offered chip as an improvement. Random resolution of multi-accepts continues to create small allocation noise.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal inventories of four chip colors but different private values for red/blue/purple (green is common-value). Each proposer can only offer chips they hold and must request a different color. Incentives push each player to trade away chips they value less to acquire chips they value more; because players only know their own values, proposals reflect each sender's private opportunism plus expectations about which counterparties will benefit. Uncertainty arises from not knowing others' private valuations and from the random tie-breaking if multiple players accept. Given the personas (generally low aggressiveness, moderate exploration, and good bookkeeping for some), we forecast modest, inventory-constrained offers that trade low-valued chips for higher-valued ones, occasional multi-unit offers to bridge value gaps, and selective acceptance when the trade increases the responder's personal value. Proposers often aim to convert widely low-valued chips (e.g., blue for dolphin) into higher-valued ones (red/purple) and responders accept when the exchange is strictly beneficial for them, producing several accepted, one-on-one trades and some declined, unattractive proposals.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers try to convert low-value holdings into higher-value chips. Responders accept when a proposed exchange clearly benefits them given their private values. Proposers may propose multi-unit offers to compensate counterparties for giving up higher-value chips.

Mid-game: updated inventories shape offers. Players who previously gained a lot of a favored color push to consolidate value further; responders remain selective and typically accept only exchanges that increase their own private value.

Final round: players exploit remaining arbitrage opportunities given inventories. Proposers make targeted, small-unit swaps likely to be accepted by a specific counterparty; responders, aware of diminishing opportunities, accept only when personally beneficial.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color chip bundles in 3 rounds (9 proposal turns). Each proposer must request one color and offer a different color; recipients accept only if the swap increases their own private total value. Green chips have a common value (0.50) for everyone; red/blue/purple differ across players and create predictable trading opportunities: elephant values BLUE highest (0.60), turtle values RED highest (0.70), and hamster values RED and BLUE equally (0.40). Given these valuations and the players' personas (elephant and turtle are search-oriented and bookkeeping-disciplined, hamster prefers quicker closure), the most likely pattern is repeated 1-for-1 swaps where elephant repeatedly offers low-value PURPLE to obtain BLUE (benefiting turtle as the marginal acceptor), hamster repeatedly offers RED for BLUE (benefiting turtle who strongly prefers RED), and turtle repeatedly offers BLUE for RED (benefiting elephant who prefers BLUE). Uncertainty arises from simultaneous accept/decline responses and the random selection if multiple players accept; however, because the value deltas are clear (positive for one intended recipient and negative for the other), proposals frequently produce a single accepting partner. Over the game we forecast a series of small mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges that concentrate RED in turtle, BLUE in elephant, and shift PURPLE modestly toward turtle, with green left untouched because simple 1-for-1 green trades are not attractive to recipients.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple 1-for-1 exchanges. Elephant uses low-value PURPLE to buy BLUE (benefiting turtle), hamster attempts to swap RED for BLUE to attract turtle (who values RED highly), and turtle capitalizes by selling BLUE for RED to elephant. These safe, small swaps are likely to be accepted by the single beneficial partner while the other non-sender declines.

Mid-game: players repeat the mutually beneficial patterns established in round 0. Elephant continues to convert PURPLE into BLUE (still profitable for him and acceptable to turtle), hamster again offers RED for BLUE to secure quick gains for turtle, and turtle again trades BLUE for RED when it benefits elephant. Because the beneficiaries remain the same given private valuations, these proposals again produce single acceptances.

Endgame: players finalize the pattern of specialization. Elephant continues to trade PURPLE for BLUE targeting turtle's marginal acceptance; hamster again offers RED for BLUE to secure quick, low-friction agreements with turtle; turtle continues to monetize remaining BLUE into high-value RED with elephant. With clear private gains for the intended recipients, each proposal is accepted by a single partner and holdings shift steadily toward the players' comparative advantages.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one chip exchanges across three rounds. Each proposer requests a positive quantity of one color and offers a different color; responders accept if the trade increases (or at least does not substantially decrease) their private chip-value. Green chips are a common-value focal point (0.50 to everyone), while red/blue/purple have heterogeneous private values (badger: RED=0.50 BLUE=0.30 PURPLE=0.10; dog: RED=0.90 BLUE=0.90 PURPLE=0.10; cat: RED=0.20 BLUE=0.20 PURPLE=0.30). Expect proposers to seek chips that raise their own value (e.g., dog seeks RED/BLUE, cat/ badger often target GREEN or RED), and to offer lower-valued colors. Responders accept when the received chip's private value exceeds the given chip's value; when both non-senders accept, one accepting recipient is chosen at random. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others' preferences from the public trade history. Given the personas, dog tends to accept obvious self-beneficial swaps quickly, badger is anchor-sensitive and reasonably willing to accept neutral or slightly positive swaps, and cat is disciplined and selective but will accept clear value increases. The forecast below uses these incentives and priors to simulate plausible proposals, private accept/decline responses, and immediate inventory updates after each accepted turn.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test pairwise exchanges that convert low-valued colors into higher-valued ones for themselves. Early trades reveal who values which chips strongly (especially dog for RED/BLUE). Expect quick acceptance when a responder clearly gains (dog often accepts gains on RED/BLUE trades); offers that hurt a responder (e.g., asking GREEN for PURPLE 1:1) are declined.

Mid-game: players use observed trades to refine beliefs. Dog continues to trade toward RED/BLUE (high private value), badger looks to turn low-value PURPLE into RED, and cat selectively trades when it improves their value. Several mutually beneficial swaps occur; proposers sometimes face multiple acceptances and the recipient is randomly selected.

Final round: players consolidate gains. Badger continues converting BLUE into RED, dog exploits opportunities to swap low-value PURPLE for high-value RED, and cat pursues additional GREEN when profitable. Several trades are accepted because they present clear private gains to at least one responder; when both responders accept, a random selection determines who trades with the proposer.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles over three rounds. Each proposer is likely to offer chips they privately value less in exchange for chips they value more; recipients decide privately based on their own valuations. Common-value GREEN (0.5 for all) acts like a stable medium but has limited bargaining leverage; heterogeneous values for RED/BLUE/PURPLE create repeated opportunities for mutually beneficial, inventory-constrained swaps. Because players observe holdings and public history but not others' valuations, proposers tend to target counterparts who, given public holdings and plausible preferences, are most likely to gain from the swap. Buffalo (low search, quicker deals) is likely to make compact, closure-oriented offers (selling low-value RED to buy BLUE/PURPLE). Goose (disciplined, patient) will make numerically consistent offers and convert lower-value chips into higher-value ones for himself, often proposing trades that a single recipient (who gains) will accept. Bear (conciliatory, search-oriented) often proposes modest, low-aggression swaps to improve their mix and will accept exchanges that clearly increase their private value. Uncertainty arises from not knowing others' private valuations and from randomized tie-breaking when multiple recipients accept; that encourages proposers to craft offers that are attractive to at least one identifiable partner rather than attempting to split surplus across both non-senders.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: proposers test partner incentives. Buffalo, valuing RED poorly, is likely to offer RED in exchange for BLUE to immediately improve her portfolio and attract an acceptor. Goose will look to trade low-value GREEN for high-value RED (he values RED highly) when beneficial; Bear will try modest swaps that secure more PURPLE while offering BLUE. Responses depend on whether recipients get positive net value given their private chip values.

With early trades observed, proposers refine targets. Buffalo continues converting low-value RED into BLUE to secure quick gains and picks counterpart(s) likely to accept.
