Goose uses textbook bookkeeping to trade BLUE for RED when profitable. Bear persists with low-aggression, small swaps aimed at increasing PURPLE holdings while offering BLUE.

Late-stage: players favor quick, marginal improvements and are more willing to accept borderline, neutral trades to finalize gains. Buffalo repeats efficient RED→BLUE conversions aimed at the single counterparty who benefits most. Goose again leverages GREEN→RED swaps that create clear private gains. Bear continues modest PURPLE accumulation via BLUE offers, relying on the player who marginally benefits to accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose pairwise chip swaps (request one color, offer another). Each player values the four colors differently (greens are common-value at 0.50). Proposers will try to trade away chips they value relatively less (or that opponents value relatively more) to acquire chips they value more. Recipients accept only when the exchange raises their own total value. Because buffalo values RED very little but values BLUE and PURPLE more, buffalo is the natural liquidity provider of RED in exchange for PURPLE (or RED for BLUE). Bear and dog both highly value RED; dog values RED slightly more than bear. Bear and dog will therefore repeatedly try to buy RED by offering PURPLE (a net gain for the proposer) or accept trades that give them marginal gains. When multiple recipients would gain from a proposal, acceptance by multiple players creates a random selection of the actual recipient, so proposers cannot direct who trades with them. Uncertainty about which other players will accept (and the random resolution when multiple accept) shapes cautious sizing and repetition of conservative, inventory-feasible proposals. Over three rounds we forecast repeated small (1-chip) offers where bear and dog accumulate RED by offering PURPLE, and buffalo uses low-value RED to buy BLUE, with many proposals accepted because they create small positive gains for both proposer and at least one recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: bear attempts to acquire high-value RED by offering PURPLE (which buffalo values more than RED). Buffalo, who values RED very little, accepts. Buffalo then uses its low-value RED to request BLUE from others, offering RED in return; both bear and dog would gain, so both accept and a random acceptance resolves (we forecast dog being selected). Dog, valuing RED most, then proposes to buy RED using PURPLE; buffalo again is the natural seller of RED and accepts.

Players repeat the profitable small exchanges: bear again buys RED using PURPLE (buffalo is the likely acceptor), buffalo again offers RED to acquire BLUE (both bear and dog would gain and both accept; random selection picks a recipient), and dog again uses PURPLE to buy RED from buffalo. Buffalo continues to act as the primary RED supplier because RED is low-value for them.

Final round: the pattern continues. Bear again targets RED by offering PURPLE and buffalo accepts. Buffalo makes another small market-making move trading away RED for BLUE; both other players benefit and accept, with dog again realized as the recipient in this forecast. Dog finishes by buying one more RED from buffalo with PURPLE. Inventory constraints (buffalo's shrinking RED stock) start to matter but remain sufficient for these small transfers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose bilateral chip exchanges in a constrained 3-round, 3-turn-per-round sequence. Each proposer requests one color and offers another; recipients privately accept if the deal raises their own chip-value given their private valuations. Strategic incentives: proposers tend to offer away chips they value least and request chips they value most; recipients accept when the incoming deal increases their own value even if it helps the proposer. Uncertainty and risk come from the fact that multiple non-senders may accept and only one accepting recipient is chosen at random; proposers balance attractiveness (to induce acceptance) against the risk of the ‘wrong’ recipient being selected. Given the players' private valuations and personas, we forecast mostly small, conservative trades that exchange low-valued colors for high-valued ones (e.g., selling PURPLE when PURPLE is low for the proposer, or selling BLUE when it is low for the proposer) and several offers structured to be clearly beneficial to at least one counterparty to secure execution. Peackock tends to make moderately generous offers to secure deals; Otter prefers trades that protect his resources and typically leverages others' desire for higher-value chips; Squid aims for clear value gains on his side and often structures offers that multiple others will accept, accepting the randomness risk.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers make relatively simple 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 trades to secure immediate value. Peacock opens with a modest, widely attractive swap; Otter uses cheap PURPLE to accumulate RED; Squid trades low-value BLUE to acquire additional RED. Expect multiple acceptances when deals are mutually advantageous.

Middle round: players respond to updated holdings. Peacock leverages GREEN (common-value) to pick up additional BLUE where a single counterpart benefits; Otter uses cheap PURPLE to obtain another BLUE; Squid again offers a generous BLUE-for-RED package that is attractive to multiple others.

Final round: players make last adjustments to improve their own portfolio value. Peacock opportunistically swaps low-valued RED for BLUE from a counterparty who gains; Otter uses cheap PURPLE to acquire one more RED; Squid closes by converting a PURPLE into a marginal RED. Acceptance is driven purely by private gains in these terminal offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly bargain over four chip types with private valuations for RED/BLUE/PURPLE and a common public value for GREEN. All start with 10 of each chip. The core incentive pattern is recurring: players who value PURPLE little (bear) are natural sellers of PURPLE, while dog and otter value PURPLE more and will try to acquire it by offering chips they value less (RED or BLUE). Bear values BLUE most and therefore repeatedly offers low-value PURPLE in exchange for BLUE; otter is anchor- and cooperation-sensitive and will propose relatively conservative swaps (selling low-value BLUE or RED to acquire higher-value PURPLE or GREEN). Dog, highly disciplined and patient with a strong preference for PURPLE, repeatedly offers RED to get PURPLE. Acceptance decisions are driven primarily by immediate private gains (value received minus value given): recipients accept whenever that net is positive. Uncertainty arises because (a) the proposer cannot choose which accepting partner is selected if multiple accept, (b) accepts are simultaneous and private, and (c) each player observes only their own values. Given personas and values, the forecast shows many small 1-for-1 exchanges centered on converting low-valued chips (for the sender) into higher-valued chips, with GREEN traded only when it offers a relative gain for the recipient. Randomization of recipients occasionally allocates gains across partners rather than being controlled by proposers.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple, high-probability 1-for-1 swaps. Bear (who holds purple cheaply) opens by offering PURPLE for BLUE; both other players strictly prefer that exchange and accept, producing a random recipient (dog). Otter then targets PURPLE by offering BLUE, which is attractive to bear but not to dog, so bear accepts. Dog ends the round by offering RED for PURPLE; bear again prefers that exchange and accepts. Early pattern: bear frequently supplies PURPLE, dog aggressively demands PURPLE, and otter trades conservatively when the immediate gain is clear.

Mid-game: roles and inventories have shifted but incentives remain similar. Bear continues to convert low-valued PURPLE into high-valued BLUE; such offers are attractive to both otter and dog and lead to at least one acceptance. Otter uses a conservative move to trade BLUE for GREEN once it is individually profitable (otter values GREEN > BLUE), and that is accepted by bear when bear values BLUE > GREEN. Dog persists in buying PURPLE with RED; bear often accepts those swaps because bear values RED more than PURPLE. Trades remain small and inventory-conscious.

Late-game: players consolidate their desirable holdings (dog increasing PURPLE holdings, bear accumulating BLUE and RED) with further small swaps. Bear continues the efficient pattern of selling low-value PURPLE for BLUE. Otter pursues additional PURPLE when selling RED is immediately profitable. Dog makes one final PURPLE purchase. Given private valuations and inventory constraints, most proposals are accepted by the party who gains immediately; multiple-accept situations continue to be resolved by random selection of the recipient.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles under inventory constraints and private valuations. Each proposer can only offer one color in exchange for one different color; recipients decide privately based on whether the swap increases their own chip-value given their private values. Strategic forces: proposers tend to offer colors they value relatively low (e.g., purple for badger) and ask for colors they value high (red or blue). Recipients accept when the offered chip yields at least as much private value as the surrendered chip, with some players (cat) biased toward acceptance on neutral deals and others (badger, otter) showing more selective patterns. Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, so they may prefer trades that many players weakly prefer (raising acceptance probability) but risk a random partner selection. Over three rounds we expect straightforward, inventory-feasible one-for-one swaps that convert low-value chips into high-value chips for the sender, with many neutral or weakly positive responses from the more conciliatory cat and targeted positive responses from players who privately gain.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers target quick, small improvements. Badger seeks to swap low-valued purple for a high-valued red; cat offers simple one-for-one swaps that appeal broadly (trading green for purple); otter targets blue (his highest) by selling purple. Expect some deals to be accepted by multiple players or by the conciliatory cat on neutral terms.

Mid-game: players have adjusted inventories. Badger continues converting low-value purple into higher-value blue. Cat makes targeted proposals to acquire red where possible, exploiting partners who privately value the swap. Otter continues to accumulate blue by selling lower-valued purple when it yields at least neutral returns to a responder.

Final round: inventory constraints and past trades make proposers favor safe one-for-one swaps that are likely to be accepted. Badger again trades purple for red; cat attempts another red-for-blue swap that appeals to the player who benefits; otter finishes by converting a remaining purple into an extra blue. Several deals are approved by multiple players, producing random partner selection where necessary.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player chip bargaining with private heterogeneous values for red/blue/purple (greens common-value). Each proposer can request one color and offer another; other players accept if the trade raises (or does not reduce by much) their own chip-value given their private valuations and acceptance thresholds. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know opponents' private valuations, only holdings and public trade history. Expect proposers to try to turn low-valued chips (red for otter, red modest for others) into higher-valued chips (blue or purple for camel and badger) and to occasionally make slightly generous offers (offer extra low-value chips) to induce acceptance. Camel (anchor-sensitive, cooperative) tends to prefer closure and may propose fairer terms; Otter (patient, low-anchor) will craft deals that marginally improve her value and is willing to make modest concessions that create clear gains for recipients; Badger (quick-deal, low-search) will accept straightforward, non-loss deals and propose simple value-improving swaps for herself. Multiple acceptances are resolved randomly; proposers cannot pick partner. Over three rounds we expect a mix of declined aggressive one-for-one attempts and several small multi-for-one trades where proposers offer extra low-value red chips to persuade at least one recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers probe opponents' willingness to trade high-value chips. Camel opens trying a direct value-improving one-for-one swap (likely too aggressive and declined). Otter can profitably trade low-value red for a green by offering two reds for one green (creates positive gains for recipients), which is attractive to both others and likely accepted by at least one (randomly selected). Badger, with extra reds after any acceptance, tries a straightforward one-for-one but faces opponents' indifference and declines.

Middle round: players adjust holdings and attempt more pragmatic deals. Camel prefers closure and offers a slightly generous two-for-one swap to secure a blue; otter repeats a value-improving red->green multi-for-one offer that benefits recipients and is accepted; badger, holding more reds, again targets blue with a two-for-one red offer that finds at least one indifferent/accepting partner.

Final round: with updated inventories, proposers try to secure last-value upgrades. Camel again uses a modest two-for-one red offer to obtain blue and is matched with a player holding extra red. Otter, reducing low-value red stock, trades two reds for a purple (a small net gain), which at least one opponent accepts quickly. Badger finishes by converting excess red into a blue via a two-for-one offer accepted by the remaining willing partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game will be driven by divergent private valuations. Two players (lion and butterfly) place the highest value on RED chips (lion 0.70, butterfly 1.00), while camel places the highest value on BLUE (1.00). GREEN is a common-value chip at 0.50 for everyone and therefore creates little trade pressure; PURPLE has intermediate private values (high for lion and camel, low for butterfly). Expect small, inventory-constrained 1-for-1 proposals where proposers offer chips they value relatively less in exchange for chips they value more. Because responses are private and proposers cannot pick a recipient when multiple players accept, proposers will tend to make simple, broadly attractive 1:1 offers that create clear positive gains for at least one counterparty (e.g., offering BLUE to someone who values BLUE more than the chip they would give up). Uncertainty arises because each responder knows only their own valuations, so a proposer can predict acceptance probabilistically rather than deterministically; when both non-senders would gain from the same terms, a random recipient selection governs execution. Over rounds we expect repetition of the same mutually beneficial small trades (BLUE <-> RED and PURPLE <-> BLUE) as players convert lower-value inventory into higher-value chips for themselves, with immediate updates to holdings after each accepted trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers make straightforward 1-for-1 offers that convert low-value holdings into higher-value chips. Lion (conciliatory) opens with a fair 1:1 exchange designed to appeal to the player who values BLUE most (camel). Camel replies by swapping PURPLE for BLUE with lion (gains for lion), and butterfly then targets RED by offering BLUE to camel (a clear win for camel). These are small, low-friction trades that build on each player's comparative valuations.

With updated inventories, players repeat similar profitable swaps. Lion again offers BLUE for RED to a camel who still gains from acquiring more BLUE. Camel again converts PURPLE into BLUE with lion. Butterfly again purchases RED by offering BLUE to camel. The pattern reinforces inventory specialization: camel accumulates BLUE, butterfly accumulates RED, lion trades to improve composition while remaining conciliatory.

Final round: as inventories diverge, proposers exploit remaining mutually beneficial 1:1 swaps. Lion switches to buying PURPLE (offering BLUE) because butterfly values PURPLE very little and both non-senders stand to gain—this can produce multiple acceptances and a random recipient. Camel continues to convert PURPLE into BLUE with lion, and butterfly again purchases RED from camel using BLUE. The final allocations reflect each player's private values: camel ends with many BLUEs, butterfly with more REDs, and lion modestly adjusts holdings toward mid-valued chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with a balanced inventory of four chip colors (RED, BLUE, PURPLE, GREEN). GREEN is common-value (0.50 to everyone); RED, BLUE, PURPLE are privately valued differently. Players act to maximize their own total chip value, so proposers will try to trade away chips they value relatively poorly and ask for chips they value highly, and responders will accept only when the incoming offer raises (or at least does not reduce) their personal value. Because proposers cannot pick the partner when multiple players accept, offers that look attractive to more than one counterparty can result in a randomly chosen recipient. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own valuations while observing public history: proposers infer likely takers from prior behavior and visible holdings, and responders weigh the immediate value change against acceptance thresholds (some players are more willing to accept small or indifferent gains). Over three rounds with nine proposal turns, the most likely dynamics are repeated small, inventory-constrained swaps where low-value sellers (to the proposer) give up chips for a good-valued chip, often driving competition for high-valued chips (BLUE and PURPLE for some players) and occasional multi-accept outcomes resolved randomly.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round testing: proposers try small one-for-one swaps to probe others' valuations and to pick up high private-value chips. Camel starts by targeting PURPLE (her high-value color) by offering a cheap-to-her RED; squid, who values PURPLE very little, will likely accept. Squid then uses cheap PURPLE to buy BLUE (which she values highly) by offering one PURPLE; this appeals to camel (who values PURPLE highly), and camel accepts. Lion uses cheap RED to try to acquire BLUE from a player who values BLUE less; camel is the likely taker. These early moves both shift inventories and reveal trading preferences.

With initial preferences partially revealed, players pursue more targeted exchanges. Camel and lion both value BLUE and PURPLE differently, and squid leverages her stock of cheap PURPLE to acquire additional BLUE. Offers that are attractive to multiple players (e.g., swapping a low-value BLUE for a GREEN that others value more) create multi-accept situations resolved randomly. Players remain inventory-constrained and generally prefer small, sure improvements to their own value.

By the final round players focus on consolidating personally high-value chips and trading away remaining lower-value chips. Camel continues to offload BLUE (low for her) to pick up GREEN or RED as small-value increases. Squid continues to convert cheap RED/PURPLE into BLUE. Lion uses low-valued RED to accumulate more PURPLE/BLUE. Multi-accept outcomes still appear when offers are broadly appealing, but trade execution remains driven by immediate private-value gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip exchange is driven by private, heterogeneous valuations for red, blue and purple chips (green is common-value). Players will propose single-color-for-single-color swaps they expect to increase their own total value, and non-senders will accept only when the trade raises their private payoff. Because proposers cannot pick a partner, offers are designed to be acceptable to at least one counterparty; that drives conservative, high-probability proposals (small quantities, trading low-value chips for high-value chips). Uncertainty arises from not knowing other players' private values (only public value ranges), from the random tie-breaking when multiple players accept, and from inventory constraints evolving over rounds. Personas matter: squid (patient, anchor-sensitive) tends to make offers that secure favorable marginal gains; alligator (bookkeeping-disciplined, low acceptance threshold) is willing to accept small gains and often acts as a liquidity provider; peacock (search-oriented, high bookkeeping) searches for favorable swaps for high-valued purple chips. Forecasted play therefore centers on proposers offering low-value chips (often red or purple depending on the proposer) to obtain chips they value more, and recipients accept when the marginal value gain is positive; many trades are 1-for-1 and executed when at least one counterparty benefits.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers make safe, high-probability 1-for-1 offers. Squid starts by trying to convert low-value blue into higher-value purple; alligator then purchases blue using her low-valued purple; peacock exploits his low-value red to buy purple from the player who values purple least.

With inventories updated, proposers continue with 1-for-1 marginal-improvement trades. Squid trades a blue to pick up a red (both recipients get a small positive gain); alligator again trades away low-value purple to increase blue holdings; peacock repeats a safe purchase of purple by offering a low-value red to the player who benefits from shedding purple.

Final round: inventories are more heterogeneous and players make last opportunistic 1-for-1 moves. Squid again converts blue into purple from the player who values purple least; alligator trades away remaining low-value purple to acquire another red; peacock finishes by purchasing another purple using a cheap red—each trade is accepted only by players who gain in their private valuation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories but different private values for red/blue/purple; green is common-value. Each proposer will try to convert chips they value less into chips they value more. Alligator (high blue value) will repeatedly try to obtain blue by offering lower-value greens or purples. Owl (high red value) will try to accumulate reds, often selling low-value blue or green. Ram (highest purple value) will target purple by trading away red or blue. Because responders only know their own values, they accept if the proposed swap raises their own private payoff; some proposals (e.g., swapping low-valued blue from Owl for high-valued purple for Ram) will attract unique acceptances, while others could attract multiple acceptors and result in a random recipient. Uncertainty comes from (1) not knowing opponents' exact private valuations during play and (2) the random resolution when multiple players accept. Given the players' personas, we expect mostly straightforward, small-quantity exchanges that are individually profitable, occasional multi-acceptance outcomes when a proposal benefits both non-senders, and no large multi-unit gamble trades because strategic patience is moderate and acceptance thresholds are not extreme.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round sorting: proposers make simple 1-for-1 exchanges that convert moderately valued chips into personally preferred types. Proposers prefer safe, small gains (one-unit trades). Some offers benefit both responders and thus may be accepted by both; if so, the recipient is chosen at random.

Mid-game: players build on prior gains. Alligator continues to accumulate blue by selling lower-valued greens. Owl opportunistically trades her remaining low-value blue for purple when it is attractive to Ram. Ram continues to acquire purple by offering reds when it benefits a single responder (Owl). Trades remain mostly one-for-one.

Final-round consolidation: proposers try to extract last easy gains. Alligator pushes a slightly larger (2-for-1 style) blue acquisition that still looks attractive to Owl; Owl continues small exchanges converting remaining low-value blue into red; Ram finishes by trading a blue for an extra purple. Responders accept when the exchange increases their own private value, and marginal or indifferent responders decline.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly negotiate one-for-one chip swaps under private valuations. Green is a common-value chip (0.50 each) while red/blue/purple values differ by player. Proposers will typically try to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more (e.g., offering a low-valued purple or red in exchange for blue or green). Responders accept if the incoming offer increases their own private total value. Because players observe holdings but not others' private values, proposals often target trades that create clear gains for the proposer while still plausibly beneficial to at least one counterparty (e.g., offering a low-valued chip that is worth more to recipients). Uncertainty comes from not knowing which opponent will accept when multiple players find the deal attractive, and from differing acceptance thresholds: badger tends to be more guarded, butterfly is more willing to accept small gains, and cat is exploratory and will accept modest immediate increases. Inventory constraints and the scheduling advantage of repeated proposers (butterfly acts first each round) shape incremental accumulation of favored chips (badger stacking blue, butterfly accumulating red/green when beneficial). Over three rounds we expect mostly small, one-chip swaps that produce modest, private-value gains for proposers and occasional acceptances by butterfly (low threshold) or badger (when gains are clear).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test one-for-one exchanges. Butterfly (first mover) makes a modest pro-self offer to obtain green (high relative value for her), targeting partners likely to accept. Badger then uses his strong preference for blue to buy blue with red, and cat looks for simple gains by offering a low-valued chip for higher-valued blue. Early trades shift holdings so later proposers can tailor offers to the new distributions.

With updated holdings, butterfly again moves first trying to upgrade marginal chips into greens; badger continues to accumulate blue using red because blue is his top value. Cat continues to pursue straightforward one-for-one gains. Butterfly's low acceptance threshold makes her a frequent acceptor of small positive swaps, so proposers often target her when only one partner will benefit.

Final round dynamics: proposers convert remaining low-value chips into marginally higher-value chips. Butterfly and badger continue targeting blue/green trades that incrementally improve their private totals. Cat keeps offering low-valued red in exchange for blue when butterfly is likely to accept. With only one shot left, players prefer small sure gains over speculative multi-chip strategies.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip-bargaining game will be driven by each player's private valuations for red/blue/purple and a common-value green. Players tend to be low-aggression, moderately patient, and relatively disciplined bookkeepers, so we expect offers that trade out the proposer's low-value chips for higher-value chips, modest concessions to secure a single counterparty, and occasional cross-player competition when multiple responders benefit. Key sources of uncertainty: (1) each player only knows their own values, so responses depend on private thresholds; (2) when multiple responders accept, a uniform random draw picks the recipient, so proposers must consider the chance of being matched with less-preferred partners; (3) inventory constraints matter — a desirable proposal can fail if potential acceptors lack the requested chips. Overall, forecast trades will mostly move low-valued chips (for the proposer) toward players who value them relatively more, and multiple-accept situations will sometimes arise where both non-senders benefit and one is chosen at random.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers look to convert chips they value least into chips they value most. Because everyone starts with even inventories, acceptance is determined by private valuations. Proposers tend to offer small bundles (1–3 chips) that make the deal clearly beneficial to at least one counterpart; when both counterparts strictly gain, both accept and the recipient is chosen at random.

After initial exchanges, inventories are uneven and players who accumulated many of a type may sell small lots to rebalance toward their high-valued chips. Proposers continue to offer modest packages designed to be attractive to at least one counterpart, sometimes creating two-sided gains and random matching.

Final-round behavior focuses on small, high-probability improvements before the game ends. Proposers tend to sell their lowest-value chips to buy slightly higher-valued ones or to pick up common-value greens when that is net-positive. Inventory constraints and random matching still shape outcomes.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players hold identical chip inventories but assign different private values to red, blue, and purple chips; green is common-value (0.50) for all. This creates persistent bilateral opportunities: whale-pink values PURPLE very highly (1.00) and is willing to trade away lower-valued chips (RED or BLUE) to acquire PURPLE; iguana-red values PURPLE very low (0.10) and values BLUE moderately (0.60), so iguana is frequently willing to give PURPLE to acquire BLUE; duck-red values BLUE and RED relatively highly and PURPLE moderately, producing occasional profitable trades to shift inventory toward preferred colors. Because players maximize their own portfolio value and accept or decline simultaneously, some proposals attract multiple acceptances (leading to a random recipient) while others are targeted to a single likely acceptor. Uncertainty arises from not knowing opponents' private valuations and from random selection when multiple players accept. Over three rounds we forecast repeated small one-for-one exchanges (mostly 1-for-1) where iguana supplies low-value PURPLE to buy BLUE, whale repeatedly purchases PURPLE (often paying RED) because it is the highest-value marginal gain, and duck alternates opportunistic trades (buying BLUE or RED by offering PURPLE) when payoffs are positive against at least one responder. Acceptance behavior follows personas: iguana has a low acceptance threshold and tends to accept favorable quick trades; whale is patient but will accept modest positive gains; duck is moderately exploratory so accepts when clearly profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers seek immediate, simple gains. Whale recognizes high marginal value of PURPLE and proposes to trade away RED for PURPLE; both iguana (who dislikes PURPLE) and duck (who slightly prefers RED to PURPLE) find this attractive, producing multiple acceptances and a random recipient. Iguana then quickly trades a low-value PURPLE for BLUE (a clear personal improvement), but only whale benefits as a counterparty. Duck finishes the round by buying BLUE with PURPLE, a trade that is beneficial to duck and to whale (who accepts by trading away BLUE for PURPLE). These trades shift PURPLE toward whale and BLUE toward iguana/duck.

Mid-game: patterns solidify. Whale continues to accumulate PURPLE by offering RED, anticipating strong marginal gains; sometimes multiple counterparts would accept but randomness determines the partner. Iguana continues the quick cycle of converting low-value PURPLE into BLUE via small 1-for-1 trades aimed at immediate improvement. Duck looks for modest gains by exchanging PURPLE for RED when that yields a positive margin; whale, who values PURPLE above RED, is the natural acceptor for those offers.

End game: players exploit the remaining clear one-for-one gains. Whale continues to buy PURPLE with RED (still profitable), sometimes drawing multiple acceptances. Iguana repeats the straightforward PURPLE->BLUE exchange when possible. Duck again converts PURPLE into RED when whale is the likely acceptor; because acceptances are simultaneous, whale frequently ends up as the recipient whenever giving RED for PURPLE is attractive to whale as well.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories (10 of each color) but different private values for RED, BLUE, PURPLE, and a common-value GREEN. Strategic incentives center on exchanging low-valued chips for high-valued chips for each actor, constrained by inventory and the fact that proposers cannot pick the recipient when multiple players accept. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own values (so they must form beliefs about who will accept a given price) and because multi-accept offers result in a random partner. Given the private values and player priors, trades that swap low-value chips for higher-value chips for the proposer (especially small, 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 exchanges) are most likely to be proposed; acceptances occur when the recipient’s private valuation makes the exchange beneficial for them as well. Lion prefers straightforward, bookkeeping-consistent swaps (often small red-for-green exchanges), Ram targets purple with compensating red payments (willing to part with reds to get high-valued purple), and Owl tends to convert between purple and red where it increases his own value and can generate clear surplus for potential partners. Because players maximize their own chip-value, many proposed exchanges are roughly zero-sum across players; bargains that leave at least one counterparty strictly better off (and the proposer still better off) are the most likely to execute. The game therefore produces several small, mutually beneficial swaps (1-for-1 or 2-for-1) and occasional multi-accept offers where both non-senders gain, with the realized recipient determined randomly.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test willing partners and extract easy gains. Lion (first mover) is likely to attempt simple red-for-green swaps since green (.50) is worth more to him than red (.10) and such a swap is attractive to Ram (.50 vs .30) but unattractive to Owl (.50 vs .80). Ram follows by trying to acquire purple (his highest-valued color at .80) and is willing to pay 2 reds to buy one purple; Owl, who values red highly, accepts. Owl then uses the newly acquired red stock to propose a deal that trades one purple for two reds, which is attractive to both potential partners; when both accept, the recipient is chosen at random.

Mid-game: players respond to updated inventories. Lion again pursues small red-for-green swaps that net him value and are appealing to Ram. Ram continues to accumulate green where profitable and is willing to sell some red to Owl for green. Owl then exploits his purple holdings to extract further red by offering one purple for two reds — again this is mutually beneficial for potential recipients and executes.

Final round: players consolidate gains and make last-value-improving swaps. Lion again executes a small red-for-green swap that is attractive to Ram. Ram pursues another purple using red payments (targeting Owl, who values red highly). Owl finishes by converting a purple into two reds; both other players benefit from receiving purple in exchange for reds, and a recipient is realized.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game will be driven by unequal private valuations for colored chips and inventory constraints. Greens are common-value (0.50 each) so they act as a focal baseline; red/blue/purple are privately heterogeneous: butterfly prefers purple > green > blue > red, owl strongly prefers blue then purple, and ram strongly prefers red then green. Each proposer will try to trade away a relatively low-value color for a higher-value color for themselves; non-senders decide to accept if the chips they would receive are worth more to them than the chips they must give up. Because acceptance is private and multiple acceptances yield a random recipient, proposers can sometimes extract favorable terms that attract at least one counterpart. Uncertainty arises from (a) not knowing others' private values, only their public holdings and past trades; (b) random selection when multiple players accept; and (c) inventory effects that make later turns slightly less flexible. Given the players' personas, butterfly is search-oriented and careful with bookkeeping (likely to make modest, well-calculated one-for-one swaps), owl is deal-oriented and quick to take clear personal gains (often trading into blue), and ram is deal-ready and often accepts modest gains; these tendencies yield repeated small mutually beneficial swaps (usually one-for-one or occasionally one-for-two when proposers push for larger gains). Overall, expect proposers to repeatedly offer their lower-valued chips (butterfly: red, owl: red, ram: blue/purple) in exchange for their individually preferred higher-value chips, with recipients accepting whenever the exchange yields a positive private value delta.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test straightforward one-for-one swaps that directly transfer a chip type each values more than the one they give up. Early offers are simple and easily evaluated; ram (low acceptance thresholds) is likely to accept modest gains, owl will accept clear, large personal gains for blue, and butterfly will propose trades to get purple.

With initial tests complete, players refine similar one-for-one trades. Butterfly continues to target purple by offering red; owl again trades red for blue when it yields large private gain; ram both proposes and accepts trades that increase his red holding. Inventory changes from round 0 make some players slightly richer in their preferred colors, but opportunities for mutually positive exchanges remain abundant.

Late-round dynamics: players consolidate preferred colors and may push slightly larger terms if they anticipate limited further opportunity. Because acceptance is private and multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers sometimes propose terms that appeal to both opponents; recipients with low acceptance thresholds (ram) or clear gains (owl for blue) accept readily. Final trades reflect accumulated inventory imbalances and continue to favor small, positive-value exchanges for each participant.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly bargain over four chip colors for three rounds. Each proposer tends to trade away chips they value relatively less to acquire chips they value more; recipients accept when the exchange raises their own private value given holdings. Owl (high PURPLE value, low BLUE value) is likely to propose exchanging BLUE for PURPLE. Dolphin (highest RED value, relatively high BLUE) will repeatedly seek RED, often offering BLUE or GREEN. Whale (prefers GREEN and values RED least) will try to convert low-value RED into GREEN. Because acceptance is simultaneous and the proposer cannot pick the partner, offers that benefit multiple recipients (e.g., trading low-value PURPLE for higher-value BLUE for Dolphin, or low-value RED for GREEN for Dolphin/Whale) can attract multiple accepts and lead to random matching. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others' incentives from public trades; this favors small, simple one-for-one proposals, and repeated patterns as players exploit consistent valuation asymmetries. Strategic considerations: proposers favor low-for-high swaps that they can afford; recipients accept when the immediate chip-value gain is positive or non-negative given a moderate acceptance threshold; players with higher bookkeeping/search scores (Owl, Whale) may propose clear, value-improving swaps and accept simple beneficial offers, while Dolphin's high strategic patience leads to targeted requests for red chips across rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage probing: Owl starts by offering low-value BLUE for high-value PURPLE, a clear gain for Owl and also attractive to both Dolphin and Whale because they get BLUE which they often value more than PURPLE. Dolphin follows by targeting RED (their top chip) and offers BLUE; Whale, who values RED least, finds giving RED to obtain BLUE beneficial and accepts. Whale then requests GREEN offering RED (turn 2), converting low-value RED into the common-value GREEN; Dolphin accepts because RED is valuable to Dolphin relative to GREEN.

Players exploit emerging inventory asymmetries. Owl repeats the profitable BLUE-for-PURPLE trade; Whale (holding extra BLUE now) sells PURPLE to Owl. Dolphin continues to accumulate RED by offering GREEN; Whale again concedes RED to obtain GREEN. Whale then again trades RED for GREEN with Dolphin, as Whale systematically converts low-value RED into higher-value GREEN.

Final-round consolidation: Owl again trades BLUE for PURPLE to stack her highest-value chip. Dolphin continues to acquire RED by offering BLUE; Whale persistently turns down RED in exchange for GREEN, reflecting Whale's strong preference for GREEN and low value for RED. Acceptances follow the same pattern: Whale often concedes RED to Dolphin, while Dolphin sometimes supplies GREEN to Whale when the exchange is favorable.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk-neutral proposers repeatedly trade one-for-one or multi-for-one chip exchanges to convert holdings toward the chip types they privately value most. Each player starts balanced (10 of each). Green has a public, common value (0.50) while red/blue/purple are privately valued: alligator prefers blue (0.90) > red (0.80) > purple (0.70); dolphin prefers red (0.80) > blue (0.70) > purple (0.30); duck values red and blue highest (0.90) and purple lowest (0.10). Proposers will tend to request chips they value more and offer chips they value less (purple often used as currency) while calibrating quantities so at least one counterparty gains. Non-senders accept only when the incoming bundle raises their own expected chip-value given their private valuations. Because proposers do not choose partners and multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers typically frame offers so that only a single counterparty has a clear gain (e.g., by offering enough low-value purple to outweigh the value of a requested red/blue for one specific counterparty). Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own private values and must infer others' tastes from public history; this leads to repeated, modest trades (mostly 1-for-1 or 2-for-1) that are attractive to one accepting partner at a time. The predicted path shows repeated small exchanges in which dolphin often supplies blue for red and alligator frequently accepts advantageous multi-purple swaps, allowing duck to convert low-value purple into high-value red/blue when the terms favor a specific counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple, low-friction exchanges. Alligator opens with a 1-for-1 red->blue swap that benefits anyone who prefers red less than blue (dolphin does), then dolphin uses purple to buy red from alligator, and duck similarly converts some purple into red when the terms advantage alligator as the single acceptor.

With a public trade history, proposers repeat successful patterns. Alligator again requests blue for red (a small mutual improvement for certain partners), dolphin repeats a purple-for-red offer that benefits the party with relatively high purple marginal value (alligator), and duck trades purple for blue when the terms meaningfully benefit a single counterparty.

Final round consolidates gains: alligator again targets blue by offering red (dolphin still finds supplying blue for red profitable), dolphin continues converting purple to red when terms favor alligator as the single accepter, and duck makes a last purple-for-red conversion accepted by the party that gains most from extra purple.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining sequence will be driven by asymmetric private valuations (each player values red/blue/purple differently; green is common-value) plus the players' personas. Whale (patient, disciplined, search-oriented) tends to craft modestly self-favoring swaps that are still attractive enough to secure acceptance; duck (conciliatory, low aggression) is willing to trade away low-valued purple chips to pick up higher-valued red/blue chips; swan (anchor-sensitive, deal-ready) prefers straightforward, small exchanges and accepts modest improvements readily. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know opponents' private valuations, so many proposals are framed to give away low-valued chips (purple or blue) in exchange for higher-valued chips (red or blue) from the proposer's perspective. Acceptance is determined privately by comparing the recipient's own value gain (chips received minus chips given); players with low acceptance thresholds (swan) accept small positive gains, duck is often willing to accept weakly positive deals, and whale accepts selective positive exchanges. The proposer schedule (whale, duck, swan each round) and inventory constraints produce repeated patterns: frequent offers of purple-for-red or purple-for-blue, typically accepted by the party that values the incoming chip more. Because multiple acceptances result in a random recipient, several early proposals are intentionally attractive to multiple partners, leading to randomized matching on those turns.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers try small, clearly positive trades for themselves while testing counterpart willingness. Whale opens with a compact purple-for-red style swap that is attractive to both opponents; duck follows by exploiting his ability to trade away low-valued purple for blue; swan uses a simple purple-for-red offer to pick up a higher-valued red. Because players begin with balanced inventories, acceptance is driven almost entirely by private marginal values and the personas' acceptance tendencies.

Mid-game: proposers respond to updated inventories. Whale continues to solicit purple by offering blue (cheap from his perspective), duck repeatedly trades purple for red when profitable, and swan again uses small purple-for-red swaps. Patterns consolidate: whale and duck accumulate more purple/blue depending on who accepts, and offers tend to be accepted by the single player who benefits most (often whale).

End-game: proposers seek finishing gains given remaining inventories. Whale shifts to buying red by offering blue when that yields a small positive margin; duck continues to convert purple into blue or red when advantageous; swan makes a final simple purple-for-red swap. Acceptances are mostly single-player (the party who benefits most), producing predictable, inventory-constrained final holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players have different private valuations for red, blue, and purple chips (greens are common-value). Each player starts with equal inventories, so bargaining centers on exchanging chips that are relatively low-value for the proposer in order to acquire chips they value highly. Zebra strongly values RED, Swan strongly values BLUE, and Pig values RED moderately and BLUE modestly but dislikes PURPLE. Because acceptances are private and multiple acceptances are possible, proposers who offer terms that create positive surplus for more than one counterparty risk having a partner selected at random. Over three rounds with one proposal per player each round, we expect repeated small, inventory-constrained trades: zebra repeatedly targets RED by offering lower-valued colors (blue or purple), swan repeatedly targets additional blue by selling low-value red, and pig uses low-value purple to buy modestly valued blue or red. Acceptance choices depend only on each player’s private values: players accept trades only when the chips they receive are worth at least as much as the chips they give up. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others’ acceptance thresholds from observed deals; this typically favors safe, slightly profitable offers and occasional multi-accept outcomes that are resolved randomly. Strategic patience and bookkeeping priors make zebra somewhat selective but persistent in acquiring red; pig is conciliatory and sometimes accepts small gains; swan is anchor-sensitive and quickly accepts exchanges that turn low-value red into high-value blue.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers test the market by offering trades that turn their relatively low-value chips into higher-value chips. Zebra opens by trying to convert cheap blue into high-value red. Pig prefers to use low-value purple to buy blue. Swan looks for efficient small swaps of red for purple when profitable. Because inventories are still large, small exchanges are feasible and multiple players often find the same deal profitable, producing multi-accept outcomes resolved at random.

After initial trades, relative inventories shift and players target remaining chips they value highly. Zebra continues to seek additional red using small purple offers. Pig targets red using modest blue offers when those terms are likely to be accepted by the player holding low marginal value for red. Swan prefers to convert low-value red into high-value blue. These proposals are typically narrowly profitable for at least one counterparty, producing mostly single-accept or predictable acceptances.

Endgame trading focuses on consolidating each player's favorite chips while inventories constrain feasible offers. Zebra continues to convert modest holdings of low-valued colors into red; pig uses small purple bundles to pick up incremental blue; swan makes a final aggressive move to turn low-value red into high-value blue. Because inventories have thinned for some colors, some trades are accepted only by a single counterparty, giving proposers a clearer idea of who will reliably accept certain exchange rates.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly bargain over four chip types with privately heterogeneous values for red/blue/purple and a common value green. Each player will try to trade away chips they value relatively less in exchange for chips they value more, but proposals must be attractive enough for a counterparty to improve their own portfolio. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know opponents' private values and responders do not know proposer's preferences beyond the public history. Turtle (more conciliatory, low search) will tend to make modest, closure-oriented proposals; Zebra (high bookkeeping, patient) will craft proposals that look value-improving on paper and will systematically target efficient exchanges (e.g., offering low-valued blue or purple for red); Swan (moderately patient, conciliatory) will propose trades that are mildly beneficial to herself and often accept clear gains. Because acceptance is simultaneous, some profitable offers attract multiple acceptors and the recipient is then chosen at random, so proposers balance attractiveness against what they must give up. Inventory constraints and the limited three-round horizon push players toward making deals that convert lower-value inventory into higher-value chips early, while modest concessions are used to secure partners. Overall we expect a mix of declined exploratory offers and a handful of executed one-for-many or two-for-one exchanges where the trade-improving gains are visible to responders.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial probing round: proposers test others' willingness to part with red (the highest-value chip) by offering multiples of low-value chips. Turtle, cautious and closure-seeking, opens with a compact proposal that benefits her but is unattractive to others and is declined. Zebra then makes a clear, gainful blue-for-red proposal that both responders find attractive; one accepts at random (Swan). Swan closes the round with an attempt to acquire red that is rejected.

After seeing one successful blue-for-red trade, proposers adjust. Turtle shifts to a more generous multi-for-one purple→red offer that is attractive to at least one partner and secures a red. Zebra repeats a compact blue-for-red pattern and gets matched. Swan, recognizing opportunities to acquire red, makes a similar blue-for-red offer that finds a single accepting partner (turtle) who benefits from the exchange.

Final-round bargaining sharpens incentives: players try to fine-tune holdings by trading occasional single chips or small bundles. Turtle uses a compact one-for-one purple→blue swap to convert low-value purple into more useful blue; Zebra again offers blue-for-red and attracts a taker; Swan finishes with a small blue→purple trade that is marginally profitable for her and acceptable to turtle. The limited remaining horizon and clearer public history motivate fairly conservative, value-improving proposals.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or asymmetric quantity) chip exchanges over three rounds. Each player privately values red/blue/purple differently and shares a common value for green, so trades are driven by comparative advantages: players try to trade away chips they value less for chips they value more. Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot choose the accepting partner if multiple players accept, and each responder only knows their own valuations (not others'), so proposers tend to target trades that at least one other player will find immediately value-increasing. Given the personas, proposals are moderately conciliatory and bookkeeping-oriented (seeking sure accepts rather than extreme gambles), whereas respondents accept whenever a trade increases their own private chip value. Therefore observers expect many small, positive-sum bilateral swaps (e.g., players offering low-value red in exchange for high-value purple, or selling blue to acquire red) with occasional multiple-acceptor outcomes resolved by random selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers test straightforward beneficial swaps that exploit clear value disparities (frog values purple very highly and red very little; kangaroo values red highly, unicorn values red/purple moderately). Proposers favor simple 1- or 2-chip uneven exchanges that are likely to be accepted by at least one counterparty. Responders accept whenever the immediate chip-value balance increases for them.

Mid game: holdings have shifted so players repeat profitable patterns. Kangaroo continues to trade away low-value blue for red. Unicorn targets a small, low-cost green concession to pick up a purple (preferring guaranteed acceptance), and frog continues to harvest purple by offering low-value red. Where multiple responders would benefit, random selection determines the partner.

Final round: players consolidate gains. Kangaroo repeats a reliable blue-for-red swap (targeting frog's low red valuation). Unicorn again purchases red by offering a small amount of blue, targeting frog as the likely seller. Frog uses remaining low-value red to try to acquire additional purple; when multiple players could accept, random selection resolves the partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical inventories (10 of each color) but different private values for red/blue/purple; green is a common-value chip (0.50). Each player seeks to maximize their own realized chip value across three rounds where each round has one proposal turn per player. Key incentives: proposers will offer chips they value relatively little to request chips they value more; responders accept if the incoming exchange raises their private total value (or is sufficiently attractive given their acceptance temperament). Uncertainty arises because (1) proposers cannot choose the partner when multiple players accept — a random accepting recipient is selected — and (2) each player only knows their own values, so they infer others' likely responses from observed offers and prior tendencies (personas). Given the personas, players tend to be conciliatory and moderately patient, with good bookkeeping for swan. We therefore expect many mutually beneficial, small-quantity swaps (1 chip typical), occasional multiple-accept offers (leading to random partner selection), and some declined offers when the terms are unattractive to both potential recipients. The forecast below uses the provided private values and these priors to predict concrete proposals, accept/decline responses, and inventory updates after each accepted trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers test simple one-for-one swaps that capture clear value gains for themselves. Because zebra values BLUE highly, his opening offer aims to trade away a low-value red to get blue; that generates a generally attractive deal for both other players, so a multiple-accept outcome (random recipient) is plausible. Swan then uses a cheap blue (low personal value) to buy a green (highest personal value for her), targeting zebra who strongly prefers blue to green. Dolphin ends the round trying to upgrade a blue into a purple, a trade that zebra will accept (gain), while swan declines.

With some inventory updated, zebra again targets green (cheap red in his hands) to pick up an extra green (still useful for balancing or future trades). Dolphin sees value in purple relative to his blue but faces rejections when asking for purple from others at equal exchange rates, so his late-round attempt is mildly aggressive and gets declined. Swan uses her bookkeeping discipline to trade away low-value blue for additional red when advantageous.

Final round: proposers adjust to remaining inventories and focus on clear value-improving swaps. Zebra again offers a one-for-one swap to obtain another blue by giving a purple; this is attractive to at least one partner. Swan uses another low-value blue to buy a green. Dolphin finishes by leveraging a favorable conversion rate (surrendering one purple to obtain two reds) that is appealing to zebra but unattractive to swan, producing a single-accept outcome.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players face repeated, inventory-constrained bilateral trades across three rounds. Each proposer will typically request chips they privately value relatively highly and offer chips they value relatively low; recipients decide using only their own private valuations. Because GREEN has a common value (0.50) it functions as a neutral medium, while RED/BLUE/PURPLE are points of competition: turtle values PURPLE highest (0.60) then GREEN, hamster values PURPLE and BLUE very highly (0.90, 0.80), and elephant highly values RED (0.90) and BLUE (0.60). Expect proposers to target partners who, from an observer view, would gain by reversing their own relative rankings (i.e., offer chips that are low for the proposer but relatively valuable to recipients). Strategic uncertainty arises from (a) private valuations that make acceptances endogenous and (b) the random selection rule when multiple players accept. Turtle and elephant are inferred to be more willing to make repeated small, inventory-efficient swaps; hamster is selective but will accept clear, favorable gains when they arise. Over turns we forecast frequent small exchanges: proposers entreat others to swap low-value units for high-value units, and some offers attract multiple accepts (resolved by random selection) while others are declined when no recipient sees a net gain.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test others' willingness to trade by offering low-valued chips for higher-valued ones. Early offers will aim to convert each proposer's weakest chip into a stronger one for themselves; responders accept only when the stated swap increases their own value given private values. Randomization matters if both non-senders benefit from a proposal.

Middle round: players respond to updated inventories and to revealed trading tendencies. Expect more targeted swaps (e.g., converting one's low-valued RED into GREEN or BLUE if another player gains), and some proposers will exploit that GREEN is common-valued. Players who have already traded may be slightly more willing to accept favorable one-for-one exchanges.

Final round: inventories reflect earlier conversions and proposers push for last profitable swaps. Because some players now hold more of their preferred chip, proposals often offer other players modest gains; multi-accept situations can reoccur and are resolved randomly. Expect small, efficient one-for-one exchanges rather than large bundle trades.
